    By Content Type

    Content TypeEmailsTotal Sent Open RateClick RateCTOR

    Announcement

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do 96 61.5% 2.1%
    Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility 245 48.6% 0.8%
    Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required for all enrollment records 244 48.1% 1.2%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding 146 46.6% 0.0%
    Manage terminations & billing with more clarity 94 45.7% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing announcement emails in this dataset share two traits: they are sent to small, targeted audiences and carry either urgent regulatory/compliance language or a personal, relationship-driven tone. The top three openers all went to fewer than 100 recipients, suggesting that tight audience segmentation drives open rates far above the broad-list average of ~30–35%. For future campaigns, the clearest lever is segmenting compliance and action-required announcements to only the affected subset of customers rather than blasting the full list, and pairing feature announcements with a clear action prompt rather than a passive 'now live' framing.

    Strong Signal

    Personal, relationship-oriented subject lines (thank-you, advisory board, welcome) outperform product-feature lines at equivalent audience sizes.

    Top 3 open rates (76.5%, 58.6%, 41.4%) all belong to relationship/community emails

    Try this next: Create a dedicated relationship-touchpoint email cadence (welcome, thank-you, event reminders) separate from product announcements to sustain elevated engagement with high-value contacts.

    Larger send lists (600+ recipients) are associated with significantly lower open rates regardless of subject line quality.

    Emails sent to 600+ recipients average 25.1% open rate vs. 44.5% for emails sent to under 300

    Try this next: Segment broad announcement sends into tighter audience cohorts by role, product tier, or affected feature set to reduce list size and improve relevance per recipient.

    Funding announcement emails show a steep open-rate drop as audience size scales up.

    Same-day '$43M raised' emails ranged from 33.3% (45 sent) to 8.4% (1,001 sent)

    Try this next: Reserve funding announcement emails for a curated high-engagement segment (e.g., executive sponsors or champion contacts) rather than the full customer base.

    Analytics report announcement emails consistently generate near-zero click rates despite solid open rates.

    All 5 'New Analytic reports are now available' emails recorded 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Redesign analytic report announcement emails to include a specific prompt (e.g., 'See your team's report') and a direct deep-link to the relevant report rather than a generic product page.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and compliance language in subject lines correlates with high open rates among mid-sized sends.

    61.5% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC'
    Show reasoning

    The 'Action required' subject line achieved the highest open rate (61.5%) among sends of 90+ recipients, well above the cohort average (~35%). However, only one subject line used this exact framing, so it is directional rather than conclusively proven by multiple examples.

    'Coming soon' teaser framing does not outperform 'now live' framing in open rates.

    'Coming soon' emails average ~37% opens vs. ~44% for 'now live' equivalents on similar list sizes
    Show reasoning

    Comparing 'Coming soon: Standard payer names' (48.1%, 244 sent) with its 'Now live' follow-up (43.0%, 251 sent) shows only a slight edge for the teaser, while 'Coming soon: Provider profile redesign' (32.8%, 305 sent) lagged behind 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' (44.0%, 209 sent). The pattern is mixed and confounded by list-size differences.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top opener (76.5%) has 38 characters; median opener has 52 characters

    Short, medium, and long subject lines appear throughout both the top and bottom of the open-rate distribution without a discernible pattern. Audience size and content urgency appear to be stronger confounders than character count, making it impossible to isolate a length effect from this data alone.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    High performers span January, February, and November; low performers also span multiple months

    Top-performing emails were sent across January 26, January 28, and February 12, while low-performing emails also span August through April. There are too few sends and too many confounding variables (list size, content type) to attribute performance differences to send timing.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply: (1) Sample size is small — 31 emails total — limiting statistical confidence for most patterns. (2) Audience size varies enormously (1 to 1,077 recipients), and smaller lists are systematically skewed toward higher open rates, making cross-list comparisons unreliable without controlling for list size. (3) At least four subject lines are near-duplicates sent to different segments on the same or adjacent dates (e.g., '$43M raised' variants on 2025-08-18 and 2025-08-20; 'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' sent on both 2025-11-20 and 2025-12-01; 'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing' sent on both 2025-10-15 and 2025-10-22; 'Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon' sent on both 2026-03-10 and 2026-03-26), suggesting A/B segmentation or re-sends — these should not be treated as independent observations. (4) Two emails with 0.0% open rates were sent to audiences of 6 and 1 respectively, making them statistical non-events that should be excluded from benchmarking.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 1,124 47.3% 2.2%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 998 44.4% 2.0%
    ☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue? 1,201 37.5% 2.4%
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 1,793 34.1% 2.5%
      Test your operational readiness with this worksheet 83 33.7% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset share two clear traits: they use bracketed format labels like '[You're invited]' or '[Report]' and they anchor the subject line to a specific, quantified business pain — revenue loss, time-to-revenue, or enrollment delays. Personalized or event-style invitations (coffee break, webinar invite) dramatically outperformed broadcast-style newsletter sends, even when sent to similar list sizes. The weakest performers were large-list blasts with vague or clever subject lines (e.g., 'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays'). The primary lever to pull next time is leading with a bracketed content type and a concrete cost or time metric in the subject line, especially for smaller, more targeted segments.

    Strong Signal

    Bracketed format labels in subject lines are strongly associated with higher open rates.

    Top 4 open rates (47.3%, 44.4%, 34.1%, 29.5%) all use bracketed prefixes like '[You're invited]' or '[Report]'.

    Try this next: Prefix future subject lines with a bracketed content label such as '[Report]', '[You're invited]', or '[Webinar]' to signal value type immediately.

    Subjects referencing specific financial or time-based costs ('$1M+', 'time to revenue') outperform those using vague operational language.

    The two emails referencing 'time to revenue' achieved 47.3% and 44.4% open rates vs. a median of ~17% for the rest.

    Try this next: Include a specific dollar or time metric in the subject line to make the cost of inaction tangible.

    Personalized or event-style framing ('coffee break,' 'you're invited') lifts open rates significantly above standard content emails.

    The coffee-break-themed emails achieved 47.3%, 44.4%, and 37.5% open rates — the top three in the dataset.

    Try this next: Frame high-priority sends as a personal invitation or informal conversation rather than a content broadcast.

    Emails focused on provider readiness and enrollment delay themes outperform hospital-closure or general access themes.

    Provider readiness and enrollment emails average ~35% open rate in the top tier vs. ~17% for hospital-closure series emails.

    Try this next: Prioritize provider readiness and enrollment delay angles over broader healthcare system topics when developing new content campaigns.

    Moderate Signal

    Shorter, more targeted list segments produce higher open rates than large broadcast sends, but the relationship is imperfect.

    The 5 emails sent to 3,357–3,852 recipients average 12.7% open rate vs. 25.5% average for emails sent to under 1,300.
    Show reasoning

    Large-list sends (3,300–3,900) consistently land in the bottom third of the open-rate ranking. However, some mid-size sends (1,640–1,793) also perform well (30–34%), so list size alone does not explain variance — content and subject line matter too. Segmentation quality cannot be assessed from this data.

    Pun-based or wordplay subject lines underperform, including the worst performer in the dataset.

    'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' — sent to 3,852 recipients — achieved only 6.6% open rate, the lowest in the dataset.
    Show reasoning

    This is a single data point but it is a striking outlier: the largest send in the dataset produced the worst open rate. The coffee pun may have read as gimmicky to a B2B audience that responded well to direct coffee-break invitations. One data point warrants caution but the signal aligns with the broader pattern that vague or clever framing underperforms specific, direct language.

    The '[Report]' subject line sent to three different segments on the same day shows click rate rising as list size falls.

    Segment of 877 achieved 2.8% click rate vs. 2.5% for 1,793 and 1.1% for 1,103 — same subject, same date.
    Show reasoning

    The January 21 report email was sent to three audience segments. The smallest segment had the highest click rate and the largest had the middle click rate, while the mid-size segment had the lowest — suggesting the smallest segment was likely the most qualified or engaged. Open rates followed a similar pattern (29.5% vs. 34.1% vs. 13.1%), though the largest list outperformed the mid-size one on opens, adding some noise.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear, consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top performer has 83 characters; emails ranked 6th and 8th use 55 and 53 characters respectively.

    Both long and short subject lines appear across the full range of open rates. The top-performing subject ('You're invited... costs some teams $1M+') is one of the longest, while some of the shortest subjects ('Why care access is breaking down') are mid-to-low performers. No directional pattern holds.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not surface a reliable pattern from this data.

    Top open rates span April 30, May 21, May 7, and January 21 — no single time window dominates.

    High and low performers are distributed across multiple months and no clustering around a specific day or week is visible from the available date fields. Without send-time (hour) data and day-of-week information, no timing conclusion can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple rows representing distinct list segments sent on the same date (notably the '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' email on 2026-01-21 sent to three segments of 1,793, 877, and 1,103 recipients, and the 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet' email sent to segments of 83, 711, and 65). Treating these as independent data points inflates the apparent sample size; performance differences between them reflect segment quality, not subject line variation. Three emails in the top 10 were sent to audiences of 569–1,201, while the weakest performers were sent to 3,357–3,852, introducing a list-size confound that makes it difficult to isolate subject line effects from audience quality. The total dataset of 31 rows covers only 23 unique subject lines across a 10-month window, limiting statistical confidence for any single pattern.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 79 44.3% 1.3%
    In Vegas? Join us for Medallion’s Emerald Evening: A Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience 70 24.3% 5.7%
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 304 22.1% 3.4%
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 1,463 12.8% 3.2%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 1,336 12.2% 0.4%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails share two traits: they were sent to small, highly targeted lists (under ~300 recipients) and they led with a specific, named venue or unique experience rather than generic 'exclusive dinner' language. The top open rate (44.3%) came from a 79-person list for a JPM conference dinner at a named restaurant, suggesting audience precision matters more than subject line polish. To improve future campaigns, prioritize tight segmentation over broad blasts, and name the venue or experience in the subject line rather than relying on scarcity or prestige framing alone.

    Strong Signal

    Smaller, more targeted send lists consistently outperform large broadcast sends on open rate.

    Emails sent to fewer than 110 recipients averaged ~24% open rate vs. ~7% for sends over 900 recipients.

    Try this next: Cap cold invite list sizes at under 150 and invest in tighter ICP filtering before sending; reserve large blasts only for reminder sends to already-engaged contacts.

    Scarcity and urgency subject lines ('Final Seats,' 'Last Call,' 'Last Chance') do not rescue late-stage reminder emails sent to large lists.

    'Final Seats Remaining' to 1,463 recipients achieved 12.8%; 'Last Call' and 'Last Chance' subjects to 659–946 recipients averaged 5.2%.

    Try this next: Stop relying on urgency subject lines as a fallback for large-list sends; instead redirect budget toward sourcing better-targeted lists before the event.

    Moderate Signal

    Naming the specific venue or experience in the subject line is associated with higher open rates.

    44.3% open rate for 'Flour + Water During JPM' vs. 3.0–5.4% for generic 'Exclusive Evening with Healthcare Executives' subjects
    Show reasoning

    The top four open rates all name a concrete venue or experience (Flour + Water, Emerald Evening Sphere, Aquavit). By contrast, the bottom cluster of emails use interchangeable language like 'Exclusive Evening with Healthcare Executives' or 'Top Healthcare Executives' and cluster between 3–5%. The caveat is that list size and targeting quality are confounded with subject line style in this dataset, making it hard to isolate subject wording as the sole driver.

    Unique experiential hooks (sports events, theatrical experiences) drive higher click rates than dinner-only invitations.

    'Emerald Evening: Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience' achieved 5.7% click rate vs. a median click rate of ~0.5% across dinner-only emails.
    Show reasoning

    The two highest click rates (5.7% and 4.3%) came from an immersive Sphere experience email and a combined dinner+booth invite. Standard dinner invitations dominate the lower end of click rates (0.0–1.6%). The sample of experiential emails is small (2–3 examples), limiting confidence, but the directional gap is notable.

    Sending the same subject line to multiple sequential list segments produces diminishing open rates.

    'A Seat at the Table: Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit' dropped from 12.2% (1,336 recipients, Sept 10) to 11.3% (1,380 recipients, Sept 4) when sent days apart.
    Show reasoning

    Several campaigns reused identical subject lines across segmented sends within days of each other (Aquavit, Las Vegas Golden Knights, Emerald Evening). In each case the later or larger send underperformed the first. This is consistent with audience overlap or fatigue, though it could also reflect segment quality differences.

    No Signal

    Casual, conversational subject lines do not clearly outperform formal invitation-style subjects.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' averaged 10.1% open rate across three sends, similar to formal subjects at comparable list sizes.

    The informal 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' subject lines (10–12% open rates) and the formal 'A Seat at the Table' or 'Invitation: Executive Dinner' subjects (11–44%) overlap in performance range. Because list sizes differ substantially across these groups, it is not possible to attribute open rate differences to tone or formality from this data alone.

    No clear send-day or timing pattern is visible in this dataset.

    Top open rates span dates from September through May with no clustering on specific days or months.

    The data does not include send day-of-week or time-of-day. Send dates span September 2025 through May 2026 and top performers are distributed across multiple months (January, February, September), so no seasonal pattern is discernible. Without day-of-week data, no timing recommendation can be supported.

    Subject line length does not show a consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Shortest subject ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?', ~22 characters) averages 10.1% open rate; medium-length subjects dominate both top and bottom of the performance ranking.

    The highest-performing subject ('Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM', ~54 characters) is mid-length, while short casual subjects and long descriptive subjects appear throughout the performance range. List size differences make it impossible to isolate length as a factor here.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments (e.g., 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' sent to 79 and 304 recipients; 'A Seat at the Table: Aquavit' sent twice; 'Emerald Evening' sent to 70 and 103 recipients; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent three times), making it difficult to isolate subject line quality from list size and targeting quality. The dataset's most dramatic performance differences almost certainly reflect audience segmentation rather than subject line copy alone. Small sends (under 100 recipients) will naturally show higher variance in open rates, so the 44.3% top result should be interpreted with caution given only ~35 opens were required to achieve it. No click-through destination or conversion data is available, so click rates cannot be tied to pipeline outcomes.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your December Roundup ✨ 235 41.9% 1.3%
    Your November Roundup ✨ 202 41.8% 2.5%
    Your July Roundup ✨ 236 40.2% 2.1%
    Your August Roundup ✨ 316 34.0% 0.9%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 305 32.9% 0.3%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing newsletter emails share a personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format and were sent to smaller, likely more engaged audience segments, consistently achieving open rates above 40%. As the list grew from ~200–300 recipients to 1,200–1,400, open rates declined noticeably into the low 30s for the 'Medallion Product Update' branded emails. The shift from the personal 'Your Roundup' framing to the more corporate 'Medallion Product Update' product update format coincided with both list scaling and a drop in engagement, making it impossible to isolate which factor drove the decline. To improve performance at scale, testing the personalized subject line format on the larger list is the most actionable next step.

    Strong Signal

    The single-recipient July Roundup test send (n=1) produced a 0% open rate and should be excluded from analysis.

    n=1 send with 0.0% open rate and 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Exclude test sends (n<10) from performance benchmarking to avoid skewing averages.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform the branded 'Medallion Product Update' format.

    ~8 percentage-point gap in average open rate (40%+ vs. ~30%)
    Show reasoning

    All five emails using 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' achieved open rates of 31.6%–52.2%, while the four 'Medallion Product Update' emails ranged from 27.6%–32.0%. However, these two formats also correspond to very different list sizes (200–300 vs. 1,200–1,400 recipients), so audience composition and list maturity are confounding factors that prevent a clean attribution to subject line wording alone.

    Shorter, possessive subject lines ('Your June Roundup ✨', 22 chars) appear to outperform longer branded ones ('Medallion Product Update - February 2026', 40 chars), but list size confounds this.

    Roundup subjects average ~22 characters vs. ~38 characters for Product Update subjects
    Show reasoning

    There is a directional pattern where shorter, first-person subject lines correlate with higher open rates. However, because subject line length co-varies with the brand format change and audience scale change, this cannot be confirmed as an independent driver from this dataset alone.

    Click rates are low across all emails and decline as open rates decline, but remain low even among top openers.

    Highest click rate is 4.3% (June Roundup, n=23); most emails are under 2%
    Show reasoning

    The June Roundup had both the highest open rate (52.2%) and click rate (4.3%), but it was sent to only 23 people, making this statistic unreliable. For emails sent to 200+ recipients, click rates range from 0.3%–2.5%, suggesting the content or CTAs are not strongly driving clicks regardless of open performance. There is no consistent content-theme signal to isolate since subject lines give no CTA detail.

    Open rates trend downward as list size grows from ~200 to ~1,300 recipients.

    Open rate drops from 40%+ at ~200–300 recipients to ~30% at 1,200–1,400 recipients
    Show reasoning

    There is a visible negative correlation between list size and open rate across this dataset. This could reflect list dilution, less-engaged contacts added over time, or the format change from 'Roundup' to 'Product Update' — all three changes happened simultaneously, so no single cause can be isolated.

    No Signal

    No clear send-day or send-timing pattern is discernible from this data.

    Emails sent across Tuesdays through Mondays with no performance clustering by day

    The dataset does not include send time, and the send dates are spread across different days of the week without a visible performance pattern that separates high from low performers. Timing may matter but this data cannot confirm it.

    Data quality notes: Several important data quality caveats apply. First, 'Your June Roundup ✨' (52.2% open rate) was sent to only 23 recipients, making it a statistical outlier that should not anchor benchmarks. Second, there are two entries for 'Your July Roundup ✨' sent two days apart — one to 236 recipients (40.2% open rate) and one to 1 recipient (0.0%) — suggesting a test send was included in the dataset. Third, the 'Your Roundup' and 'Medallion Product Update' series differ in both subject line format AND audience size (200–300 vs. 1,200–1,400), meaning any performance differences between them cannot be cleanly attributed to subject line wording alone. Conclusions about subject line format should be treated as directional hypotheses to test rather than confirmed findings.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing onboarding emails share a curiosity-gap framing that names a specific operational pain without resolving it in the subject line — phrases like 'silent slowdown,' 'smarter onboarding,' and 'credentialing bottleneck' outperformed more explicit, benefit-forward subjects. Emails that led with an implicit question or incomplete thought drove open rates of 18–38%, while emails that spelled out the value proposition directly (e.g., 'Save up to 64% on cost,' 'scalable credentialing infrastructure') achieved 0% opens. The one standout click performer — 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' at 18.2% CTR — suggests that curiosity-driven opens can also convert when the content delivers on the intrigue. Next time, lean into implied tension and insider framing rather than explicit ROI claims in subject lines.

    Strong Signal

    Curiosity-gap and pain-naming subject lines dramatically outperform benefit-statement subjects.

    37.9% vs. 0.0% open rate

    Try this next: Write subject lines that name a specific operational pain or imply a surprising insight rather than stating the benefit or outcome directly.

    Moderate Signal

    The one email combining a high open rate with a high click rate used 'no one talks about' framing to signal exclusive insider knowledge.

    18.2% click rate on 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about'
    Show reasoning

    This subject line achieved the highest CTR by a wide margin and also delivered a solid 18.2% open rate, suggesting the 'secret/unspoken' framing may drive both opens and engagement. However, this is a single email with only 11 sends, so the result could be a small-sample outlier.

    Emails sent to very small audiences (3–5 recipients) show extreme open-rate variance that is unreliable for comparison.

    Audience sizes range from 3 to 37 recipients
    Show reasoning

    The 0% openers sent to 3 and 4 recipients could represent just 1–2 non-opens, while the 25% result on a 4-person send means only 1 open. These figures are statistically meaningless on their own but directionally suggest the low-send-count emails were sent to cold or poorly matched segments.

    Explicit numeric claims in subject lines ('64%,' '1 day instead of 1 week,' '2x the volume') are associated with low or zero open rates.

    3 of 3 emails with specific numbers in the subject achieved ≤12.5% open rate, two hit 0%
    Show reasoning

    Every subject line containing a quantified claim underperformed the dataset average. This is a consistent directional signal, though sample size is small (n=3) and confounded by the fact that these emails were also more explicit in their benefit framing.

    No Signal

    Subject lines starting with a leading non-breaking space character appear in both top and bottom performers, so the prefix itself is not a differentiating factor.

    8 of 11 emails use the leading space prefix

    The '\ xa0' prefix is present in the majority of emails across the entire open-rate spectrum, from 37.8% down to 0.0%. There is no correlation between its presence and performance, so it cannot be treated as a signal in either direction.

    Subject line length shows no consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top subject ('What smarter onboarding looks like') = 36 chars; bottom performer ('How credentialing teams are handling 2x the volume') = 50 chars

    Short and medium-length subjects appear at both the top and bottom of the open-rate rankings. The range of character counts is not wide enough and the sample not large enough to isolate length as a driver of performance.

    All emails were sent on the same two adjacent dates, so no send-timing pattern can be established.

    10 of 11 emails sent on 2025-12-09, 1 on 2025-12-10

    There is effectively no variation in send timing across this dataset. Any difference attributed to the single December 10 send is confounded by audience and subject line differences, making it impossible to draw timing conclusions.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 11 emails with audience sizes ranging from 3 to 37 recipients — far too small for statistically significant conclusions. Several emails were sent to audiences of 3–5 people, where a single open or non-open swings rates by 20–33 percentage points, making those rows unreliable for pattern analysis. All emails were sent on December 9–10, 2025, eliminating any ability to test send-timing effects. No subject lines appear to be exact repeats, but several address overlapping themes (credentialing, provider data) which may reflect audience segment splits rather than true A/B tests, making direct performance comparisons between thematically similar emails unreliable.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    New profile completion logic is now live 78 55.1% 10.3%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing 94 51.1% 0.0%
    A better analytics experience is coming your way 180 48.3% 0.0%
    A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅ 102 44.1% 0.0%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing 74 33.8% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this set tend to be specific, concrete, and announce something already live rather than previewing something upcoming. The highest open rate (55.1%) went to a short, direct subject confirming a feature was live, while the teaser-style 'coming your way' email underperformed it despite a much larger send list. The one email with meaningful click-through was the same live-announcement email, suggesting that confirmed availability drives not just opens but actual engagement. Future sends should prioritize 'now live' framing and specific feature names over vague anticipatory language.

    Strong Signal

    Sending the same subject line to a second segment one week later produced a significantly lower open rate.

    33.8% vs. 51.1% open rate for identical subject lines sent 7 days apart

    Try this next: When re-sending to a second segment, modify the subject line to differentiate it — even minor wording changes may preserve open rates for the later cohort.

    Moderate Signal

    'Now live' or 'now available' framing outperforms teaser/preview language for open rates.

    55.1% vs 44.1% open rate for live vs. preview announcements of the same profile completion feature
    Show reasoning

    The email announcing profile completion logic was live (55.1%) outperformed the earlier preview teaser for the same feature (44.1%). Similarly, the 'coming your way' analytics teaser (48.3%) was outpaced by the analytics 'now available' send to a comparable audience (51.1%). The pattern is directional but the sample is only 5 emails across two feature pairs.

    Specific, named features in the subject line appear to drive stronger opens than generic benefit language.

    55.1% open rate for 'New profile completion logic is now live' vs. 48.3% for 'A better analytics experience is coming your way'
    Show reasoning

    The top two emails by open rate both name specific features ('profile completion logic', 'Provider Summary and Credentialing'), while the lowest-performing of the top three uses a vague benefit phrase ('a better analytics experience'). However, the teaser vs. live framing is a confounding variable, making it hard to isolate subject line specificity alone.

    Shorter subject lines performed better than longer ones in this dataset.

    The top-performing subject line is 38 characters; the two lowest-performing average over 60 characters
    Show reasoning

    'New profile completion logic is now live' (38 chars, 55.1% open) is the shortest subject and the best performer. The two analytics 'now available' emails share the same long subject (78 chars) and are the two lowest openers at 51.1% and 33.8%. The 'coming your way' email sits in the middle on both length and performance. The pattern is directional but confounded by other variables.

    Emails announcing live features drive clicks; preview and analytics announcement emails generated zero clicks.

    Only 1 of 5 emails had any clicks; that email had a 10.3% click rate
    Show reasoning

    The sole email with a non-zero click rate was the confirmed-live profile completion announcement. All four other emails, including both analytics announcements and both preview/teaser emails, had 0.0% click rates. This may indicate a CTA or content design issue in the non-clicking emails, or that 'now live' copy more naturally prompts users to act. The pattern is notable but a single data point limits confidence.

    No Signal

    No send-timing pattern is detectable in this data.

    5 emails sent across 5 different dates with no repeat day-of-week clustering

    The emails span July through September 2025 on different days of the week, and there is no repeated send day to compare against performance. With only 5 data points and no controlled timing variation, no conclusion about send day or time can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails, which is too small to establish statistically robust patterns — all findings should be treated as directional hypotheses to test rather than confirmed truths. Audience sizes vary significantly (74 to 180), meaning raw open counts differ substantially even when rates look comparable; the 180-recipient send in particular could behave differently due to list composition. Two emails share an identical subject line but were sent to different segments one week apart, making it unclear whether performance differences reflect timing, segment quality, or both — these should not be treated as independent data points for subject-line analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Share your experience with Medallion? 156 20.1% 1.9%
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 5,447 19.7% 3.0%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 4,888 19.1% 1.1%
    Your insights are needed! 6,006 17.6% 2.1%
    Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments 95 16.0% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    Survey emails with explicit incentive mentions or urgent urgency framing in the subject line consistently outperform generic 'share your thoughts' phrasing, with top openers clustering around 17–20% open rates. The clear outlier is 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20,' which despite having the largest audience (10,608) and including a monetary incentive posted only a 5% open rate — suggesting audience fatigue or list quality issues when blasting at scale rather than a targeted segment. Small-list sends (under 200) show competitive open rates but negligible click volume, making them statistically unreliable. The playbook implication: monetary incentives help only when paired with personalization or urgency, not as a standalone hook to a cold, large list.

    Moderate Signal

    Monetary incentive copy lifts opens only when combined with personalization or urgency — not alone.

    5.0% open rate for '$20' offer sent to 10,608 vs. 19.7% for personalized '$20 for your insights' sent to 5,447
    Show reasoning

    The '$20 for your insights' email with a first-name personalization token achieved a 19.7% open rate, while 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' sent to nearly double the audience got only 5.0%. The incentive alone did not drive performance; the combination of personalization and incentive framing appears to matter. However, the two sends also differ in audience size and date, so audience composition could also explain the gap.

    Personalization tokens in the subject line are associated with higher open rates among incentivized survey emails.

    19.7% open rate for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' vs. 5.0% for non-personalized '$20' subject
    Show reasoning

    The only email using a first-name token achieved the second-highest open rate (19.7%) and the highest click rate (3.0%) among large-list sends. This directional signal is meaningful, but only one email used personalization, so it cannot be isolated from other variables like send date or audience segment.

    Urgency language ('Last call,' 'Last chance') sustains strong open rates on follow-up sends.

    19.1% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' on a 4,888-person list
    Show reasoning

    'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' achieved a 19.1% open rate — the third-highest in the dataset — suggesting that urgency framing on a reminder send can maintain engagement rather than decay. However, only one valid urgency-framed send had meaningful volume (the '\ Last chance' entries had 0 sends), so the pattern cannot be fully confirmed.

    Click rate is highest when both personalization and monetary incentive appear together in the subject line.

    3.0% click rate for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' — highest among all sends with meaningful volume
    Show reasoning

    The personalized incentive email achieved the highest click rate (3.0%) among sends over 1,000 recipients, nearly double the next-best large-list click rate (2.1%). This suggests the combination drives not just opens but actual survey engagement. Caveat: only one email used this combination.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~25 to ~75 characters with no monotonic relationship to open rate

    The top opener ('Share your experience with Medallion?', ~38 chars) and the bottom valid opener ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20', ~41 chars) are nearly the same length. Longer descriptive subjects like 'Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments' (~72 chars) fall in the middle of the range. No length-based pattern is supported.

    Send timing (day/month) cannot be isolated as a driver of performance with this dataset.

    Top-performing sends span June 2026 and September–October 2025 with no clustering

    The highest open rate email was sent June 2026, but the dataset has only 8 emails spread across different months and years, with audience sizes varying by two orders of magnitude. There is no basis to attribute open rate differences to send timing rather than subject line, personalization, or list composition.

    Product-specific, niche subject lines perform adequately on small lists but cannot be evaluated at scale.

    16.0% open rate for 'Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments' sent to only 95 recipients

    This highly specific subject line achieved a 16.0% open rate but was sent to only 95 people, likely a pre-qualified micro-segment. The result is not generalizable to larger sends and does not confirm or deny whether specificity drives opens independently of audience targeting.

    Data quality notes: Three of eight emails have critical data issues: two entries show 0 sends and 0% rates (likely draft or unsent emails from 2026-06-29) and should be excluded from analysis; one send ('Share your experience with Medallion?') had only 156 recipients, making its 20.1% open rate statistically unreliable. Audience sizes across valid sends range from 95 to 10,608, meaning open rate comparisons are heavily confounded by list quality and segmentation differences rather than subject line effects alone. No subject lines appear to be exact duplicates across sends, but several are clearly sequential campaign touches (e.g., 'Your insights are needed' → '$20 for your insights' → 'Last call'), so opens on later sends may reflect the cumulative effect of the campaign sequence rather than individual subject line performance.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 2,010 64.6% 1.8%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 735 54.7% 2.2%
    You’re So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today 56 53.6% 32.1%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  1,021 48.8% 4.3%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 972 42.8% 1.8%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing webinar emails in this dataset are overwhelmingly sent to small, targeted audiences (fewer than 100 recipients) and follow up on specific actions — confirming attendance, thanking attendees, or delivering on-demand content to people who already engaged. For large-list sends (1,000+), urgency-framed subject lines with concrete incentives like gift boxes or coffee kits consistently outperform vague invitations or feature-focused pitches. The clearest opportunity going forward is to replicate the segmentation logic of the top performers — treat post-registration and post-event audiences as distinct high-value segments with dedicated messaging — rather than optimizing subject line wording alone on broad cold lists.

    Strong Signal

    Post-event and on-demand follow-up emails to small engaged audiences generate the highest open rates.

    Top 6 emails by open rate all had fewer than 60 recipients and ranged from 53.6% to 66.7% open rate.

    Try this next: Always build dedicated post-event and on-demand notification segments, even if small, and send them purpose-built emails rather than repurposing broad-list templates.

    Urgency framing combined with a tangible physical incentive (gift, coffee kit) drives strong open rates on mid-to-large lists.

    'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (735 sent, 54.7%) and 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (1,021 sent, 48.8%) outperform comparably sized sends by 15–25 percentage points.

    Try this next: For large-list sends, lead with a specific, tangible incentive in the subject line and pair it with scarcity language ('last chance,' 'claim today') rather than topic-only descriptions.

    Broad cold-list sends (10,000+ recipients) consistently underperform, rarely exceeding 26% open rate regardless of subject line approach.

    All 6 sends with 10,000+ recipients fell between 15.7% and 25.7% open rate, with a median near 21%.

    Try this next: Segment large lists by engagement tier before sending — even splitting into 'previously opened any email' vs. 'cold' subsets — rather than treating the full list as one audience.

    On-demand content emails ('watch now,' 'available on demand') drive notably high click rates alongside strong open rates.

    'Demo Day available on demand' (15 sent): 26.7% click rate; 'Watch Medallion Demo Day on demand' (25 sent): 20.0% click rate; '[Watch on demand]: Navigating cost...' (83 sent): 9.8% click rate.

    Try this next: Prioritize a dedicated on-demand notification email for every live webinar, sent to the registered-but-did-not-attend segment within 24 hours of the event.

    Moderate Signal

    'You're So Close' phrasing — implying near-conversion — outperforms standard urgency language on both small and large lists.

    'You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!' (2,010 sent) hit 64.6% open rate; 'You're So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (56 sent) hit 53.6%.
    Show reasoning

    Both subject lines using the exact 'You're So Close' framing performed exceptionally. However, the 2,010-recipient send is an outlier — most large sends in this data peak around 30–32%, making this result unusual enough to warrant retesting before drawing firm conclusions. The small-list version is harder to isolate from the audience quality effect.

    Personalization tokens used as fallback text (not actual first names) show no open rate advantage over non-personalized subject lines.

    Two emails using '{{ personalization_token('contact.firstname', 'Time is running out') }}' sent to 10,864 and 10,332 recipients achieved only 22.8% and 20.0% open rates respectively.
    Show reasoning

    These sends went out with broken or fallback personalization tokens visible in the subject line, which likely rendered as 'Time is running out' for many recipients. This may have actively harmed performance relative to cleanly written urgency lines. Without a clean A/B test this cannot be fully isolated, but it serves as a clear data quality and execution warning.

    'We missed you' re-engagement subject lines achieve above-average open rates relative to their list size.

    'We missed you!' (11 sent, 54.5%) and 'We missed you at Elevate!' (224 sent, 30.9%) both outperform the baseline for their respective audience sizes.
    Show reasoning

    Both versions of the 'We missed you' framing outperform typical sends to similar-sized audiences, suggesting that acknowledging a missed connection resonates. However, the 11-recipient send is too small to weight heavily, and the 224-recipient version, while solid at 30.9%, is within range of other well-performing mid-list sends, making the pattern directional rather than definitive.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent directional relationship with open rates in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short lines ('We missed you!', 54.5%) and long lines ('[Watch on demand]: Navigating cost, innovation and credentialing and payer enrollment trends', 40.2%).

    Across this dataset, audience size and lifecycle stage dominate open rate variation so strongly that subject line length cannot be isolated as a meaningful independent variable. Short lines appear at both the top and bottom of the ranking, as do long lines. No actionable length pattern is evident.

    Send timing (day of week or time of year) does not show a clear, consistent pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing sends are distributed across 2025 and 2026 with no clustering around specific months or days.

    The data provides send dates but not send times, and the dominant variable driving open rates is audience segment size and lifecycle stage rather than calendar timing. Without controlling for audience type, no send-timing signal can be reliably extracted from this data.

    Data quality notes: This dataset has a significant audience-size skew: the top-ranked emails by open rate were sent to as few as 8–56 recipients, making their rates statistically unreliable and almost certainly driven by audience quality (post-event attendees, near-converters) rather than subject line copy alone. Several subject lines appear in multiple rows with different sent counts and open rates, indicating the same or similar message was sent to different segments on different dates (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' appears twice; 'You're invited: Rethinking cost and innovation' appears twice; 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' appears three times). Two subject lines contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens, suggesting a deliverability or template error that may have suppressed performance. With 65 total emails spanning a wide range of audience sizes, lifecycle stages, and campaign types, cross-email comparisons should be treated as directional signals rather than statistically validated findings.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 833 69.3% 4.0%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 558 61.8% 5.4%
    Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit 183 58.5% 9.3%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 734 56.0% 6.4%
    Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere  120 55.8% 12.5%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing webinar emails in this dataset cluster around two scenarios: highly personalized post-event follow-ups (thank-you and on-demand watch emails) and urgency-driven pre-event reminders with a tangible incentive (coffee kit, gift box). The common thread is that recipients already have a relationship with the content — they registered, attended, or were very close to the event date — which inflates opens organically. For cold or broad audiences, open rates drop sharply into the 20–35% range regardless of subject line creativity. The key implication is to invest more in segmenting warm engaged contacts and sending them distinct, reward-focused messaging rather than treating the full list with the same promotional copy.

    Strong Signal

    Post-event follow-up and on-demand availability emails achieve the highest open rates in the dataset.

    Top 5 emails by open rate (57.9–80%) are all post-event or on-demand access messages.

    Try this next: Always send a dedicated on-demand or thank-you email to attendees and registrants within 24 hours of an event; treat this as a separate, high-priority segment with its own subject line distinct from the general list.

    Physical incentive subject lines (coffee kit, gift box) drive strong opens even on large sends.

    Coffee/gift-box subjects averaged ~59% open rate across sends of 19–852 recipients.

    Try this next: Frame pre-event reminder emails around a claimable physical reward (e.g., 'Claim your [item]') rather than a thematic event perk; use 'Last Chance' urgency on the final send to recover non-openers.

    Very large sends (n>2,000) consistently underperform, rarely exceeding 40% open rate.

    All 10 sends with n≥2,469 recipients scored between 28.1% and 39.6% open rate.

    Try this next: Prioritize list segmentation over subject line optimization for large sends; reserve high-performing subject line frameworks (incentive, post-event) for the warmest, most engaged sub-segments rather than the full database.

    Moderate Signal

    'Bitter truth' curiosity-gap subject lines drove above-average opens on very large sends.

    'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' averaged 52.4% open rate across two sends (n=734 and n=931).
    Show reasoning

    Both sends of this subject exceeded 48% open rate on audiences of 700–900+, well above the 35–38% typical for broad promotional sends. However, there are only two data points and they may share audience overlap since they were sent one week apart (Oct 1 and Oct 8), so the second send to a largely different segment could be inflating apparent consistency.

    Urgency language ('Last Chance,' 'So Close,' '⏰ Last Chance!') is associated with higher open rates when paired with a concrete hook.

    Urgency + incentive subjects averaged ~60% open rate vs. urgency-only subjects averaging ~39%.
    Show reasoning

    'Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit' (58.5%), 'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (61.8%), and 'You're So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (57.9%) all combine urgency with a tangible reward. Pure urgency without an incentive — '⏰ Last Chance! Time is running out, Elevate is on Wednesday!' (39.6%), '[Last Chance!] Executive briefing on credentialing risk & AI adoption' (38.5%) — performs near the broad-list average. The distinction is the claimable reward, not the urgency alone, though audience warmth differences could also explain part of the gap.

    No Signal

    Personalization tokens in subject lines did not produce a notable open-rate advantage.

    Two emails using a broken personalization token ('{{ personalization_token... }}') scored 36.6% and 34.9% open rates — near the broad-list median.

    The data contains two emails where the HubSpot personalization token failed to resolve, displaying raw template syntax in the subject line. Despite this apparent defect, both emails still achieved open rates within the normal range for their large audience sizes (3,962–4,183 recipients), suggesting either the fallback value ('Time is running out') was displayed or subject-line personalization has negligible impact on opens at this list size. There are no equivalent sends with correctly resolved tokens to compare against.

    Subject lines with bracketed tags like '[You're invited]' or '[Watch on demand]' perform near the middle of the range and show no consistent lift.

    Bracketed-tag subjects ranged from 36.1% to 54.2% open rate — essentially the same range as the full dataset.

    Seven emails use leading bracket tags. The highest performer is '[Watch on demand]: Navigating cost...' at 54.2%, but that is a warm on-demand audience. The remaining bracketed subjects (38–46%) are not distinguishably better or worse than non-bracketed equivalents sent to similar audiences. There is no clean A/B comparison to isolate the bracket effect.

    Shorter subject lines (under ~50 characters) do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short ('We missed you!' at 77.8%) and long ('You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!' at 69.3%).

    The highest-performing subjects span a wide length range. Short lines dominate the very top but those are tiny warm-list sends. Among statistically meaningful sends (n>100), length does not sort cleanly with open rate — e.g., the 47-character 'How AI is transforming hospital operations' (n=804) scores 50.9% while the shorter 'Payer enrollment timelines: are you ahead or behind?' (n=885) scores 41.9%. No actionable length threshold emerges from this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of year) shows no clear pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails appear in July, August, September, October, November, and 2026 months with no seasonal clustering.

    The data does not include send-time (hour) information. Looking at date alone, top open rates appear in August and November, but those correspond to warm post-event segments rather than calendar timing. Broad sends in the same months score 25–40%. Without send-time data and without controlling for audience segment, no timing signal can be isolated.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply: (1) The top 5 emails by open rate were sent to 4–19 recipients, making those rates statistically unreliable — a single extra open swings the rate by 5–25 percentage points. (2) Two subjects ('Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' and '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' and 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality') appear multiple times with different send sizes and dates, likely representing resends to non-openers or different segments; their rates should not be averaged without knowing audience overlap. (3) Two emails contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens in the subject line, indicating a send error. (4) Several subject lines contain leading non-breaking space characters (\xa0), suggesting copy-paste formatting issues that could affect how subjects render in inboxes. (5) No click-through-to-open rate is provided, so click rates conflate opens and clicks; the 44.4% click rate on 'Thank you for spending time with us' (n=9) equals 4 clicks and should not be over-indexed.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon 74 73.0% 14.9%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding 70 71.4% 1.4%
    New fields now live for provider, group, facility, and practice location profiles  103 61.2% 1.0%
    Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience 95 59.0% 1.1%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing 89 58.4% 3.4%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails tend to be action-oriented and specific — they name a concrete feature, regulatory change, or benefit rather than speaking generically. The highest-engagement emails (excluding single-send outliers) combine urgency or novelty cues in the subject line with a clear reason the recipient needs to act or learn something. Broad-list funding announcements consistently underperform compared to product and compliance-focused sends, suggesting this audience responds to operational relevance over company news. Future sends should prioritize specificity, compliance/deadline framing, and targeted segmentation over large-list blasts.

    Strong Signal

    Action-required and urgency-framed subject lines drive above-average open rates on meaningful list sizes.

    78.6% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' (n=42)

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with 'Action required' or 'Upcoming changes' framing whenever the email involves a compliance deadline, regulatory shift, or mandatory platform change.

    Funding announcement emails significantly underperform product and compliance emails with this audience.

    The four $43M funding emails averaged 36.3% open rate vs. ~57% for top product/compliance emails

    Try this next: Reframe any future funding or company-news emails around the operational or product benefit to the recipient rather than the company achievement.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under ~20 recipients) produce unreliable open rate signals and should not be used for benchmarking.

    The single-recipient send logged 100.0% open rate; the 3-recipient send logged 0.0%

    Try this next: Exclude sends with fewer than 40 recipients from open-rate benchmarking and optimization decisions.

    Click rates are generally low across the dataset, but feedback/survey and advisory board emails are clear outliers.

    'Thank You + Quick Feedback Request' achieved 64.3% CTR; 'Welcome + Member Kit for Medallion Customer Advisory Board' achieved 42.9% CTR — next highest is 14.9%

    Try this next: For product announcement emails targeting the broader list, test including one explicit, low-friction CTA (e.g., 'See what changed') rather than informational-only copy to close the gap between open and click rates.

    Moderate Signal

    'Coming soon' and preview-framing subjects generate solid engagement and can prime a follow-up 'now live' email.

    'Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required' opened at 54.6% (n=108), and its follow-up 'Now live' email opened at 54.0% (n=111)
    Show reasoning

    Several 'Coming soon' emails performed at or above the dataset median and appear paired with corresponding 'Now live' sends that maintained similar open rates, suggesting the two-touch approach sustains engagement. However, 'Coming soon: Smarter payer selection' opened at only 45.6% (n=92), so the tactic is not universally strong.

    New analytics report announcements perform well on open rate but generate very low click-through, suggesting recipients read but don't explore.

    Four 'New Analytic reports' emails averaged ~54% open rate but averaged only 3.2% CTR
    Show reasoning

    Analytics report announcement emails consistently achieve above-median opens (ranging from 38.9% to 71.4%) but almost no clicks, suggesting recipients find the subject line relevant but the email itself doesn't compel them to act. The 71.4% open rate send (n=70) achieved only 1.4% CTR.

    No Signal

    Shorter, benefit-specific subject lines do not consistently outperform longer descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Top subjects range from 7 words ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do') to 4 words ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request')

    There is no clear correlation between subject line character or word count and open rate in this dataset. Both short and long subjects appear across the performance spectrum. The data does not support a length-based recommendation.

    Send timing (day of week or month) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    Top-performing emails are spread across October 2025 through April 2026 with no clustering on specific days

    The data includes dates but not send times, and emails span many months with varying cadences. Without day-of-week granularity and with confounding variables like content type and list size, no timing signal can be extracted reliably from this dataset.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments on the same or adjacent dates (e.g., four '$43M raised' variants on 2025-08-18 and 2025-08-20; two 'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing' sends; two 'Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon' sends; two 'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' sends), making it impossible to isolate subject line wording as the variable — list composition and segmentation also differ. Audience sizes range from 1 to 547, heavily skewing rate comparisons; small-list sends (n<40) should be treated as unreliable benchmarks. The dataset of 31 emails is small enough that a few outlier sends materially affect any aggregate averages.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 864 53.9% 5.9%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 768 53.6% 3.6%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 819 46.9% 4.6%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 834 46.6% 5.2%
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 853 45.7% 3.9%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this dataset consistently use bracketed content-type labels (e.g., '[You're invited]', '[Report]', '[Final chance]'), reference concrete financial or operational consequences (e.g., '$1M+', 'time to revenue', 'payer delays'), and were sent to mid-sized, highly targeted segments rather than the full 2,500+ list. Emails sent to the large broadcast list (2,500–2,600 recipients) clustered at the bottom of open rates (19–27%), suggesting the segmented sends dramatically outperform cold or broad sends. To improve performance, prioritize segmented sends with bracketed subject line framing and lead with specific, quantified pain points rather than abstract process language.

    Strong Signal

    Bracketed labels or emoji prefixes in subject lines are associated with the highest open rates.

    Top 6 open rates (44–54%) all include a bracket '[...]' or coffee-cup emoji '☕' prefix; bottom 5 open rates (19–27%) have none.

    Try this next: Prefix subject lines with a bracketed content-type label ('[Report]', '[Invite]', '[Data]') or a thematically relevant emoji to signal content value upfront.

    Subjects that quantify a financial or operational cost outperform those that describe the problem abstractly.

    The two emails referencing '$1M+' or 'time to revenue' averaged 53.8% open rate vs. a dataset median of ~33%.

    Try this next: Incorporate a specific dollar figure, time metric, or quantified consequence (e.g., '$1M+', '30 days vs. 6') directly in the subject line.

    Sends to smaller, segmented lists (under ~1,000 recipients) consistently outperform large broadcast sends (2,500+).

    All 8 emails sent to 2,500+ recipients averaged 26.1% open rate; the 23 emails sent to under 1,000 averaged 35.8% open rate.

    Try this next: Prioritize segmented sends to high-fit subsets rather than broadcasting to the full list; treat the full list as a fallback or retargeting layer.

    Duplicate subject lines sent to different segments show dramatically different performance, confirming audience segmentation as the dominant variable.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' ranged from 19.4% to 45.7% open rate across three sends on the same date.

    Try this next: Before optimizing subject line copy, audit and tighten list segmentation — audience fit produces larger open-rate swings than any wording pattern observed here.

    Moderate Signal

    A 'coffee break' or casual meeting-hook framing drives strong open rates when paired with a business pain point.

    Both coffee-themed emails averaged 49.5% open rate vs. a dataset median of ~33%.
    Show reasoning

    '[Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue' (53.6%) and '☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue?' (45.4%) both use a low-stakes social hook alongside a substantive pain point. However, only two emails use this pattern, and both went to segmented lists under 1,000, so audience quality may partly explain the lift.

    Hospital closure and operational strain themes perform better in segmented sends but poorly in broad sends — context matters.

    'When hospitals close, the work doesn't stop' to 244 recipients achieved 34.0% open and 9.0% click rate; a comparable broad send of the same subject hit only 37.4% open and 1.4% click.
    Show reasoning

    The hospital closure theme appears in multiple sends across different segment sizes. Smaller, more targeted segments show higher click rates (9.0% vs. 1.4%), suggesting the topic resonates strongly with a specific audience but dilutes on a broad send. The open rate gap is smaller, so the real value is in click-through quality for the right segment.

    Payer enrollment and credentialing report content drives high open rates but only moderate click rates.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' achieved 45.7% open but only 3.9% click in its best send; a larger send of the same subject got 19.4% open and 2.2% click.
    Show reasoning

    Report-framed emails attract opens through the '[Report]' label but the click rate (2.2–5.3%) is not proportionally elevated, suggesting recipients open out of curiosity but do not consistently act. The same subject line sent to three different segments shows a wide open-rate range (19–46%), reinforcing that audience fit matters more than the content label alone.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open rate performance.

    Top subject line is 79 characters; bottom subject line is 57 characters — no consistent directional trend.

    Long subjects appear at both the top ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)') and the bottom of the performance range. Short subjects also span the full range. There is no monotonic or threshold pattern visible in this dataset.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not surface a clear performance pattern from this data.

    Top open rates span April, May, August, October, and November — no single month or period dominates.

    High-performing emails are distributed across 2025-07 through 2026-05 with no clustering around a particular month, week, or apparent day pattern. Without send-time-of-day data and with confounding variables (segment size, subject framing), no timing conclusion can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across 2–3 separate sends (e.g., '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' sent three times on the same date; 'When hospitals close, the work doesn't stop' sent twice on the same date; 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet' sent three times on the same date), almost certainly representing A/B segment splits rather than independent experiments — performance differences reflect audience segment quality, not subject line variation. Additionally, two sends had very small audiences (n=45 and n=36), making their open rates statistically unreliable. The four large broadcast sends (2,500+ recipients) represent a structurally different audience from the segmented sends and should not be directly compared without controlling for list source and engagement history. Several subject lines also contain a leading non-breaking space character ('\xa0') which may affect rendering in some email clients.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 159 34.0% 5.0%
    You’re Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy 176 30.1% 1.7%
    Reserve Your Seat: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Atlanta 136 29.4% 1.5%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 705 28.9% 2.7%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 688 26.2% 3.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails combine specific venue names, exclusive framing, and conference or event context (e.g., JPM, Vegas experiences) to drive above-average open rates in the 25–34% range. Emails tied to a named conference moment or a unique experiential hook (sports event, Sphere experience) consistently outperform generic 'healthcare executives dinner' framing. Urgency language ('Final Seats,' 'Last Chance') shows mixed results — it lifts performance when the underlying event concept is already strong, but fails to rescue weak concepts. The clearest opportunity is to lead with the specific venue or experience name and tie invitations to a recognizable external event anchor rather than relying on seniority or exclusivity language alone.

    Strong Signal

    Emails naming a specific restaurant or experience in the subject line outperform generic 'exclusive dinner' subjects.

    Top 6 emails by open rate (25–34%) all name a specific venue or event (Flour + Water, Aquavit, Golden Knights, Sphere, Juniper and Ivy, Atlanta Michelin-Starred Lazy Betty) vs. bottom 6 averaging 10.2% open rate with only vague descriptors.

    Try this next: Always include the specific venue or experience name in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive dinner' or role-based framing alone.

    Tying the invite to a known external conference or cultural moment (JPM, Vegas trip) correlates with the highest open rates.

    The three emails referencing an external event anchor (JPM conference, Las Vegas trip) averaged 31.6% open rate vs. 18.4% for non-anchored dinner invites.

    Try this next: When a target conference or city trip is known, frame the invite around that trip ('Already heading to [City] for [Conference]?') rather than as a standalone event.

    Repeat sends of the same subject line to additional or re-engaged segments show modest but consistent open rate decay.

    'A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York' dropped from 28.9% (705 sent, Sept 4) to 26.2% (688 sent, Sept 10) on a near-identical resend.

    Try this next: Limit repeat sends of the same subject line to a maximum of one follow-up, and modify the subject line meaningfully on any subsequent send rather than reusing identical copy.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, conversational subject lines ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') perform comparably to formal exclusivity framing at similar list sizes.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (49 sent) achieved 24.5% open rate vs. 20.8% for the more formal 'A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Table in Boston' (149 sent).
    Show reasoning

    The Stillwell's emails use an informal register with '@' and a question mark, stripping out the 'exclusive/seat at the table' language entirely. They perform solidly, suggesting formal exclusivity copy is not required when the venue name is present. However, the Stillwell's sends went to smaller, likely more curated lists, so list quality may partially explain performance rather than subject line wording alone.

    Urgency/scarcity language ('Final Seats,' 'Last Chance,' 'Last Call') works as a follow-up tactic for strong event concepts but fails as a primary hook.

    'Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston' hit 25.4% open rate, while 'Last Chance: Exclusive Evening with Tennessee Healthcare Executives' hit only 10.0%.
    Show reasoning

    When urgency language is applied to an event that already had strong earlier sends (Boston dinner sequence peaked at 25.4% on the final-seats email), it can maintain or lift performance. But when it is used on a campaign that was underperforming from the start (Nashville/Tennessee sequence), it does not recover the open rate. This suggests urgency amplifies existing interest rather than creating it. Sample is limited to follow-up emails within the same campaigns, so causal direction is uncertain.

    Small, targeted list sends (under 60 recipients) show higher open rates but provide unreliable benchmarks due to sample size.

    Emails sent to fewer than 60 recipients averaged 32.8% open rate vs. 19.1% for emails sent to 100+ recipients.
    Show reasoning

    The two highest open-rate emails (100% on 2 recipients, 31.8% on 44 recipients) and several other top performers went to very small lists, which are likely hand-curated and thus higher quality. The 100%-open-rate email with only 2 recipients provides no actionable signal. The general pattern of smaller lists performing better is likely an audience-quality effect, not a subject line or format effect. However, the Aquavit email (705 sent, 28.9%) shows strong performance is achievable at scale with the right concept.

    No Signal

    Shorter subject lines do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Top-performing subjects range from 7 words ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') to 17 words ('A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York'), with no clear length-to-open-rate correlation.

    The highest open rates appear in both very short (casual Stillwell's) and very long (Aquavit, Flour + Water) subjects. The bottom performers include both short and medium-length subjects. Length alone does not predict performance in this dataset; content specificity appears to be the differentiating factor regardless of length.

    There is no detectable send-day or send-timing pattern that explains open rate differences in this dataset.

    High-performing emails were sent across Monday, Thursday, Wednesday, and Friday with no single day dominating the top quartile.

    The data does not include send time, and send dates span weekdays without a clustering pattern among top performers. August, September, October, November, and May all contain both high and low performers. Without time-of-day data and with confounding variables (list size, event type, conference anchors), no timing signal can be isolated from this dataset.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains 31 sends but includes several repeated subject lines across multiple segments or follow-up sends (Aquavit sent twice, Golden Knights sent five times, Stillwell's sent three times, Emerald Evening sent twice), meaning there are roughly 20 distinct campaign concepts rather than 31 independent experiments. Audience sizes vary enormously (2 to 749 recipients), which heavily influences the reliability of open and click rates — the 100% open rate on 2 recipients and several sub-50-recipient sends should not be weighted equally with sends of 500+. Click rate data is sparse and low across the board (most under 6%), limiting conclusions about CTAs or content. No send-time data is available, and no information on list segmentation criteria, industry targeting, or prior engagement history is provided, making it difficult to isolate subject line effects from audience-quality effects.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your December Roundup ✨ 102 60.8% 3.9%
    Your July Roundup ✨ 114 58.8% 0.9%
    Your September Roundup ✨ 142 58.5% 2.8%
    Your November Roundup ✨ 99 57.6% 6.1%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 138 57.2% 1.5%
    What this tells us

    The 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject line format consistently outperformed the 'Medallion Product Update' format, achieving open rates of 57–67% versus 46–51% — a gap of roughly 7–15 percentage points. This advantage held even at comparable audience sizes, suggesting the personalized, possessive framing ('Your') and sparkle emoji drive meaningfully stronger engagement. However, the Roundup emails show highly variable click rates (0–6.1%), indicating that opens are reliable but click-through depends on content quality or CTA strength within each issue. Future newsletters should retain the 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' framing and prioritize clearer in-email CTAs to convert the high open rate into clicks.

    Strong Signal

    The 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject line format drives higher open rates than 'Medallion Product Update - [Month Year]'.

    ~7–15 percentage point open rate advantage (57–67% vs. 46–51%)

    Try this next: Continue using the 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject line format for newsletter emails rather than the product-update style naming convention.

    Shorter, personalized subject lines outperform longer, brand-prefixed ones.

    ~23 characters ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') vs. ~35 characters ('Medallion Product Update - [Month Year]')

    Try this next: Keep newsletter subject lines short and personally framed; avoid leading with the brand name or product label.

    The n=1 'Your July Roundup ✨' send on 2025-08-08 recorded a 0% open rate, likely a test or mis-send.

    1 recipient, 0% open rate

    Try this next: Exclude any sends with fewer than 10 recipients from performance reporting to avoid skewing aggregate metrics.

    Moderate Signal

    Click rates are highly variable within the Roundup format, suggesting CTA/content consistency is an issue.

    Roundup click rates range from 0.0% to 6.1% despite similar open rates
    Show reasoning

    The November Roundup achieved 6.1% clicks while June and July (2025-08-06) achieved 0.0–0.9% clicks, even though all had open rates above 57%. This suggests that the subject line reliably gets opens but the in-email content or CTA is inconsistent. Small audience sizes (6–147) add noise to these click rate estimates.

    The Product Update series shows stable but lower open rates as the list grows, but no clear volume-related degradation.

    Product Update open rates: 45.9–51.0% across 270–286 recipients
    Show reasoning

    The four Product Update emails were sent to nearly identical audience sizes (270–286) and achieved open rates within a 5-point range, suggesting list quality is stable at this scale. However, the sample of four emails is too small to confirm a trend, and there's no pre-growth baseline to compare against.

    No Signal

    There is no clear send-day or send-timing pattern distinguishing high from low performers.

    Emails sent on varying weekdays all achieve 45–67% open rates

    The data does not include send times, and send dates span multiple days of the week without a consistent pattern correlating to performance. The top and bottom performers are distributed across different months and days, so no timing conclusion is supported.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is small (12 sends, 11 meaningful). Audience sizes differ substantially between the two formats: Roundup emails were sent to 6–147 recipients while Product Update emails reached 270–286, meaning the formats were not tested on equivalent audiences. The 'Your July Roundup ✨' subject line appears twice — once as a real send (n=114) and once as what appears to be a test send (n=1, 0% open rate); the latter should be excluded. Click rate estimates for small sends (n=6 to ~100) carry wide confidence intervals and should be interpreted cautiously.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us
