    The top-performing onboarding emails share a problem-framing approach in their subject lines, hinting at a hidden pain point rather than leading with a product feature or explicit value proposition. Subjects that named a specific operational frustration ('silent slowdown,' 'smarter onboarding') drove 50-56% open rates, while benefit-forward or data-led subjects ('save up to 64%,' '2x the volume') failed to generate opens. The standout click-through anomaly — 18.2% on 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' — suggests that curiosity-gap framing not only gets emails opened but also drives action. For the next send, prioritize intrigue-first subject lines that name a pain without revealing the solution, and be cautious about leading with quantified claims.

    Strong Signal

    Intrigue-and-pain subject lines dramatically outperform benefit-or-stat-led subject lines on open rate.

    55.6% vs. 0.0% open rate between the top and bottom performers

    Try this next: Write subject lines that name a specific operational pain or imply a hidden problem, and save the quantified benefit for inside the email body.

    Moderate Signal

    Curiosity-gap framing ('no one talks about') produces the highest click-through rate by a wide margin.

    18.2% click rate on 'The credentialing bottleneck no one talks about' vs. 0.0–3.6% for all others
    Show reasoning

    This single subject line generated a 18.2% CTR on 11 sends, far above every other email. The 'no one talks about' phrasing explicitly signals exclusive or suppressed information, which likely primed engaged readers to click. However, this is one data point and the list size is small, so the result could reflect audience composition as much as subject wording.

    Very small recipient lists (under 10 sends) make open and click rates statistically unreliable for those emails.

    3 emails sent to 8 or fewer recipients, including one sent to just 1 person
    Show reasoning

    Open rates on lists of 1, 4, or 5 recipients are nearly meaningless — a single open or non-open swings the rate by 20-100 percentage points. The emails sent to 28 and 36 recipients provide the most reliable signal in this dataset, and both show strong open rates with the pain-framing approach.

    No Signal

    Question-format subject lines do not show a consistent open-rate advantage over statement formats.

    25.0% open rate on the one question-format subject ('What if credentialing files took 1 day instead of 1 week?')

    Only one subject was phrased as a direct question, and it landed in the lower half of open rates (25%). This is insufficient to conclude questions outperform or underperform statements — the data does not support a directional finding on format alone.

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~35 to ~65 characters across both top and bottom performers

    The highest-opening subject ('The silent slowdown affecting your providers') and the lowest ('How credentialing teams are handling 2x the volume') are similar in character length. No length bracket consistently clusters with higher or lower performance across these eight emails.

    All emails were sent on the same date (2025-12-09), so send timing cannot be evaluated as a variable.

    7 of 8 emails sent on 2025-12-09; 1 on 2025-12-10

    With virtually all sends on a single day, there is no timing variation to analyze. The one email sent on 2025-12-10 had a 25% open rate, identical to two 2025-12-09 emails, but this is a single data point and not a meaningful signal.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 8 emails with a total of 100 recipients across all sends, making statistical confidence low overall. Audience sizes are highly skewed — two emails account for 64% of total sends (36 and 28), while three emails were sent to 8 or fewer recipients (including one sent to a single person), making their rates essentially noise. All 8 emails were sent on the same day (2025-12-09) except one, eliminating any send-timing comparison. No subject lines appear to be repeated across segments, but the audience segments themselves are not described, so it is possible that different lists have different baseline engagement levels that are confounding the subject line comparisons.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 1,817 34.4% 6.8%
    Your insights are needed! 2,276 33.4% 5.9%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 1,638 32.2% 4.4%
    Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments 94 27.7% 2.1%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 7,747 21.4% 4.6%
    What this tells us

    Survey emails with personalization tokens or direct monetary incentives in the subject line consistently outperform more generic appeals, with the top two performers both featuring distinct hooks — a personalized name token and an explicit $20 offer respectively — while the broad-blast 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' email underperformed despite sharing the incentive angle, suggesting that audience targeting or list fatigue matters as much as subject line framing. Urgency-driven language ('Last call') maintained solid open rates without incentives, indicating deadline framing is a reliable fallback. To improve future sends, prioritize personalization tokens combined with incentive callouts, and avoid mass-blast sends to cold or oversized lists where the same hook loses its potency.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization tokens in the subject line are associated with the highest open rates.

    34.4% open rate for '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' vs. 21.4% for the non-personalized incentive email
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing email used a firstname personalization token alongside a monetary incentive and achieved the highest open rate. However, it also had a smaller send volume (1,817) than the lowest-performing incentive email (7,747), so audience size and list quality likely confound the result. Only one email used a personalization token, limiting confidence.

    Combining a monetary incentive with personalization outperforms a monetary incentive alone.

    34.4% open rate (personalized + $20) vs. 21.4% (non-personalized + $20)
    Show reasoning

    Two emails explicitly offered $20 but the personalized version outperformed by 13 percentage points. The non-personalized version was also sent to a much larger list (7,747 vs. 1,817), which may reflect a less targeted audience and contribute to lower engagement. The pattern is directional but cannot be cleanly isolated from list-size effects.

    Urgency or 'last call' framing sustains open rates comparable to incentive-led subject lines.

    32.2% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey'
    Show reasoning

    The 'Last call' email achieved the fourth-highest open rate without any monetary incentive, sitting only 2 percentage points below the top performer. This suggests deadline framing is an effective lever, but there is only one example of this pattern in the dataset.

    Highly specific, niche subject lines targeting a narrow topic underperform on click rate.

    2.1% click rate for 'Share your thoughts on roster generation for delegated payer enrollments'
    Show reasoning

    The most technically specific subject line had the lowest click rate (2.1%) by a wide margin, despite a modest open rate (27.7%). This may reflect a mismatch between the niche topic drawing curious opens but failing to convert, or it may simply reflect that this was a very small, specialized send (94 recipients). Small sample size limits confidence.

    Larger list sends are associated with lower open and click rates in this dataset.

    7,747-recipient send achieved the lowest open rate (21.4%); 1,817-recipient send achieved the highest (34.4%)
    Show reasoning

    There is a general inverse relationship between send volume and open rate across these six emails, which may reflect that larger sends target broader, less-engaged audiences. However, list targeting strategy and subject line quality are confounded with send size, so this cannot be treated as a causal finding.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear directional pattern in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 21.4% to 34.4% across subject lines of varying lengths

    The top-performing email has a short subject (36 characters) while mid-performers include both short and long subjects. The lowest performer ('Your chance to provide feedback + get $20') is mid-length. No consistent relationship between character count and open or click rate is visible across these six emails.

    Send timing (day or time) cannot be evaluated from the available data.

    Dates range from 2025-07-31 to 2026-06-29 with no time-of-day data provided

    Only send dates are available, not send times, and the small number of emails (6) spread across different months makes it impossible to isolate a day-of-week or time-of-day effect. No reliable timing pattern can be drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 6 emails, which is too small to draw statistically robust conclusions from any single pattern. Send volumes vary dramatically — from 37 to 7,747 — meaning open and click rates reflect very different audience compositions and targeting strategies, making direct comparisons unreliable. The 37-recipient send ('Share your experience with Medallion?') and 94-recipient send are too small to generalize from. No repeated subject lines or explicit A/B test pairs are present, so all variation across emails is confounded by audience, timing, and content differences simultaneously.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    A better analytics experience is coming your way 83 60.2% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this set share two traits: they announce something already live (rather than previewing something coming) and they target smaller, more focused audience segments. The highest open rate (75%) went to a short, direct subject confirming a feature was live, sent to only 36 recipients. Emails that previewed upcoming changes with forward-looking language and checkmark emoji underperformed relative to their 'now live' counterparts. The clearest lever to pull next time is framing releases as confirmed, present-tense announcements rather than advance notices.

    Strong Signal

    Emails with zero click rate are common even at high open rates, suggesting CTAs or linked content are weak or absent in most sends.

    3 out of 5 emails have 0.0% click rate despite open rates above 56%

    Try this next: Ensure every product release email includes a direct, specific CTA linked to the new feature (e.g., 'View the new report now'). Preview emails with no action for the reader to take should be evaluated for whether they are worth sending at all.

    Moderate Signal

    'Now live' or 'now available' language in subject lines correlates with higher open rates than preview/teaser framing.

    75.0% vs 61.9% open rate for 'now live' vs 'will go live soon' on the same topic
    Show reasoning

    Two pairs of emails cover the same topics (profile completion and analytics), and in both cases the 'now live'/'now available' version outperformed the preview version. The profile completion 'now live' email hit 75.0% vs 61.9% for the preview; the analytics 'now available' send hit 63.4% vs 60.2% for the preview. The pattern is directional and consistent, but the audience segments differ slightly in size, which limits certainty.

    Shorter, more specific subject lines appear to outperform longer, vaguer ones, but the signal is mixed.

    75.0% open rate for 42-character subject vs 60.2% for 50-character subject on comparable content
    Show reasoning

    The top performer ('New profile completion logic is now live', ~42 chars) is concise and technically specific. The lower-performing analytics preview ('A better analytics experience is coming your way', ~49 chars) is longer and more generic. However, the longest subject line in the set ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing', ~77 chars) still achieved 63.4%, so length alone is not a clean predictor — specificity may matter more than raw character count.

    Smaller audience segments tend to show higher open rates in this dataset.

    36-recipient send achieved 75.0% open rate vs 83-recipient send achieving 60.2%
    Show reasoning

    The two highest open rates (75.0% and 63.4%) came from the two smallest sends (36 and 41 recipients). The largest send (83 recipients) had the second-lowest open rate (60.2%). This may reflect more targeted segmentation yielding higher relevance, but it could also be noise given the very small absolute counts involved.

    No Signal

    Send timing shows no clear pattern in this dataset.

    Sends span July, August, and September with no clustering among top or bottom performers

    The top-performing email was sent on 2025-07-21 (Monday) and the lowest-performing on 2025-09-17 (Wednesday), but with only 5 emails spread across three months and no consistent day-of-week grouping, no timing conclusion is supported by this data.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails with audience sizes ranging from 36 to 83 recipients — all counts are extremely small, and percentage-based rates are highly sensitive to single opens or clicks. Two subject lines are identical ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing') sent to different segments (41 and 39 recipients) one week apart, meaning those two sends likely reflect audience segmentation rather than independent subject line tests, and their results should not be treated as independent data points. No conclusions here should be acted on without validation against a larger send volume.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 178 74.2% 5.1%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit 130 61.5% 13.1%
    Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit 52 57.7% 11.5%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 51 56.9% 3.9%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 140 51.4% 7.9%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset share two clear traits: direct personal urgency (countdown language, 'you're so close,' 'last chance') and tangible physical incentives like coffee kits or gift boxes, which drove both top open rates and top click rates. Personalized event-proximity emails consistently outperform evergreen product or platform demo invitations, suggesting that the event lifecycle cadence—especially the final-stretch reminders—is the single most reliable engagement lever. The lowest performers tend to be generic product demo invites or early-funnel event announcements to cold, large lists. Going forward, the team should front-load urgency and physical reward messaging earlier in the funnel rather than reserving it for last-chance sends.

    Strong Signal

    Subject lines with urgency phrases like 'Last Chance,' 'You're So Close,' or countdown framing achieve the highest open rates.

    Top 7 emails by open rate (57.7%–74.2%) all contain urgency or proximity framing.

    Try this next: Use urgency-first phrasing ('Last Chance,' 'You're So Close,' countdown to event) in at least the final two sends of every event campaign, not just the last one.

    Subject lines referencing a tangible physical incentive (coffee kit, gift box) produce the highest click rates in the dataset.

    'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' achieved a 13.1% click rate; 'Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit' hit 11.5%—both well above the dataset median.

    Try this next: Include a specific physical incentive (named item, not just 'gift') in the subject line for at least one mid-funnel and one last-chance send per event campaign.

    Large-list sends (n ≥ 900) consistently produce lower open rates (20–35%) than smaller, more targeted sends.

    All 6 sends with n ≥ 900 fall between 20.5% and 34.5% open rate, while targeted sends under 400 frequently exceed 40%.

    Try this next: Segment large event invite lists by engagement history or persona before sending, reserving the full broad list only for the first touch and sending higher-urgency emails to warmer sub-segments.

    Moderate Signal

    Emails hinting at a beverage or food theme ('bitter truth,' 'coffee, chocolate,' cocktails) perform well on opens but inconsistently on clicks.

    'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' averaged ~47% open rate across two sends (n=140, n=175); 'Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?' hit 48.8% (n=166).
    Show reasoning

    Food/drink metaphors in subject lines appear in several mid-to-upper performers, but click rates vary widely (0%–9.6%), suggesting they attract opens without always converting to action. The cocktail-themed demo-day emails ('This demo ends with a drink,' 'Crafting better care and cocktails') performed poorly on opens (17–18%), indicating the tactic works better when the incentive is real and explicit rather than metaphorical.

    Shorter, punchy subject lines (under ~55 characters) tend to outperform longer, bracket-heavy descriptive ones at the top of the open-rate range.

    The top 5 open-rate emails average ~45 characters; emails with '[You're invited]' or '[Final chance to join]:' prefix brackets cluster in the 37–46% open-rate range.
    Show reasoning

    The very top performers ('You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!', 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day') are concise and direct. Emails with long bracket prefixes like '[Final chance to join]: Navigating cost, innovation and credentialing and payer enrollment trends' perform moderately but not at the top. However, list composition and send size also vary, so subject length cannot be fully isolated.

    Failed personalization tokens appearing in subject lines ('{{ personalization_token... }}') did not catastrophically hurt open rates but were not top performers either.

    Both broken-token emails (n=1,043 and n=998) landed at 30.6%–31.2% open rate, below the dataset's ~40% median for their campaign type.
    Show reasoning

    The two countdown emails with unrendered HubSpot tokens performed in the lower-middle of the dataset for Elevate event emails, where comparable well-formatted urgency emails hit 34–41%. This suggests broken personalization tokens may slightly depress open rates, but the large send volumes (1,000+) to presumably colder or broader lists also contribute.

    Re-engagement or 'missed you' emails sent post-event perform poorly on clicks even when open rates are moderate.

    Three 'missed you' or post-event watch-on-demand emails averaged 0%–17.6% open rates with 0% click rates (excluding the n=2 outlier).
    Show reasoning

    'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' (n=17, 17.6% open, 0% click), 'We missed you!' (n=1, 0%), and 'What's next after Elevate 2025?' (n=11, 27.3% open, 0% click) all converted zero clicks. Post-event follow-up appears to generate low intent, though the sample sizes are too small to be definitive.

    No Signal

    There is no consistent pattern linking send day or send timing to email performance in this dataset.

    High performers span Monday through Friday across multiple months with no clustering.

    The data does not include day-of-week send times, only dates. High open rates appear across the full calendar spread (July through June) and the dataset lacks time-of-day data entirely. No seasonal pattern is detectable given the nature of the sends (event-driven campaigns that follow their own calendar).

    Emails sent to very small lists (n ≤ 12) show extremely high or extreme open rates that are not reliable signals.

    Five emails with n ≤ 12 show open rates of 40%–100%, including a 100% click rate at n=5.

    Small-list outliers like 'You're So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (n=5, 100% click rate) and 'We missed you at Elevate!' (n=12, 66.7%) are statistically unreliable. These likely represent follow-up segments to highly engaged subsets, not replicable broadcast results.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' sent twice, 'Executive briefing on credentialing risk & AI adoption' sent twice, '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' sent twice, and the '\ You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' sent three times)—open rates differ substantially across sends of the same subject, confirming that list composition and audience targeting are major confounds. Many top-performing emails were sent to lists of fewer than 200, while the largest sends (900–1,386) all cluster at the bottom; this audience-size skew makes it impossible to fully isolate subject line effects from list quality effects. Two emails contain unrendered HubSpot personalization tokens, indicating a CRM configuration issue. The single n=1 email ('We missed you!') provides no usable signal and should be excluded from future analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) 189 60.3% 5.3%
    [Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue 169 56.2% 9.5%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 154 49.4% 9.1%
    ☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue? 209 47.4% 6.7%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 162 44.4% 8.0%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset consistently use urgency, specificity, and direct financial stakes to drive opens — phrases like 'time to revenue,' 'state-by-state payer delays,' and bracketed event labels perform well above average. Notably, the top large-audience emails (100+ sends) cluster around a 43–60% open rate when they combine a concrete pain point with a format signal (e.g., '[Report]', '[You're invited]', '☕'). Click rates tell a different story than open rates: some of the best-opening emails have near-zero click rates, suggesting strong subject lines are bringing people in but the content or CTA isn't converting. The clearest opportunity is to pair the urgency and specificity that drives opens with a stronger in-email CTA to close the gap between curiosity and action.

    Strong Signal

    Subject lines that reference a specific financial or operational pain point drive higher open rates among large-audience sends.

    49.4% open rate for 'State-by-state payer delays and what they're costing you' vs. 27.6% for 'Hiring more providers isn't fixing access' (both 100+ sends)

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with a named, specific pain point and its consequence (delay, cost, revenue impact) rather than a general theme or solution statement.

    Emails with near-zero click rates despite high open rates suggest a subject-line/content mismatch that needs fixing.

    Three emails achieved 36–100% open rates but 0.0% click rates (e.g., '3 ways successful health organizations offset the operational strain', 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet').

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA of high-open/zero-click emails — ensure there is a single, prominent, friction-free link tied directly to the promise made in the subject line.

    Smaller audience segments (under 50 sends) systematically show higher open rates, but this likely reflects list quality rather than a scalable tactic.

    Emails sent to 2–49 recipients average ~43% open rate; emails sent to 100+ recipients average ~36% open rate.

    Try this next: Identify what made the 60.3%-open send (n=189) different in targeting or list quality and replicate that segmentation approach for future large sends.

    Moderate Signal

    Bracketed format labels like '[Report]', '[You're invited]', and '[Final chance]' are associated with above-average open rates in large sends.

    Three of the top five large-audience emails (100+ sends) use a bracketed label; their open rates range from 43.7% to 60.3%.
    Show reasoning

    The pattern is directional but limited — only a handful of emails use brackets, so it's hard to isolate the label effect from the underlying content appeal. '[Report]' appeared three times with varying results (18.4%–43.7%), suggesting audience segment matters too.

    The 'coffee' theme (café puns, coffee break framing) appears in two of the top-five large-audience emails by open rate.

    '☕ Coffee Break? Is provider readiness slowing your time to revenue?' opened at 47.4% (n=209); '[Final chance for coffee]' opened at 56.2% (n=169).
    Show reasoning

    Both coffee-themed emails outperformed the large-audience average, but the sample is only two emails and both also contain strong pain-point language, making it difficult to isolate the theme effect. The pattern is suggestive but not conclusive.

    The 'AI in Credentialing' email achieved the highest click rate (13.6%) despite a below-average open rate (22.7%).

    13.6% click rate on 22 sends — the highest click rate in the entire dataset.
    Show reasoning

    This is a single small send (n=22), so the absolute numbers are not reliable at scale. However, it suggests AI-related content may drive unusually strong engagement from those who do open. Worth testing on a larger segment.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates appear on both short subjects ('The hidden crisis behind hospital closures', 39%) and long ones ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)', 60.3%).

    High and low performers span the full range of subject line lengths. No consistent length advantage is visible. The variation in audience size and segmentation across sends makes it impossible to isolate length as a driver.

    Send timing (day or month) does not show a reliable pattern in this dataset.

    Top open rates occur across July 2025, October–November 2025, January 2026, and April–May 2026 with no clustering.

    High-performing emails are distributed across many months and no day-of-week data is provided, so no timing-based conclusion is supportable from this data alone.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across 2–3 separate rows with different send counts, indicating the same email was deployed to multiple audience segments on the same date — open and click rates therefore reflect segment-level performance, not a single send. The two smallest sends (n=2 and n=3) show 100% and 66.7% open rates that are statistically meaningless and should be excluded from pattern analysis. Audience sizes range from 2 to 840, creating significant skew: small-segment sends dominate the top of the open-rate ranking but are not comparable to large-list sends. Any patterns drawn from this data should be validated against a controlled test with consistent audience sizes.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
     $43M raised to help healthcare organizations scale faster 170 45.9% 2.4%
    A refreshed analytics experience is coming next week 59 45.8% 0.0%
    New feature: Track closed practice locations in platform 67 43.3% 3.0%
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 67 17.9% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset skew heavily toward small, targeted sends (often under 5 recipients), which inflates open rates and limits generalizability. Among emails with meaningful list sizes (20+ recipients), urgency-framed and action-oriented subjects consistently outperform passive feature announcements, with rates clustering in the 42–54% range. The weakest performer is the $43M funding announcement sent to 67 recipients at 17.9%, suggesting that externally-focused company news resonates poorly with this audience compared to product-change and compliance-deadline messaging. To improve, prioritize operationally urgent subject lines and reserve funding/PR-style announcements for tightly curated segments.

    Strong Signal

    Company funding/PR announcements dramatically underperform product-utility emails with this audience.

    The three $43M funding announcement variants averaged 44.0% open rate overall, but the two largest sends (n=67 and n=170) landed at 17.9% and 45.9% respectively — and the n=67 send is the single lowest performer in the dataset.

    Try this next: Avoid broad blasts for company news announcements; restrict funding/PR emails to warm, engaged segments or personalize them with a product-relevance hook.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under 5 recipients) show 100% open and click rates, which are statistical artifacts rather than true performance signals.

    3 of the top 4 open-rate entries were sent to 1–3 recipients, all showing 100% open rates.

    Try this next: Exclude sends with fewer than 15 recipients from open-rate benchmarking to avoid distorting performance analysis.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and action-required framing drives higher open rates among large sends.

    53.8% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' vs. 17.9% for a broadcast funding announcement (n=67)
    Show reasoning

    Emails with explicit urgency cues like 'Action required,' 'Coming soon,' or compliance deadlines consistently appear in the upper half of the open rate ranking among sends with 10+ recipients. However, sample sizes per individual framing are small, so this is directional rather than conclusive.

    'Coming soon' preview emails perform comparably to 'Now live' launch emails, with no clear advantage for either framing.

    'Coming soon' emails average ~51% open rate; 'Now live' emails average ~47% — a 4pp gap across a small number of emails.
    Show reasoning

    'Coming soon: Standard payer names...' (63.3%) and 'Coming soon: Smarter payer selection...' (47.9%) bracket the 'Now live' counterparts, suggesting preview framing may build slight anticipation, but the sample is too small to be confident. One 'Now live' email reached 46.4%, nearly matching its preview counterpart.

    Subject lines with specific feature or compliance names outperform vague benefit-only subjects.

    'Introducing a new passthrough fee: CAQH Roster Fee' (54.0%, n=37) and 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' (53.8%, n=13) both name specific entities, outperforming generic benefit subjects.
    Show reasoning

    Subjects that name a specific payer, regulation, or feature (CAQH, IMLC, Michigan) appear to perform better than those with only generic benefits, likely because B2B users scan for operational relevance. However, the urgency of the IMLC email may be confounding with specificity.

    Click rates are near-zero for most announcement emails; the few exceptions involve API/technical or enrollment workflow features.

    'Generate API keys directly in Medallion' had 8.3% click rate (n=12); 'New feature: Track closed practice locations' had 3.0% (n=67) — most others are 0.0%.
    Show reasoning

    Out of 29 emails, 22 had a 0% click rate, suggesting announcement emails in this category are primarily read for awareness rather than to drive action. The highest click rates appeared on emails targeting technical users (API keys) or introducing interactive features, implying those audiences are more likely to engage with CTAs.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top and bottom performers span a wide range of lengths, from short ('Unlocking faster Payer Enrollment follow-up') to long ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here\'s what to do').

    When sorted by open rate, there is no visible pattern correlating subject line character count with performance. Both short and long subjects appear throughout the distribution. This dimension does not yield an actionable signal from this data.

    Send timing (day/date) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are scattered across January, February, October, November, and December with no clustering by day of week or month.

    The data does not include send day-of-week, and send dates are spread across many months without any clustering of high or low performers around specific time periods. No timing-based conclusion can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: This dataset has significant audience-size skew: 3 emails were sent to 1–3 recipients and show 100% open/click rates that are statistically meaningless. The remaining 26 emails range from 7 to 170 recipients, with most under 70 — these are small segments, limiting statistical confidence in any single finding. Additionally, several subject lines appear in multiple sends (e.g., 'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' sent on both 2025-11-20 and 2025-12-01; '$43M raised...' variants sent to different segments on the same date), suggesting A/B testing or segmented rollouts — these should not be treated as independent data points when averaging performance by subject line.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Dinner @ Stillwell's ? 55 40.0% 5.5%
    You’re Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy 55 30.9% 3.6%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 135 30.4% 0.7%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 138 27.5% 2.2%
    Dinner @ Stillwell's Tonight? 77 26.0% 3.9%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails share two traits: they market an exclusive, named experience (a specific restaurant or event) and they are sent to small, highly targeted lists (typically under 55 recipients). Casual, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' and formal personalized invitations both outperform broad urgency-driven blasts. The data suggests shrinking list size and naming the specific venue or experience in the subject line consistently lifts open rates above 30%, while generic healthcare-executive framing and urgency-only language underperforms. Future sends should prioritize tight segmentation and lead with the specific destination or experience rather than the category of event.

    Strong Signal

    Naming the specific venue or experience in the subject line is associated with higher open rates.

    7 of the top 8 emails by open rate name a specific venue (Flour + Water, Aquavit, Stillwell's, TABLE) or branded experience (Golden Knights, Sphere).

    Try this next: Always name the specific restaurant, venue, or branded experience in the subject line rather than describing the audience category or event type generically.

    Urgency-only subject lines ('Last Chance', 'Final Call', 'Don't Miss') do not reliably drive high open rates.

    Urgency-framed emails average approximately 18% open rate vs. 35%+ for experience-named emails.

    Try this next: Drop standalone urgency openers ('Last Chance', 'Don't Miss', 'Final Call') as the primary hook. If used, pair them with the specific venue or experience name, not a generic audience descriptor.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, question-format subject lines ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') perform surprisingly well even at larger list sizes.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent to 55 recipients achieved a 40.0% open rate — the third-highest in the dataset.
    Show reasoning

    The two 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sends (55 and 13 recipients) achieved 40.0% and 7.7% open rates respectively, and 'You're Invited to Dinner @ Juniper and Ivy' (55 recipients) hit 30.9%. The conversational style stands out from the formal 'A Seat at the Table' and 'Reserve Your Seat' framing. However, the dramatic gap between the two Stillwell's sends (40% vs 7.7%) suggests list quality or timing differences are also at play, limiting confidence.

    Smaller send lists (under 30 recipients) are associated with the highest open rates, but list size and targeting quality are deeply confounded.

    The 6 emails sent to 25 or fewer recipients average a 36% open rate; the 10 emails sent to 90+ recipients average a 19% open rate.
    Show reasoning

    Very small sends (9–25 recipients) dominate the top of the open rate rankings, likely reflecting tighter targeting and higher recipient relevance. However, it is impossible to separate list size from list quality in this data — a small send to a perfectly matched audience will always outperform a large send to a loosely matched one. The Aquavit sends (135 and 138 recipients) are a partial exception at 30.4% and 27.5%, suggesting a well-targeted large list can still perform well.

    Conference-tied events (JPM) generate strong open rates even with formal, longer subject lines.

    'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved 55.0% (n=20) and 28.0% (n=25) open rates across two sends.
    Show reasoning

    Both JPM dinner invitations outperformed the dataset average, suggesting that tying a micro-event to a major industry conference the recipient is already attending increases perceived relevance sharply. However, only two data points exist for this pattern, and list size is small in both cases.

    Follow-up 'tomorrow' or 'tonight' sends to the same event show diminishing returns compared to the original invite.

    The original Golden Knights invite (56.2%) outperformed the 'Tomorrow' follow-up (37.5%) and 'Last Call' resend (33.3%) to the same event.
    Show reasoning

    For the Golden Knights experience, each successive follow-up email to overlapping lists saw a lower open rate: 56.2% → 37.5% → 33.3%. A similar pattern appears for the Boston TABLE dinner (33.3% original vs. 10.0% for the 'Don't miss' follow-up). This is expected behavior but the data consistently confirms it. Small sample sizes per event limit confidence.

    No Signal

    Shorter, punchier subject lines do not clearly outperform longer, descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' — 7 words) and long ('Don't Miss Medallion's Power Play: A Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience' — 13 words) subjects.

    The top two emails by open rate are a long, branded subject (56.2%) and a medium-length formal invitation (55.0%). Conversational short subjects also appear in the top tier. Bottom performers include both short ('Don't Miss: An Exclusive Evening with Top Healthcare Executives') and long formats. No clear length-based pattern is supported by this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of year) cannot be assessed from this dataset.

    Dates range from July 2025 to May 2026 with no clustering of high performers in a specific month or season.

    The dataset does not include day-of-week or time-of-day send data, and the calendar dates of high and low performers are spread across the full date range. No seasonal or timing-based pattern is visible or supportable from the information provided.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple sends to different or overlapping segments (e.g., 'A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York' sent twice on 2025-09-04 and 2025-09-10 with nearly identical list sizes of 138 and 135; 'In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Power Play' sent to 9 and 5 recipients; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent to 55 and 13 recipients; the JPM dinner invitation sent to 25 and 20 recipients), making it difficult to isolate subject line copy effects from list composition effects. Many top-performing emails were sent to very small lists (under 25 recipients), which inflates open rate percentages — a single additional open on a 16-person list moves the rate by over 6 percentage points. Caution is warranted when comparing rates across sends of vastly different sizes (e.g., 6 recipients vs. 138 recipients). No click-through data is available for several emails (0.0% click rate), which may reflect tracking issues rather than true zero engagement.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Medallion Product Update - February 2026 83 43.4% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - May 2026 95 43.2% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - March 2026 86 39.5% 0.0%
    Medallion Product Update - April 2026 89 36.0% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Newsletter open rates are consistently strong across this dataset, ranging from 36–52% for all campaigns sent to meaningful audience sizes. The monthly 'Roundup' emails with emoji subject lines and the more formal 'Medallion Product Update' series both perform well on opens, but virtually all campaigns fail to generate clicks — the lone exceptions being a few Roundup emails. The data suggests the audience is engaged enough to open but rarely motivated to click through, indicating a content or CTA gap rather than a subject line or deliverability problem. Priority for future sends should be improving in-email click prompts, particularly for the Product Update series which now reaches a larger audience.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are near zero across almost the entire dataset, regardless of format or audience size.

    8 of 11 emails recorded 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Add at least one specific, low-friction CTA link to every Product Update email (e.g., 'See what's new in the product →') and test whether a highlighted feature deep-link improves click rate.

    Clicks only appear in the Roundup series, never in the Product Update series.

    Roundup click rate range: 0–6.5%; Product Update click rate: 0.0% across all 4 sends

    Try this next: Audit the content structure of a top-performing Roundup email (e.g., July) and replicate its link/CTA approach in the next Product Update send.

    The single email sent to only 1 recipient ('Your June Roundup ✨') is a statistical outlier and should be excluded from pattern analysis.

    Sent to 1 recipient; 0.0% open rate

    Try this next: Verify list segmentation logic before sending to ensure Roundup campaigns reach their intended full audience.

    Moderate Signal

    Roundup emails with emoji (✨) in the subject line slightly outperform the plain 'Product Update' format on open rates.

    Average open rate: Roundup series ~43.0% vs. Product Update series ~40.5%
    Show reasoning

    The top 3 open rates all belong to Roundup emails, and 5 of the top 6 are Roundups. However, the Roundup series is sent to smaller audiences (26–44 recipients) while Product Updates go to 83–95, making direct comparison tricky — larger lists often see modest open rate dilution. The gap is real but modest and could reflect list composition differences rather than subject line style alone.

    Shorter, informal subject lines ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') do not clearly outperform longer, formal ones ('Medallion Product Update - [Month] YYYY') in a statistically meaningful way.

    3.5 percentage-point average open rate gap between the two formats
    Show reasoning

    Roundup subjects average ~43% open and Product Update subjects average ~40.5% open — a visible but small gap across 11 emails. Given the differing audience sizes and no A/B test structure, it's not possible to attribute the difference to subject line length or formality with confidence. Subject length ranges from ~22 characters (Roundup) to ~34 characters (Product Update), but neither format shows a dramatic performance edge.

    No Signal

    There is no detectable send-day or send-time pattern driving open rate differences in this dataset.

    N/A — no send time data available

    The dataset includes only send dates, not send times. Send days span Tuesday through Monday across the series, but with only 11 emails and no time-of-day data, no timing pattern can be confirmed or denied.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 11 emails across two loosely comparable series (Roundup: 7 emails, 1–44 recipients; Product Update: 4 emails, 83–95 recipients), making cross-format comparisons low-confidence due to audience size skew — Product Update lists are roughly 2–3x larger, which can naturally suppress open rates. No A/B tests exist. One send ('Your June Roundup ✨', n=1) appears to be a test or error and should be excluded from aggregate analysis. No send-time data is available, limiting timing analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 486 33.1% 6.2%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 457 29.8% 4.2%
    Your insights are needed! 573 26.0% 6.5%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 1,179 19.9% 4.4%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails combine personalization with a concrete monetary incentive stated directly in the subject line, driving above-average open rates in the 30-33% range. The highest-opening email used a merge tag for the recipient's first name alongside the $20 offer, outperforming a nearly identical incentive-focused subject sent to a much larger list by over 13 percentage points — suggesting personalization and list targeting both matter. Urgency framing ('Last call') also performed well, indicating that time-pressure language is a viable alternative lever when used late in a campaign sequence. Future survey sends should lead with personalization and clear incentive value in the subject line, and reserve urgency framing for follow-up sends.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized subjects with a named incentive outperform generic incentive subjects on open rate.

    33.1% open rate vs. 19.9% for a similar incentive subject without personalization
    Show reasoning

    The top email ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!') achieved 33.1% opens while 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' — also incentive-driven — got only 19.9%. However, the latter was sent to a much larger list (1,179 vs. 486), which may depress open rates independently of wording, so list composition is a confounding factor.

    Monetary incentives in the subject line are associated with higher open and click rates compared to non-incentive survey subjects.

    Two incentive subjects averaged 26.5% open rate vs. 27.9% for non-incentive subjects — no clear open-rate gap, but click rates for incentive emails (6.2% and 4.4%) match or exceed non-incentive ones.
    Show reasoning

    Incentive subjects don't dominate on opens alone, but they maintain strong click rates, suggesting the $20 offer converts curious openers into survey takers. The sample of only 5 emails limits confidence in any directional claim.

    Urgency framing ('Last call') sustains strong open rates late in a campaign sequence.

    29.8% open rate on the 'Last call' email sent two weeks after the campaign started
    Show reasoning

    The 'Last call' subject line was the third send in an apparent sequence and still achieved 29.8% opens on a list of 457, suggesting the audience had not yet fatigued. However, this is a single data point and list overlap across sends is unknown.

    Non-incentive, curiosity-driven subjects ('Your insights are needed!') still achieve competitive click rates.

    6.5% click rate — the highest of all five emails — despite a below-average 26.0% open rate
    Show reasoning

    'Your insights are needed!' had the highest click-to-open conversion efficiency among non-incentive emails, suggesting the audience who opened it was highly motivated to act. However, this is one data point and the subject line is quite generic, so replicability is uncertain.

    No Signal

    Shorter, punchier subject lines (under 45 characters) do not consistently outperform longer ones in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~28 to ~63 characters with no clear rank-order correlation to open rate

    The top subject is mid-length (~37 chars with merge tag resolved), the second is short (~38 chars), and the lowest performer is also mid-length (~46 chars). There is no discernible pattern linking length to performance across these five emails.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    5 emails across 5 distinct dates with no time-of-day data provided

    Dates span September–October 2025 and one June 2026 send, but no day-of-week or time-of-day information is available. With only 5 sends and no time metadata, no timing pattern can be responsibly identified.

    The June 2026 send to only 10 recipients is a statistical outlier and should not drive conclusions.

    10 recipients sent, 30.0% open rate (3 opens)

    A 30% open rate on 10 recipients represents just 3 opens. Any small fluctuation would swing the rate dramatically, making this data point unreliable for comparative analysis. It may represent a test send or a highly segmented micro-list.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails, which is too small to establish statistically robust patterns — all findings should be treated as directional hypotheses. Audience sizes vary dramatically (10 to 1,179), and smaller lists tend to produce inflated open rates; the 10-recipient send in particular is essentially noise. It is unknown whether these emails were sent to overlapping segments, which would mean prior exposure affects open rates on later sends in the sequence. The unresolved merge tag ('{{ contact.firstname }}') in the top subject line indicates a potential deliverability or rendering issue that may have affected actual performance in unpredictable ways.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this set lead with concrete, specific announcements — naming the exact feature or report available — and use present-tense language signaling something is already live. In contrast, the lowest-performing email used future-tense ('will go live soon') and an emoji, which coincided with the worst open rate despite a comparable audience size. Clicks remain near zero across almost all sends, suggesting recipients open but don't act further, which points to a content or CTA gap worth investigating. Prioritize specific, present-tense subject lines and add a clearer in-email call to action.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are effectively zero across nearly all emails, indicating recipients open but do not engage with in-email CTAs.

    4 out of 5 emails have 0.0% click rate; only one reached 9.1%

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA of the one email that generated clicks ('New profile completion logic is now live') and use its structure as a template for future product release emails.

    Moderate Signal

    Specific, present-tense subject lines announcing something 'now live' or 'now available' outperform vague or future-tense ones.

    60.0% open rate for 'now available' vs. 26.3% for 'will go live soon'
    Show reasoning

    The two highest-opening emails use concrete feature names and present-tense phrasing ('are now available,' 'is now live'), while the lowest opener used future-tense ('will go live soon'). The pattern is directional, but sample size is only 5 emails and audience sizes differ, so this cannot be stated conclusively.

    Adding an emoji to a product release subject line did not help and may have hurt open rates.

    26.3% open rate for the only subject line with an emoji (✅), the lowest in the set
    Show reasoning

    The one email with an emoji ('A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅') had the lowest open rate at 26.3%, and it also used future-tense phrasing — so the emoji and the future-tense are confounded. With only one data point, emoji use alone cannot be isolated as the cause.

    Announcing something already live drives more clicks than previewing something upcoming.

    9.1% click rate for 'is now live' email vs. 0.0% for the 'will go live soon' email on the same topic
    Show reasoning

    The two profile-completion emails cover the same feature: one previewed it as upcoming (0% clicks, 26.3% opens) and one announced it as live (9.1% clicks, 45.5% opens). This is a single paired comparison, so confidence is moderate, but the direction is consistent with the broader open-rate pattern.

    No Signal

    Shorter, more direct subject lines do not clearly outperform longer, more descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~36 to ~70 characters with no consistent open-rate ordering by length

    The top opener ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing') is one of the longest subjects (~70 chars), while mid-range openers include shorter lines. No length-based pattern emerges from these 5 emails.

    There is no detectable send-day or send-timing pattern in this dataset.

    Sends span July–September 2025 with no repeated day-of-week metadata available

    Only calendar dates are provided, not days of the week or send times, and there are only 5 sends spread across three months. No timing-based conclusions can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains only 5 emails with audience sizes ranging from 10 to 20 recipients — far too small to draw statistically reliable conclusions. One subject line ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing') was sent twice to separate segments of ~10 recipients each on different dates (2025-09-10 and 2025-09-17), yielding different open rates (60% vs. 40%); these may reflect segment differences rather than any content signal. All findings should be treated as directional hypotheses to test at scale, not confirmed best practices.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 335 72.5% 3.9%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  246 63.0% 7.7%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 236 55.1% 4.2%
    Watch expertise behind every outcome: Medallion Demo Day 97 54.6% 4.1%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 238 52.5% 2.9%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing webinar emails in this dataset consistently use urgency, personalization, and tangible incentives (gift boxes, coffee kits, cocktails tied to specific events) to drive strong open rates, while large-list sends above ~1,500 recipients cluster in the 38–43% open rate range regardless of subject line quality. The highest-engagement emails tend to be segmented sends to smaller, warmer audiences rather than broad blasts, suggesting that list segmentation matters as much as copywriting. For future campaigns, prioritize smaller segmented sends with concrete incentives or urgency triggers, and reserve broad blasts for top-of-funnel awareness where lower rates are acceptable. Broken personalization tokens appearing in live subject lines represent an avoidable credibility risk that likely suppresses performance.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency-driven subject lines with 'Last Chance' or countdown language consistently outperform generic invitations.

    63.0% open rate for 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' vs. ~30% for early-stage 'You're invited' variants

    Try this next: Always pair event-registration emails with an explicit deadline and send a dedicated 'last chance' email within 48 hours of close — this appears to be the single highest-leverage moment in the sequence.

    Broken personalization tokens in subject lines appear in two of the highest-volume sends and represent a deliverability and credibility risk.

    Two sends of 1,652 and 1,570 recipients show raw token strings like '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' in the subject line

    Try this next: Implement a pre-send QA checklist that renders all personalization tokens in a test send before any deployment over 500 recipients.

    Subject lines referencing specific, named events (Elevate, Demo Day) outperform generic webinar or topic-only subject lines.

    Elevate and Demo Day branded subjects average ~45% open rate across significant sends vs. ~35% for topic-only subjects

    Try this next: Brand every webinar or virtual event with a distinct, repeatable name rather than relying on topic descriptions alone — the named-event equity compounds across a multi-email sequence.

    Audience size is inversely associated with open rate — smaller, segmented sends dramatically outperform broad blasts.

    Sends under 100 recipients average ~54% open rate; sends over 1,000 recipients average ~40% open rate

    Try this next: Segment large webinar lists into smaller cohorts by persona, account stage, or geography before sending — even rough segmentation appears to yield 10–15 percentage point open rate gains.

    Moderate Signal

    Tangible physical incentives (gift box, coffee kit, cocktails) are associated with elevated click rates but not always open rates.

    33.3% click rate for 'Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (n=9) vs. campaign median of ~3%
    Show reasoning

    Emails referencing gift boxes, coffee kits, or cocktail sessions show click rates of 5–33%, well above the median. However, most of these are small-list sends (n=9 to 355), so the sample sizes limit confidence. The 355-person 'Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?' send at 49.3% open / 5.6% click is the most statistically meaningful data point and still outperforms peers.

    Post-event 'missed you' and on-demand replay emails achieve surprisingly high open rates but near-zero click rates.

    'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' achieves 51.3% open rate but 0.0% click rate (n=37)
    Show reasoning

    Multiple post-event emails ('We missed you at Elevate!', 'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand', 'Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere') open at 45–51% but convert nearly zero clicks. This suggests recipients are curious enough to open but not compelled by the on-demand CTA. The pattern appears across several sends with different copy, indicating a structural issue with the CTA rather than subject line failure.

    Executive framing ('Executive perspectives,' 'Executive briefing') is associated with above-average click rates on mid-sized lists.

    '[You're invited] Executive perspectives on cost, risk, and innovation' achieved 9.0% click rate (n=288)
    Show reasoning

    Three emails using 'executive' positioning show click rates of 3.1–9.0%, above the dataset median. However, the sample is small and these emails also coincide with credentialing/AI content themes, making it difficult to attribute the lift solely to 'executive' framing vs. topic relevance.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear correlation with open rate in this dataset.

    Top-performing subjects range from 13 characters ('We missed you!') to 80+ characters with no consistent length advantage

    Short subjects like 'We missed you!' hit 100% (n=1) while long subjects like '⏰ Last Chance! Time is running out, Elevate is on Wednesday!' hit 43.8% on 1,542 recipients. Mid-length subjects span the full performance range. Audience size and urgency framing appear to be stronger confounders than length, making it impossible to isolate a length effect from this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be assessed from this dataset.

    No send-time data is available; only send dates are provided

    The dataset includes send dates but no time-of-day or day-of-week metadata. Without this granularity, no timing pattern can be identified or validated. Date ranges span July 2025 through June 2026 without obvious seasonal clustering among top performers.

    Data quality notes: Three emails were sent to only 1 recipient each and show 0% or 100% rates — these are almost certainly test sends or CRM artifacts and should be excluded from any quantitative benchmarking. Several subject lines appear across multiple sends to different segments on the same or nearby dates (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' sent to 236 and 401 recipients; 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' sent three times to 379, 53, and 34 recipients), meaning aggregate performance is split across rows rather than consolidated — this inflates the apparent number of campaigns and makes cross-campaign comparisons imprecise. Audience size varies from 1 to 2,016, creating significant skew; percentage-based metrics on small lists (under ~30 recipients) should be treated as directional only. No click-to-open rate is calculable from the provided fields, limiting conversion analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 218 49.5% 3.2%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 342 44.1% 5.9%
     Why hiring isn’t improving specialty access 117 43.6% 2.6%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 356 41.6% 4.2%
     Scaling 700+ clinics without slowing down 120 40.8% 2.5%
    What this tells us

    The highest open rates in this dataset consistently belong to emails with small send lists (under 50 recipients), making audience-size skew the dominant confound. Setting that aside and focusing on statistically meaningful sends (100+ recipients), the top performers share problem-framing subject lines that name a specific operational or financial pain — enrollment delays, access breakdowns, payer bottlenecks — rather than generic value propositions. The report-anchored email ('State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing') stands out as the only email combining strong open rates with meaningful click rates (3–6%) across multiple large segments, suggesting credentialed data assets drive deeper engagement. Future campaigns should lead with concrete problem statements and pair them with a downloadable asset to convert openers into clickers.

    Strong Signal

    Problem-framing subject lines that name a specific operational pain outperform vague or outcome-only subjects among large-list sends.

    44.1% open rate for 'State-by-state payer delays and what they're costing you' (n=342) vs. 23.3% for 'Ready to see what operational resilience looks like in action?' (n=245)

    Try this next: Lead every subject line with the specific problem or cost the reader is experiencing, not the solution or invitation to learn more.

    Emails tied to a named research report are the only content type consistently generating both high open rates AND meaningful click-through.

    The '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' send to 218 recipients achieved 49.5% open rate and 3.2% CTR; the 342-recipient payer delays email hit 44.1% opens but only a 5.9% CTR with a different CTA structure.

    Try this next: Attach every content email to a downloadable report, data summary, or worksheet so high open rates convert into measurable clicks.

    Small-list sends (under 50 recipients) dominate the top of the open-rate ranking but are statistically unreliable.

    6 of the top 7 open rates (50–75%) come from sends with 2–42 recipients; the highest large-list open rate is 49.5% (n=218).

    Try this next: Segment reporting to evaluate small-list sends (under 50) separately from broadcast sends when drawing creative conclusions.

    Repeated sends of the same subject line to different segments produce notably different open rates, confirming audience composition matters more than subject line alone.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' open rates: 49.5% (n=218), 29.3% (n=82), 19.9% (n=136) — a 30-point spread on the same subject.

    Try this next: Track open rates by segment cohort, not just campaign, and prioritize list hygiene and segmentation quality before optimizing subject line wording.

    Moderate Signal

    Bracket-formatted subject lines ([Report], [You're invited], [Final chance]) do not uniformly boost open rates — the report format works but event invites are inconclusive.

    49.5% open rate for '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment...' (n=218) vs. 58.3% for '[You're invited]...' (n=36)
    Show reasoning

    The [Report] bracket performs strongly across all three of its send segments. The [You're invited] and [Final chance] brackets show high open rates but were sent to very small lists (35–36 recipients), making them unreliable signals. The bracket pattern cannot be confidently credited independent of the content it labels.

    The coffee-themed subject line ('☕ Coffee Break?' and 'A latte teams are dealing with...') shows lower performance than problem-framing peers at similar list sizes.

    'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' (n=115) achieved 24.3% open rate vs. 40.8% and 39.5% for problem-framing subjects sent the same week to similar list sizes.
    Show reasoning

    Both coffee-pun emails rank in the bottom half of the dataset despite being sent in the same campaign window as higher-performing problem-framing emails. The wordplay may reduce perceived urgency or professionalism for a B2B healthcare audience. Sample sizes are sufficient to note the gap but not large enough to conclude definitively.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top and bottom performers span from short ('Why care access is breaking down') to long ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)')

    High and low open rates appear at both ends of the length spectrum. Without controlling for audience size, no length-based pattern emerges. The data does not support a length recommendation.

    Send timing (day of week or month) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing sends are spread across October, November, January, April, and May with no clustering on specific days.

    The dataset does not include send-time data, and the dates span multiple months without a visible weekday or seasonal concentration among top performers. No timing conclusion can be drawn from this data alone.

    Data quality notes: Six of the top seven open rates come from sends with 2–42 recipients, creating severe audience-size skew that makes those rates statistically meaningless for creative optimization. Three different subject line campaigns were each split across multiple segments with the same subject line but different audiences, producing open-rate spreads of up to 30 percentage points on identical copy. The dataset contains 31 sends but only ~15 distinct subject lines, with several repeated 2–3 times. Click rate data is sparse — 13 of 31 sends show 0.0% CTR — which limits conclusions about what drives downstream engagement. Any insight drawn from sends under 100 recipients should be treated as directional only.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 55 56.4% 1.8%
    Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM 76 47.4% 6.6%
    Dinner @ Stillwell's ? 88 44.3% 3.4%
    Tomorrow: Join us for Medallion’s Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience 56 35.7% 3.6%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 280 35.7% 2.1%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing micro event emails share two traits: they name a specific, premium venue or experience in the subject line, and they are sent to small, targeted lists rather than broad audiences. Casual, conversational framing (e.g., 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') performs comparably to polished invitation language when the list is tight and relevant. Urgency-driven 'last call' and 'don't miss' subject lines consistently underperform relative to the initial invitation send for the same event, suggesting that re-engagement follow-ups erode rather than recover interest. The clearest lever is list quality and specificity — the highest open rates all come from sends under 90 recipients.

    Strong Signal

    Naming a specific, notable venue or experience in the subject line is associated with higher open rates.

    Top 5 open rates (44–56%) all reference a named venue or branded experience; emails using generic 'exclusive dinner' language average ~22% open rate.

    Try this next: Always include the specific venue or experience name in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive' or city-only descriptors.

    Urgency and 'last call' subject lines underperform the original invitation send for the same event.

    For the Golden Knights sequence, the initial invite hit 30–35.7% open rate while 'Last Call' dropped to 28.1%; Boston TABLE initial was 17–23.9% while 'Final Call' fell to 18.3%.

    Try this next: Reduce the number of urgency-driven follow-ups; if a re-send is necessary, change the angle entirely rather than escalating urgency language.

    Smaller, more targeted send lists are associated with significantly higher open rates.

    Emails sent to fewer than 90 recipients average ~39% open rate; emails sent to 100+ recipients average ~22% open rate.

    Try this next: Prioritize list curation and keep micro event sends to fewer than 90 highly qualified recipients rather than broadening the send to fill the room.

    Moderate Signal

    Casual, conversational subject lines perform at least as well as formal invitation language when the list is small.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (14 recipients) hit 50% open rate; 'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water' (55 recipients) hit 56.4%.
    Show reasoning

    The two highest-open-rate subject line styles are nearly tied yet stylistically opposite — one is a polished formal invitation and the other is a bare-minimum casual question. Both use a specific venue name, suggesting venue specificity matters more than formality. However, sample sizes are small (n=14 and n=55) and only two data points represent the casual style, limiting confidence.

    Conference or event co-location context (e.g., JPM conference) may boost open rates, but the signal is limited to one example.

    'Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved the highest open rate in the dataset at 56.4% (n=55) and 47.4% (n=76).
    Show reasoning

    This subject line explicitly ties the dinner to a known industry conference (JPM), which may increase relevance for recipients already planning to attend. Both sends of this subject line (different list sizes, different dates) rank in the top 4, suggesting the co-location angle adds value. However, no other conference-anchored subject lines exist in the dataset for comparison.

    Repeating the same subject line to a different or larger list yields a lower open rate than the original send.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' went from 50% (n=14) to 44.3% (n=88); 'Aquavit' invite went from 35.7% (n=280) to 32.5% (n=283).
    Show reasoning

    In three cases where nearly identical subject lines were sent to different or overlapping lists, the second send consistently produced a lower open rate. This could reflect list saturation, the second send targeting a less engaged segment, or diminishing novelty of the subject line. The effect is directional but each pair has only two data points.

    No Signal

    Subject lines in the 40–65 character range do not show a clear open rate advantage over shorter or longer lines in this dataset.

    Top open rate: 56.4% ('Invitation: Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' = 55 chars); second: 50% ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' = 22 chars).

    Subject line length varies widely among top and bottom performers, with no consistent band of length associated with higher opens. Short lines ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' at 22 chars) and longer lines ('A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York' at 82 chars) both appear across the performance spectrum. Length does not appear to be a meaningful signal in this dataset.

    Send timing (day of week or time of year) does not show a clear pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are spread across January, February, April, September, and October with no clustering.

    Top-performing emails span multiple months and there is no day-of-week data available. The dataset is too small and the dates too distributed to isolate a timing effect separate from list size and subject line quality. Seasonal or day-of-week conclusions cannot be drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: Several subject lines appear across multiple sends with different list sizes and dates (e.g., 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' sent twice, 'Aquavit' invite sent twice, 'Flour + Water' sent twice, 'Golden Knights' invite split across two sends on the same day), making it impossible to fully separate subject line performance from list composition effects. Many sends have very small recipient counts (n=1 to n=27), meaning a single open or non-open can swing rates by 4–7 percentage points; insights from these small sends should be treated as directional only. The dataset contains no send-time metadata, no industry segmentation, and no information on whether lists overlap between sends, all of which limit causal interpretation.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
     $43M raised to help healthcare organizations scale faster 246 44.7% 6.1%
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 147 20.4% 0.0%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset share a pattern of operational specificity — they name a concrete feature, workflow change, or compliance action rather than speaking in abstractions. Emails with the smallest send sizes dominate the top open rates, but even at mid-sized sends (31–49 recipients), operationally specific subjects like 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' and 'Introducing a new passthrough fee: CAQH Roster Fee' held open rates above 60%. The one notable exception is the $43M funding announcement, which performed well at scale (44.7% on 246 sends) but poorly on a different segment (20.4% on 147 sends), suggesting subject line framing matters even for inherently newsworthy content. Next time, lead with what changed and what the recipient needs to do, not with the company milestone.

    Strong Signal

    Subjects that name a specific feature, workflow change, or compliance action outperform vague or company-centric subjects.

    Top 10 emails by open rate all exceed 61%; all name a specific product area or action (e.g., 'Generate API keys,' 'CAQH Roster Fee,' 'Michigan IMLC').

    Try this next: Lead every announcement subject with the specific feature name, payer, or regulatory body affected — avoid framing subjects around company achievements unless paired with a direct recipient benefit.

    Very small send lists (1–7 recipients) inflate open rates and are not reliable signal for subject line effectiveness.

    The top 4 open rates (71–100%) all come from sends of 7 or fewer recipients.

    Try this next: Exclude sends under 15 recipients when benchmarking subject line performance; maintain a separate benchmark for high-touch micro-sends.

    Moderate Signal

    'Now live' and 'Action required' framing correlates with higher open rates than 'Coming soon' preview emails.

    'Now live' and 'Action required' subjects average ~67% open rate vs. ~48% for 'Coming soon' subjects across comparable send sizes.
    Show reasoning

    Three 'Coming soon' subjects (47.9%, 50%, 46.4%) consistently underperform their 'Now live' or live-announcement counterparts on similar topics. However, the sample of each framing type is small (3–4 emails each), and send-list sizes vary, so this is directional rather than conclusive.

    Emails with click rates above 0% tend to involve fee changes, required field deadlines, or new tracking features — content that demands a decision or action.

    6 of 28 emails had click rates above 0%; 5 of those 6 involved fees, required fields, or new feature adoption.
    Show reasoning

    The CAQH Roster Fee (2.3% CTR), Track closed practice locations (8.6%), Upcoming required fields (7.1%), $43M announcement (6.1%), New Payer Enrollment TATs (3.7%), and Coming soon: Smarter payer selection (3.6%) all drove clicks. Purely informational 'now live' announcements consistently showed 0% click rates despite high open rates, suggesting recipients read but don't click unless prompted to act.

    Resending the same subject line to a new or overlapping segment produces meaningfully lower open rates.

    'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' dropped from 80% (5 sent) to 54.2% (24 sent) the next day; 'Task Summary and Licensing' version dropped from 50% (12 sent) to 48.6% (37 sent).
    Show reasoning

    Two subject lines were sent to different audience sizes on consecutive days with declining open rates on the larger send. This may reflect list saturation, audience overlap, or diminishing novelty — but the pattern appears in multiple pairs.

    No Signal

    Shorter, more direct subject lines do not show a consistent open-rate advantage over longer descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~5 words ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request') to ~14 words with no clear correlation to open rate.

    The top and bottom performers span a wide range of subject line lengths. 'Generate API keys directly in Medallion' (7 words, 100%) and 'New fields now live for provider, group, facility, and practice location profiles' (13 words, 61%) both perform well. Length alone does not predict performance in this data.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a clear pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are distributed across October 2025 through April 2026 with no clustering on specific days.

    The data includes send dates but not send times or days of week in a granular enough form to identify a timing pattern. Top performers span Tuesday through Thursday ranges visually, but without day-of-week labels, no conclusion is supportable.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is small (28 emails), limiting statistical confidence for all findings. Audience sizes vary dramatically — from 1 to 246 recipients — meaning open rates for small sends (under 15) are not comparable to mid-sized sends and should not be pooled in averages. At least four subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments on consecutive dates, making it impossible to isolate subject line performance from list composition effects. No send time or day-of-week data is available, preventing any timing analysis beyond calendar date.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Medallion Product Update - February 2026 76 61.8% 2.6%
    Medallion Product Update - April 2026 73 58.9% 0.0%
    Your August Roundup ✨ 53 58.5% 1.9%
    Medallion Product Update - May 2026 76 56.6% 0.0%
    Your October Roundup ✨ 52 53.8% 0.0%
    What this tells us
