    Newsletter open rates across these 11 sends are consistently strong (42–72% for statistically meaningful list sizes), suggesting a highly engaged subscriber base. The earlier 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' series and the newer 'Medallion Product Update' series both perform well, but the roundup format with emojis shows a slight edge in open rates among comparable audience sizes. Click rates are uniformly low across all emails, pointing to a systemic engagement drop-off after the open that isn't tied to subject line format. Prioritizing click-driving content over open optimization should be the next lever to pull.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are near-zero across every email in this series, regardless of subject line or format.

    8 of 11 emails have a 0.0% click rate; the highest recorded is 3.5%

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA placement of the highest-open emails (November Roundup, July Roundup) to understand why readers open but do not click, then A/B test a single prominent CTA button vs. the current link format.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under ~30 recipients) produce unreliable open-rate data.

    The 1-recipient 'Your June Roundup ✨' shows 100% open rate, skewing comparisons

    Try this next: Exclude sends with fewer than 30 recipients from performance benchmarking to avoid misleading optimization decisions.

    Moderate Signal

    Branded roundup subject lines with an emoji outperform plain product update lines in open rates.

    65.7% average open rate for 'Roundup ✨' emails (excluding 1-send outlier) vs. 57.3% for 'Medallion Product Update' emails
    Show reasoning

    The 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format consistently pulls higher open rates than the 'Medallion Product Update - [Month Year]' format across comparable send sizes. However, the roundup series also ran earlier when the list was smaller and potentially more self-selected, so list maturity may partly explain the gap rather than subject line wording alone.

    As the subscriber list grows over time, open rates trend gradually downward.

    Open rates declined from ~65% range (35–53 recipients) to ~55% range (73–76 recipients) as list size grew
    Show reasoning

    The 'Medallion Product Update' series runs at 73–76 recipients and averages ~57% opens, while earlier smaller-list roundups averaged higher. This could reflect natural list dilution as less-engaged contacts are added, rather than content degradation. The trend is directional but not conclusive given the confounding format change.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from ~20 to ~36 characters with no monotonic open-rate trend

    Both short ('Your July Roundup ✨') and longer ('Medallion Product Update - February 2026') subjects appear across the top and bottom of the open-rate rankings. There is no consistent pattern linking character count to performance.

    Send timing (month or day of send) does not reveal a reliable performance pattern.

    Open rates of 52–73% span sends from August 2025 through June 2026 with no seasonal trend

    Emails sent in winter months are not consistently stronger or weaker than those sent in summer months, and the data lacks send-time (hour/day-of-week) information needed to draw timing conclusions. No actionable timing signal exists in this dataset.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 11 sends with audience sizes ranging from 1 to 76 recipients — extremely small for statistical reliability. The single-recipient June send (100% open) is a clear outlier and should be excluded from benchmarking. Two distinct subject line series exist ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨' and 'Medallion Product Update'), making direct subject line comparisons confounded by the concurrent shift in list size and time period. No send-time or day-of-week data is available, limiting timing analysis. Click rate conclusions are robust given the consistency of the 0% result, but open rate differences of fewer than 10 percentage points should be treated as noise at these audience sizes.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 619 38.8% 4.5%
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 720 37.5% 5.7%
    Your insights are needed! 737 35.5% 5.4%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 748 20.9% 5.2%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails share urgency framing and clear value exchange — either deadline pressure ('Last call') or explicit monetary incentive ('$20') — and both achieve open rates above 37%. The bottom performer led with the incentive in the subject line but phrased it as an opportunity ('Your chance to...') rather than a direct offer, suggesting assertive framing outperforms softer hedging. For future sends, prioritize deadline-driven or direct-incentive subject lines over benefit-teasing language, and consider that urgency signals may be especially effective for this audience.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and direct incentive framing in subject lines correlate with higher open rates.

    38.8% open rate for 'Last call' vs. 20.9% for 'Your chance to...'
    Show reasoning

    The top opener used explicit deadline urgency ('Last call') and the second used a direct monetary offer ('$20 for your insights!'). The lowest performer also mentioned $20 but framed it as 'Your chance to...', suggesting the framing matters more than the incentive itself. However, with only 5 emails this is directional, not conclusive.

    Subjects containing '$20' as a direct offer outperform the same incentive framed as an opportunity.

    37.5% open rate ('$20 for your insights!') vs. 20.9% ('get $20' after a softer lead-in)
    Show reasoning

    Both emails referenced the $20 incentive, but the one leading with the dollar amount achieved 16.6 percentage points higher open rate. The weaker performer buried the incentive after a softer hook. This is a directional signal worth testing explicitly, though the emails were sent to different-sized audiences at different times.

    The $20 incentive appears across multiple top emails, suggesting monetary rewards are effective for this audience.

    Two of the top 3 open rates belong to emails explicitly mentioning '$20', averaging 29.2% open rate; the one non-incentive email in the top 3 hit 35.5%.
    Show reasoning

    Monetary incentives appear in 2 of the 5 emails and both perform above the dataset median. However, the highest performer uses no monetary incentive at all, relying purely on urgency, which complicates a definitive conclusion about incentives alone.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate.

    Top 3 subject lines range from 28 to 52 characters with no monotonic relationship to open rate.

    The highest open rate belongs to a 51-character subject, the second-highest to a 28-character subject, and the third to a 26-character subject. There is no consistent directional trend between subject length and performance in this dataset.

    Send timing does not reveal a clear pattern in this dataset.

    All high-performing sends occurred in a 2-week window (Sep 17 – Oct 7, 2025) with no consistent day-of-week signal.

    Four of five emails were sent within a tight 20-day window, making it impossible to isolate timing as a variable. The outlier date (June 2026) had only 13 recipients, making it statistically unusable for timing analysis.

    Personalization tokens in subject lines do not clearly boost open rates in this sample.

    The email with '{{ contact.firstname }}' achieved 37.5% opens — second highest, but nearly matched by non-personalized subjects.

    The personalized subject performed well, but the top performer had no personalization and outperformed it by 1.3 percentage points. With only one personalized email in the set, no reliable conclusion can be drawn about personalization's effect.

    Audience size skew makes the June 2026 send (n=13) an outlier that cannot be compared to other sends.

    13 recipients vs. 619–748 for all other sends.

    The 'Share your experience with Medallion?' email had only 13 recipients, yielding a 30.8% open rate and 7.7% click rate that are statistically unreliable. It cannot meaningfully inform subject line or content conclusions.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails, which is too small a sample for high-confidence conclusions. One send ('Share your experience with Medallion?', June 2026) had only 13 recipients and is a statistical outlier that skews click-rate comparisons; it has been treated as unreliable throughout. The remaining four sends ranged from 619 to 748 recipients, which is reasonably consistent for audience-size comparisons. No subject lines appear to be exact repeats across sends, so duplicate-send contamination is not a concern, but the emails were sent to potentially overlapping or sequential segments of the same list across a 20-day window, which may introduce fatigue or novelty effects that cannot be disentangled from subject line performance.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails by open rate used specific, feature-named subject lines announcing that something is 'now available' or 'now live,' suggesting that concrete, delivery-confirmed language outperforms anticipatory or benefit-framing language. Notably, the highest open rates came with zero clicks, meaning recipients opened but did not act further — the one email that generated clicks ('New profile completion logic is now live') had the smallest audience but the only non-zero click rate at 16.7%. The pattern suggests subject line specificity drives opens, but the body content or CTA is not converting readers into clickers across most sends. Prioritizing both specific subject language and a clearer in-email action should be the focus for the next campaign.

    Strong Signal

    Across all five emails, click rates are effectively zero for any audience larger than 6 recipients.

    0.0% click rate across 4 of 5 emails (combined 63 recipients)

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA of the next product release email: add a single, prominent, specific action (e.g., a direct link to the new feature) and test whether click rates improve independently of subject line changes.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines announcing something is definitively 'now available' or 'now live' outperform anticipatory or vague benefit language.

    68.4% open rate for 'now available' vs. 52.0% for 'coming your way'
    Show reasoning

    The two highest open rates (68.4% and 63.6%) both used the same subject line announcing reports are 'now available.' The 'coming your way' teaser email and the 'will go live soon' email both landed at 52.0%. The 'now live' email also hit 50.0%. The pattern is directional, but audience sizes are very small (6–25 recipients), limiting statistical confidence.

    Shorter, noun-phrase subject lines naming a specific feature perform at least as well as longer or emoji-decorated lines.

    50.0% open rate for the emoji subject line vs. 68.4% for the plain specific subject
    Show reasoning

    The emoji subject line ('A new approach to profile completion will go live soon ✅') achieved only 50.0% opens, while plain feature-named lines hit up to 68.4%. However, the emoji email also had a smaller send list (8) and a different message stage (pre-launch teaser), so wording and timing are confounded. Confidence is moderate rather than strong due to these overlapping variables.

    The only email to generate clicks was sent to the smallest audience and featured a completed-state announcement ('now live').

    16.7% click rate on a 6-recipient send — all other emails had 0.0%
    Show reasoning

    'New profile completion logic is now live' was sent to only 6 people and is the sole email with any clicks. It's impossible to determine whether the click rate reflects subject line wording, audience segment, list size, or body content. The pattern is notable but cannot be generalized without more data.

    No Signal

    Send timing (day or date) shows no discernible pattern in this dataset.

    5 sends across 5 different dates

    Each email was sent on a different date with no repeated days of the week documented, and the audience sizes differ substantially. There is no basis in this data to draw any conclusion about optimal send day or time.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is extremely small: 5 emails sent to between 6 and 25 recipients each (63 total). No single insight meets a statistical significance threshold. The top subject line ('New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing') was sent twice to different segments on 2025-09-10 (n=19) and 2025-09-17 (n=11), meaning two of the five data points are not independent sends — they share identical copy and likely represent a segmented rollout rather than a true A/B test. Audience-size skew is also a concern: the one email with clicks had the smallest list (6), which inflates its percentage metrics considerably.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 105 74.3% 5.7%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  113 60.2% 8.8%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 91 58.2% 4.4%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 104 56.7% 2.9%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays 76 56.6% 2.6%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing webinar emails in this dataset share two clear traits: they are sent to small, targeted segments (often under 10 recipients) and they use urgency or exclusivity signals like 'last chance,' 'you're so close,' or personalized enrollment-state references. The large-volume sends (400–900 recipients) consistently land in the 23–37% open-rate range, suggesting list fatigue or weaker targeting at scale. To improve performance on broader sends, the team should borrow the urgency and curiosity-gap language that drives the smaller sends, and consider tighter segmentation to replicate the relevance that lifts open rates on targeted messages. The broken personalization token appearing in two high-volume sends is a fixable execution error that likely suppressed performance.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and 'last chance' subject lines consistently outperform invitation-style subject lines.

    Emails with 'last chance,' 'so close,' or deadline language average ~55% open rate vs. ~32% for general invitation subjects.

    Try this next: Add a dedicated urgency/deadline email as the final touchpoint in every webinar sequence, explicitly naming the closing date or seat limit in the subject line.

    Curiosity-gap or 'bitter truth' style subject lines outperform straightforward descriptive ones for webinar content.

    'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' averaged 48.7% open rate across two sends (n=76 and n=108) vs. ~33% for descriptive alternatives.

    Try this next: Frame webinar subject lines around a surprising or counterintuitive insight rather than the event title or speaker name.

    A broken personalization token was deployed in two large sends, likely suppressing open rates.

    Two sends totaling 1,267 recipients used the literal string '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' as the subject line opener.

    Try this next: Add a pre-send QA checklist item that renders subject line tokens against a test contact before any send over 100 recipients.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized or state-specific subject lines drive strong open rates even at moderate send volumes.

    'You're in one of the slowest enrollment timeline states' (n=9) and 'fastest' variant (n=61) opened at 55.6% and 37.7% respectively.
    Show reasoning

    The enrollment-state segmentation tactic shows a meaningful lift over generic webinar subjects at comparable send sizes. However, the slowest-state version was sent to only 9 people, making the absolute rate unreliable. The fastest-state version at 61 sends still outperforms most large sends, suggesting real signal.

    Emails sent to very small lists (under 10 recipients) dominate the top open rates but are statistically unreliable.

    6 of the top 12 open rates come from sends with 9 or fewer recipients.
    Show reasoning

    A 100% open rate on 1–2 recipients, or 55.6% on 9 recipients, carries almost no statistical weight. These likely represent re-sends to specific contacts, sales follow-ups, or segmentation tests. They skew the apparent top-of-list performance and should not be used as benchmarks for campaign decisions.

    Short, intriguing subject lines (5–7 words) tend to outperform longer descriptive ones at large send volumes.

    'How AI is transforming hospital operations' (8 words, n=95) hit 46.3%; longer bracket-heavy subjects like '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation in payer enrollment and medical credentialing' (n=261) hit 36.4%.
    Show reasoning

    At large send volumes, shorter subjects like 'How AI is transforming hospital operations,' 'AI: Empathetic or Efficient?' and 'Rethink revenue cycle performance at Elevate 2025' tend to cluster above 30%, while long descriptive subjects cluster below. The dataset has too many confounding variables (audience, timing, event type) to isolate length cleanly, so this is directional.

    On-demand 'watch now' follow-up emails perform well relative to their send size, suggesting a warm re-engagement use case.

    'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand' (n=9, 55.6%), 'Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere' (n=8, 62.5%), and 'Watch Medallion Demo Day on demand' (n=1, 100%) all exceed the dataset median.
    Show reasoning

    Post-event on-demand emails consistently appear in the upper half of the open-rate distribution. Sample sizes are small, but the pattern is consistent across different events (Elevate, Demo Day) and time periods. These are inherently sent to warm audiences who registered but didn't attend, which contributes to the lift.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clean, consistent performance pattern across the full dataset.

    Open rates range from 18% to 60%+ across both short and long subjects at comparable send volumes.

    When controlling for send size, both short and long subjects appear in both high and low open-rate buckets. The data does not support a standalone length rule without also accounting for urgency framing and audience targeting, which are stronger confounds.

    There is no detectable send-day or send-timing pattern in this dataset.

    High-performing sends are scattered across multiple months and days of the week with no visible clustering.

    The data does not include day-of-week or time-of-day metadata, and dates alone span a broad range without a pattern linking specific calendar timing to open rate performance. No timing insight can be responsibly drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: 57 emails vary enormously in audience size (1 to 888 recipients), which makes direct open-rate comparisons misleading: a 100% open rate on 1 recipient and a 36% open rate on 800 recipients are not comparable signals. At least 6 of the top 12 open rates come from sends of 9 or fewer recipients and should be treated as anecdotes rather than benchmarks. Several subject lines were reused across multiple sends to different segments (e.g., 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' appears twice, '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' appears twice), making it unclear whether performance differences reflect the subject line or the audience. The broken personalization token appears verbatim in two sends, confirming a systemic QA gap rather than an intentional variant test.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    $43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse 88 12.5% 1.1%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing announcement emails in this dataset share two traits: small audience size (1–10 recipients) and subject lines that signal specific, actionable urgency — either naming a concrete feature, a compliance deadline, or a direct request. The lowest open rates cluster around large-list sends (88, 31, 25 recipients), suggesting the audience segmentation itself may be driving performance more than messaging. To improve future sends, prioritize tight segmentation by relevance and lead with concrete product names or required actions rather than company milestones like funding announcements.

    Strong Signal

    Very small recipient lists (1–8) dominate the top open-rate slots, inflating apparent performance.

    All 5 emails with 100% open rates were sent to 1–3 recipients

    Try this next: Establish a minimum send-volume threshold (e.g., 20+ recipients) before drawing open-rate conclusions, and report segmented benchmarks separately for micro-sends vs. full-list sends.

    Click rates are effectively zero across all announcement emails except the $43M funding sends.

    Only 2 of 29 emails recorded any clicks; both were '$43M raised' variants with 1.1%–3.2% CTR

    Try this next: Add a single, specific CTA link to product announcement emails (e.g., 'See it in the platform') and track whether click engagement improves, starting with mid-list-size sends where open rates are measurable.

    Moderate Signal

    Emails with action-oriented or urgency-signaling subject lines outperform milestone announcements.

    100% open rate for 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' vs. 12.5%–48.4% for '$43M raised' variants
    Show reasoning

    Subjects framing a required action ('Action required', 'Coming soon', 'Now live', 'Submit') consistently appear in the top half of opens, while the funding announcement ('$43M raised') appears in the bottom third across all three sends. However, audience size confounds this — the $43M sends went to 25–88 recipients while urgency emails went to 1–10, so the pattern is directional but not cleanly isolated.

    Subject lines naming a specific product feature or compliance event outperform generic benefit framing.

    Subjects naming specific features or regulations average ~75%+ open rates (small lists); benefit-framed subjects like '$43M raised to help healthcare organizations scale faster' hit 48.4% on a 31-person list
    Show reasoning

    Subjects that name a concrete deliverable — 'Generate API keys directly in Medallion', 'New fields now live for provider, group, facility', 'Standard payer names required' — cluster in the top half of the open-rate ranking. Vague benefit framing scores lower even on comparably sized lists. The caveat is that list size differences make a clean comparison impossible.

    Duplicate subject lines sent to different segments show degraded performance on the re-send.

    'New Analytic reports are now available for Task Summary and Licensing': 57.1% open (7 sent) vs. 0.0% (2 sent, one week later)
    Show reasoning

    Several subject lines appear more than once across different sends and dates. In two observable cases ('Task Summary and Licensing' analytics, '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse'), the second or third send to a smaller or overlapping segment produced lower or zero open rates. This suggests audience fatigue or overlap, though the very small re-send sizes make this inconclusive.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent pattern correlated with open rate in this dataset.

    Top and bottom performers span a wide range of subject lengths with no directional trend

    The highest open-rate subjects range from short ('Thank You + Quick Feedback Request') to long ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do'), and the lowest open-rate subjects are similarly mixed in length. List size variance prevents any length-based conclusion.

    Send timing (day or date) shows no identifiable pattern correlated with open rate in this dataset.

    High open rates occur across dates spanning Aug 2025 to Mar 2026 with no clustering by weekday or month

    The dataset does not include send-time data, and the date range spans many months with no visible seasonal or day-of-week clustering among top or bottom performers. Without send-time granularity and larger volumes, no timing conclusion is supportable.

    Data quality notes: This dataset has significant sample size limitations: 17 of 29 emails were sent to 10 or fewer recipients, meaning open rates are dominated by whether 1–2 individuals opened a given email. Five emails with 100% open rates went to just 1–3 people, making those rates statistically meaningless for benchmarking. Several subject lines appear in multiple rows representing separate sends to different segments on the same or nearby dates (e.g., '$43M raised' appears in three variants on 2025-08-18 and 2025-08-20; 'New Analytic reports for Task Summary and Licensing' appears twice; 'Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon' appears twice). These duplicate sends may share overlapping audiences, creating fatigue effects. Audience composition across sends is unknown, preventing any normalized comparison. No send-time data is available, limiting timing analysis.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 109 50.5% 2.8%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 97 46.4% 3.1%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 95 45.3% 4.2%
    Why specialty care access is breaking down 56 28.6% 1.8%
     Why hiring isn’t improving specialty access 55 27.3% 7.3%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing emails in this dataset combine urgency or exclusivity signals (final chance, report brackets, coffee-break hooks) with concrete, pain-focused subject lines tied to financial or operational impact. The highest open rates cluster around state-of-industry reports and timely event-driven messaging, while the highest click rates appear on emails with clear content payoffs like curated reading lists or financial framing. Low-volume sends (under 10 recipients) frequently distort open rates, making small-list results unreliable benchmarks. The clearest opportunity is to pair the open-driving formats (bracketed report labels, urgency tags) with content that actually earns clicks, since several high-open emails recorded 0% click rates.

    Strong Signal

    Pain-point subject lines referencing financial or operational consequences outperform abstract or vague subject lines on click rate.

    'State-by-state payer delays and what they're costing you' earned a 4.2% click rate; 'AI in Credentialing: what to read next' earned 11.1%.

    Try this next: Lead every subject line with a named consequence or a numbered content promise rather than a vague readiness or resilience label.

    Emails with 0% click rates are concentrated among sends that use worksheets, readiness checks, or demo-teaser formats.

    7 of the 9 emails with 0% click rate feature 'worksheet,' 'operational readiness,' 'ready to see,' or similar passive-engagement framing.

    Try this next: Audit the in-email CTA for all worksheet and readiness-check sends; replace passive CTAs ('see what it looks like') with specific outcome-driven CTAs ('download the 3-step checklist').

    The highest click rates appear on emails with explicit content curation or financial-impact framing, not event or report announcements.

    'AI in Credentialing: what to read next' (11.1% CTR), 'Offsetting operational and financial strain starts behind the scenes' (9.5% CTR), and 'When hospitals close, the work doesn't stop' (7.3% CTR) are the top three click-rate performers.

    Try this next: Build more emails around curated 'what to read next' formats and explicit financial-impact narratives, and reduce reliance on report announcements as standalone sends.

    Duplicate sends to different segments show meaningful open-rate variance, suggesting audience quality matters more than subject line alone.

    'The hidden crisis behind hospital closures' ranged from 24.5% to 27.1% open rate; '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' ranged from 19.8% to 50.5% across three segment sends.

    Try this next: Prioritize tightly segmented sends over broad list blasts; use the higher-engagement segments to validate subject lines before scaling.

    Moderate Signal

    Bracketed prefixes like '[Report]' and '[Final chance]' are associated with above-average open rates.

    All three '[Report]' sends averaged 39.0% open rate; the '[Final chance]' send hit 75.0%.
    Show reasoning

    The '[Report]' subject line appeared three times across different audience sizes and consistently outperformed the dataset median (~31%). The '[Final chance]' result is striking but came from only 4 recipients, making it unreliable on its own. Together they suggest bracketed prefixes signal clear value or urgency, but the small-list caveat limits confidence.

    Coffee-themed and conversational subject lines drive high opens but zero clicks in this dataset.

    Three coffee-related or conversational sends ('☕ Coffee Break?', 'A latte teams are dealing with', '[Final chance for coffee]') averaged 48.3% open rate but 0.6% average click rate.
    Show reasoning

    The playful tone appears effective at getting opens, but none of these emails converted to meaningful click activity. This may indicate audience curiosity without sufficient content payoff, or a mismatch between the casual tone and the CTA inside. The sample is only three emails, limiting confidence.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent relationship with open or click rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates span from very short ('AI in Credentialing: what to read next') to long ('[Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue').

    Both long and short subject lines appear throughout the top and bottom of the open-rate ranking, with no discernible linear trend. Length does not appear to be a meaningful lever based on the data available here.

    Send timing (day of week or month) cannot be reliably assessed from this dataset.

    Sends span May 2025 through June 2026 with no repeated send-day metadata available.

    The data includes dates but not days of week, and many high performers are one-off or low-volume sends tied to specific campaign moments rather than a regular cadence. No timing pattern can be extracted with confidence.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains 29 sends but several are duplicate subject lines mailed to different segments on the same date, meaning there are closer to 20 unique campaigns. Audience sizes range from 1 to 116 recipients, and several top open-rate results (75%, 60%, 50%) come from sends of 4–8 people — at these volumes, a single open or non-open swings the rate by 12–25 percentage points, making those results statistically unreliable. Open and click rates should be interpreted primarily from sends of 40+ recipients. Additionally, several subject lines contain encoding artifacts (\xa0) suggesting formatting issues that may have affected deliverability or display for those emails.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 84 28.6% 3.6%
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 88 28.4% 9.1%
    Join Us Next Week in Boston for an Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner 83 22.9% 2.4%
    Final Call: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner at Boston’s Acclaimed TABLE 70 22.9% 7.1%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 82 20.7% 1.2%
    What this tells us

    The highest-performing emails in this dataset share two clear traits: they reference a specific, named experience (a restaurant, a venue, or a branded event like the Sphere or Golden Knights) and they were sent to very small lists (2–27 recipients). Casual, conversational subject lines like 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' consistently outperformed formal 'Invitation:' or urgency-driven 'Last Chance' framings. The lowest-performing emails were concentrated in a single early campaign (Nashville/Tennessee, July 2025) with vague subject lines that withheld the venue and leaned on generic exclusivity language. Going forward, the playbook should lead with the specific venue or experience name, keep phrasing casual and short, and reserve urgency language for follow-up sends rather than initial invitations.

    Strong Signal

    Casual, venue-specific subject lines dramatically outperform formal or urgency-first ones.

    54.5% open rate for 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' vs. 3.3% for 'Don't Miss: An Exclusive Evening with Nashville Healthcare Executives'

    Try this next: Always name the specific restaurant or experience in the subject line rather than relying on 'exclusive' or 'executive' descriptors alone.

    Moderate Signal

    Short, conversational subject lines (under ~50 characters) outperform long, formal ones.

    Top 5 emails average ~47 characters vs. bottom 5 average ~60 characters in subject line length
    Show reasoning

    The highest-performing subjects tend to be short and colloquial ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' is just 22 characters), while lower-performing subjects are longer and more structured ('Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston'). However, some mid-performing emails also have long subjects, and list size differences confound this comparison, so confidence is moderate rather than strong.

    Urgency-framing ('Last Chance,' 'Final Call,' 'Don't Miss') does not consistently lift performance.

    Urgency-framed subjects range from 40.0% ('Last Chance to RSVP... Lazy Betty') down to 10.6% ('Last Chance: Exclusive Evening with Tennessee Healthcare Executives')
    Show reasoning

    Urgency language appears in both high and low performers. When combined with a specific venue name, it can perform well (40% open rate for the Lazy Betty 'Last Chance'). When used without a venue name, it performs poorly. The pattern suggests urgency alone is not the driver — the venue specificity is the active ingredient.

    Small send lists (under ~30 recipients) are associated with the highest open rates in this dataset.

    8 of the top 10 open rates come from sends of 27 or fewer recipients
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing sends were to 2, 4, 8, 19, 22, and 27 recipients, while the lowest performers were sent to 30–88 recipients. However, small lists naturally produce more volatile rates, and it's unclear whether the list size itself drives opens or whether small sends are simply more targeted to warm prospects. This limits confidence.

    Branded experience events (Sphere, Golden Knights) generate strong opens but not necessarily strong clicks.

    Vegas Sphere emails averaged 56% open rate but Golden Knights emails averaged only 26% open rate across all sends
    Show reasoning

    The two Sphere-experience emails achieved 50–62.5% open rates, the highest in the dataset. Golden Knights emails were more mixed (20–50%), likely because the top-performing one was sent to only 2 people. Unique, novel experiences appear to drive curiosity-based opens, but click rates for these events were not consistently higher than dinner invites, suggesting the open appeal does not always translate to RSVP intent.

    No Signal

    There is no detectable send-day or send-timing pattern in this data.

    Dates range from July 2025 to May 2026 with no clustering of high performers on specific days

    The dataset does not include send time of day, and the send dates of top performers are spread across Sunday through Wednesday with no clear pattern. There are too few sends per day-of-week bucket to draw any conclusion about timing effects.

    Follow-up or reminder sends do not show a consistent lift or drop versus initial sends for the same event.

    Boston 'TABLE' dinner initial send: 43.8% open; follow-up send: 29.4% open — but Stillwell's reminder sent to 22 people achieved 54.5%

    For some events (Boston TABLE, New York Aquavit), repeated sends show declining open rates, which is expected. But the Stillwell's reminder to a larger list actually outperformed the initial send to 4 people, making it impossible to draw a reliable conclusion about follow-up cadence from this data alone.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very small overall (29 emails, most sent to fewer than 90 recipients), which means individual open and click rates are highly sensitive to single-person behavior — a one-person difference on an 8-person send shifts the open rate by 12.5 percentage points. Several subject lines were reused across multiple segmented sends (e.g., 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' appears on April 23 and April 29; the Aquavit and Golden Knights subjects each appear twice), making it difficult to isolate subject line performance from audience quality or timing effects. The Nashville/Tennessee campaign (July 2025) represents a cluster of 6 low-performing emails that may reflect a particularly cold or unresponsive list segment rather than subject line failures alone, which could be skewing the wording-pattern conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing newsletter emails all use the 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject line format, consistently outperforming the 'Medallion Product Update - [Month] YYYY' format by a meaningful margin. However, these roundup emails were sent to very small audiences (6–10 recipients), making direct comparisons to the product update emails (23–27 recipients) unreliable. Despite strong open rates, every single email in this dataset has a 0% click rate, which is the most actionable problem to address — opens are healthy but no one is clicking through. The immediate priority is diagnosing and fixing the click-through problem before optimizing further for open rates.

    Strong Signal

    Every email in the dataset has a 0% click rate, indicating a systemic content or CTA problem.

    0.0% click rate across all 10 emails

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA placement of both email formats immediately — add at least one prominent, specific CTA (e.g., 'See what's new →') and verify all links are functional before the next send.

    Audience size varies significantly across sends, undermining direct open-rate comparisons.

    Audience sizes range from 6 to 27 recipients

    Try this next: Standardize send lists or segment analysis by audience size before drawing conclusions about which format performs better.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalized 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform 'Medallion Product Update - [Month]' on open rates.

    54.7% average open rate for Roundup emails vs. 47.1% for Product Update emails
    Show reasoning

    The five Roundup emails averaged roughly 54.7% open rates while the four Product Update emails averaged roughly 47.1%. The 'Your' possessive framing and emoji may contribute to higher engagement, but Roundup emails were sent to substantially smaller lists (6–10) versus Product Update lists (23–27), which can inflate open rates due to list composition effects.

    Subject lines using the word 'Your' and an emoji (✨) are associated with higher open rates.

    All top 5 open rates (40%–75%) belong to the 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' format
    Show reasoning

    Every top-5 email uses the 'Your … ✨' pattern, while none of the bottom-5 do. This is a consistent directional signal, but audience size differences mean the format difference and audience size difference are confounded — it cannot be isolated as the sole driver.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate performance.

    Subject line character counts range from ~22 ('Your July Roundup ✨') to ~36 ('Medallion Product Update - April 2026')

    Shorter Roundup subjects do tend to have higher open rates, but this is fully confounded with the subject line wording and audience size differences. There is no clean length-only signal in this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be assessed from the available data.

    Dates span from 2025-08-06 to 2026-06-09 with no time-of-day data provided

    Only calendar dates are available, and with one email per month there is no within-format variation to analyze. No timing pattern can be supported or refuted.

    Data quality notes: This dataset contains only 10 emails sent to very small audiences ranging from 6 to 27 recipients, which severely limits statistical reliability — open rate swings of 10–15+ percentage points can result from a single person opening or not opening an email. The two email formats (Roundup vs. Product Update) are also sent to different-sized lists, making direct performance comparisons confounded. All click rates are 0.0%, which may indicate a tracking issue, broken links, or genuinely absent CTAs — this should be verified against the email platform's raw data. No subject lines appear to be repeated across segments in this dataset.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 291 32.0% 5.5%
    Your insights are needed! 308 28.9% 7.5%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 278 25.2% 4.7%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 220 22.3% 4.1%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing survey emails combined personalization or monetary incentive framing in the subject line with moderate list sizes (220–308 recipients), achieving open rates of 22–32%. The highest open rate (32%) went to an email with a merge-tag personalization and explicit $20 incentive, but notably the highest click rate (7.5%) came from 'Your insights are needed!' — which had no incentive mention — suggesting incentive framing drives opens but not necessarily clicks. The one outlier (7 recipients, 0% open/click) should be excluded from pattern analysis. Future sends should test personalization plus incentive in the subject line while also exploring what drove stronger click-through on the non-incentive version.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines mentioning a monetary incentive ($20) tend to drive higher open rates but not higher click rates.

    32.0% open rate (incentive + personalization) vs. 7.5% click rate on non-incentive email
    Show reasoning

    The two emails mentioning '$20' averaged a 27.2% open rate vs. 27.1% for the two non-incentive emails (excluding the 7-recipient outlier), so the open rate lift is modest overall — but the single highest open rate (32%) belongs to the incentive + personalization combo. Meanwhile, the highest click rate (7.5%) belongs to 'Your insights are needed!' with no incentive, suggesting incentive framing may attract opens from people who don't follow through.

    Personalization (first-name merge tag) in the subject line is associated with the highest open rate in this dataset.

    32.0% open rate for the only personalized subject line vs. 22.3–28.9% for non-personalized peers
    Show reasoning

    Only one email used a first-name token ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!') and it achieved the highest open rate at 32.0%. However, this email also included a $20 incentive, so it is impossible to isolate the personalization effect from the incentive effect with this data alone.

    Shorter, curiosity-driven subject lines appear to drive stronger click-through rates than longer descriptive ones.

    7.5% click rate for 'Your insights are needed!' (30 chars) vs. 4.1–5.5% for longer alternatives
    Show reasoning

    'Your insights are needed!' is the shortest subject line (excluding the outlier) and achieved the highest click rate at 7.5%, while longer lines like 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' had only a 4.7% click rate. The sample is too small to be conclusive, but the directional pattern is consistent.

    'Last call' urgency framing in the subject line does not appear to boost performance relative to other approaches.

    25.2% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' — third out of four valid emails
    Show reasoning

    Despite using scarcity/urgency language ('Last call'), this email ranked third in open rate and second-lowest in click rate (4.7%) among the four valid sends. This suggests urgency alone is insufficient without personalization or a clear value proposition, though the small sample limits confidence.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear, consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 22.3% to 32.0% with no monotonic trend across subject line lengths

    The shortest subject line ranks second in open rate (28.9%), the longest ranks third (25.2%), and the highest open rate belongs to a mid-length personalized line. No reliable length-to-open-rate pattern emerges from these five emails.

    Send timing does not show a clear performance pattern across this dataset.

    Top open rates span September 17 through October 7 with no clustering by day of week

    The four valid sends occurred on September 17, 23, 29, and October 7 across different days of the week. Open rates do not increase or decrease directionally with send date, and there is no day-of-week or time-of-day data available to assess intra-week timing effects.

    Data quality notes: This analysis covers only 5 emails with a very small sample — 4 usable sends ranging from 220 to 308 recipients each, which is too small to draw statistically significant conclusions. The fifth send (subject: 'Share your experience with Medallion?', sent June 2026) had only 7 recipients and 0% open and click rates, making it an extreme outlier likely caused by a test or segment error; it has been excluded from all pattern analysis. Audience sizes vary by up to ~40% across sends (220 vs. 308), which could independently influence rates. No subject lines appear to be exact repeats across sends, so segmentation overlap cannot be assessed from this data alone.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (3 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! 115 78.3% 4.3%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  112 59.8% 7.1%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? 103 57.3% 2.9%
    How AI is transforming hospital operations 100 54.0% 5.0%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar 81 53.1% 2.5%
    What this tells us

    Top-performing webinar emails in this dataset cluster around two content themes: urgency-driven event reminders for 'Elevate' (the flagship conference) and personalized or incentive-based outreach (coffee kits, gift boxes). The highest open rates consistently appear on smaller, targeted sends, while large-list sends (500+) plateau in the 33–38% range regardless of subject line approach. To improve performance on large sends, the data suggests leading with specific, provocative value propositions (e.g., 'bitter truth about payer enrollment delays') rather than generic invitation language. Cocktail/mixology-themed subject lines underperformed relative to other incentive framings, suggesting the coffee/gift incentive angle resonates more strongly with this audience.

    Strong Signal

    Urgency and scarcity language in subject lines drives above-average open rates on large sends.

    59.8% open rate on 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' (n=112)

    Try this next: Include explicit deadline or scarcity language ('Last chance,' 'ends tonight,' 'final spots') in at least one email per campaign sequence, particularly for sends to lists over 100.

    Curiosity-gap or 'bitter truth' subject lines outperform straightforward descriptive ones for payer enrollment topics.

    50.6% open rate on 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' (n=83) vs. 39.9% on '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation in payer enrollment and medical credentialing' (n=163)

    Try this next: Frame payer enrollment and credentialing webinar subjects around a specific pain or counterintuitive insight rather than topic descriptions or '[You're invited]' openers.

    Cocktail and mixology-themed incentive subject lines significantly underperformed coffee and gift-box incentives.

    16.5% open rate on 'Crafting better care and cocktails at Medallion's Demo Day' (n=127) vs. 43.1% on 'Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?' (n=109)

    Try this next: Replace cocktail/mixology incentive framing with coffee, chocolate, or curated gift-box language in Demo Day and event promotion emails.

    Large-list Elevate campaign sends (500+) appear to plateau around 33–38% open rates regardless of subject line variation.

    Open rates for sends of 500+ range from 23.8% to 37.8%, with most clustering at 33–38%

    Try this next: Segment large Elevate lists into smaller, more targeted cohorts based on role or past engagement to break through the 33–38% ceiling rather than relying solely on subject line optimization.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines referencing a specific, named geographic or personalized insight ('slowest enrollment timeline states') drive strong opens on targeted segments.

    56.2% open rate on 'You're in one of the slowest enrollment timeline states' (n=16)
    Show reasoning

    This personalized-insight subject line achieved 56.2% opens, compared to 43.5% for the 'fastest enrollment timeline states' variant on a larger list (n=62), suggesting the negative/pain framing outperforms the positive. However, the n=16 sample for the top performer is small, limiting confidence.

    Broken personalization tokens in subject lines do not appear to suppress open rates on large sends.

    36.1% open rate on the broken-token subject line sent to 584 recipients
    Show reasoning

    Two emails with unrendered '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' placeholders achieved 36.1% and 34.1% open rates on lists of 584 and 551 respectively, on par with similar Elevate reminder emails. This suggests the audience still opened despite visible rendering errors, though this may reflect brand familiarity with the Elevate event rather than tolerance for broken tokens in general.

    Post-event 'thank you' and on-demand emails to attendees achieve high open rates but near-zero click rates, suggesting engagement drop-off after the event.

    'Thank you for joining us! Discover more inside' — 100% open, 0.0% click (n=1); 'Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere' — 92.3% open, 7.7% click (n=13)
    Show reasoning

    Multiple post-event emails ('Thank you for being a part of Elevate 2025,' 'We missed you at Elevate!,' 'Sorry we missed you! Watch on-demand') show strong opens but low or zero click rates, indicating recipients open out of recognition but don't take the next action. Sample sizes are small for several of these, limiting confidence.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear, consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 17.6% to 78.3% across both short and long subject lines at comparable audience sizes

    Short subjects like 'Demo Day available on demand' (100% open, n=2) and long ones like '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation in payer enrollment and medical credentialing' (100%, n=1 vs. 39.9%, n=163) both appear at the top and middle of the distribution. Audience size and content theme appear to be stronger predictors than subject length, making it impossible to isolate a length effect from this data.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not show a discernible pattern affecting open rates in this dataset.

    High-performing emails are distributed across dates from July 2025 through June 2026 with no clustering

    Top-performing sends occur across a wide spread of dates and months with no apparent clustering around particular days or periods. Without send-time metadata (day of week, hour), and given that campaign sequence position (first invite vs. last chance) appears to be the dominant variable, no send-timing insight can be responsibly drawn from this data.

    Data quality notes: Several emails were sent to only 1–4 recipients, producing 100% or 0% open/click rates that are statistically meaningless and likely represent test sends or single-contact sequences — these should not be weighted equally with sends of 100+. At least three subject lines appear in multiple rows with different send counts and dates, indicating segmented or resent campaigns (e.g., '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation...' appears at n=1 and n=163; 'You're invited: The 2026 medical credentialing & payer enrollment reality' appears three times). Comparing these variants directly is only valid when audience size and segment composition are known. The dataset lacks send-time, day-of-week, and audience-segment metadata, which limits causal attribution of open rate differences to subject line alone.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Final Seats Remaining for Our Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner in Boston 68 30.9% 4.4%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 88 27.3% 1.1%
    A Seat at the Table: Join Medallion for an Exclusive Dinner at Aquavit in New York 90 24.4% 1.1%
    Final Call: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner at Boston’s Acclaimed TABLE 63 22.2% 3.2%
    One Week Away: Dinner at The Finch – Few Spots Left 60 21.7% 1.7%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing micro event emails consistently feature casual, specific venue names or experiential hooks in the subject line, and they tend to go to smaller, more targeted lists. Emails referencing a concrete venue (e.g., 'Flour + Water,' 'Juniper and Ivy,' 'Stillwell's') or a unique experience (Golden Knights) outperform generic 'exclusive evening' framing. Urgency language like 'Last Call' and 'Final Seats' drives strong click rates when the audience is already warm. The takeaway: lead with the venue or experience name, keep lists tight and targeted, and reserve urgency language for follow-up sends rather than opening outreach.

    Strong Signal

    Casual, venue-specific or experience-named subject lines outperform generic 'exclusive evening' phrasing on open rates.

    Top 4 open rates (38–50%) all name a specific venue or branded experience; generic 'exclusive evening' emails average ~15% open rate.

    Try this next: Lead every event invite subject line with the venue name or branded experience title rather than generic 'exclusive' descriptors.

    Urgency and 'last call' language in follow-up sends produces the highest click rates, not the highest open rates.

    'Last Call: Golden Knights' (11.5% CTR), 'Don't Miss: Golden Knights' (12.5% CTR), and 'Final Seats: Boston' (4.4% CTR) all exceed their sequence's earlier sends on clicks.

    Try this next: Structure sequences so the first send uses a venue/experience hook, then deploy urgency language ('Last Call,' 'Final Seats') in the second or third follow-up to maximize clicks from engaged prospects.

    Moderate Signal

    Shorter, conversational subject lines with a question mark or casual tone perform better than longer formal ones.

    'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' (38.9% open rate, 18 sent) vs. the longer 'Final Call: Exclusive Healthcare Leaders Dinner at Boston's Acclaimed TABLE' (22.2% open rate, 63 sent).
    Show reasoning

    The two 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' and 'Dinner @ Stillwell's Tonight?' lines (29–39% open rates) are among the shortest in the dataset and use informal punctuation. However, the sample sizes are small (18–30 recipients) and both targeted audiences may have been pre-warmed, so list temperature could confound this finding. The pattern is directional but not conclusively proven at scale.

    Smaller, highly targeted send lists (under 30 recipients) tend to produce higher open rates than large broadcast sends.

    Emails sent to fewer than 30 recipients average ~32% open rate; emails sent to 60+ recipients average ~21% open rate.
    Show reasoning

    The top 8 open rates all come from sends of 30 or fewer recipients. However, small lists are also more likely to be sent to pre-warmed or pre-qualified contacts, which confounds whether list size itself or list quality is the true driver. The two Boston TABLE emails sent to only 6 people each had low open rates (16.7%), showing small size alone doesn't guarantee performance.

    Conference-anchored invites ('During JPM') can outperform standalone dinner invites when the hook is contextually relevant.

    'Executive Dinner at Flour + Water During JPM' achieved 46.2% open rate and 7.7% CTR at 13 sends.
    Show reasoning

    This email explicitly ties the dinner to an industry conference (JPM Healthcare Conference), which likely made attendance feel logical and low-friction for already-traveling recipients. However, with only 13 recipients this is a very small sample and could reflect list quality rather than the conference anchor itself.

    The Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience emails (0% open rate) underperformed every other experiential subject line, suggesting branded experience hooks are not universally effective.

    Both 'Emerald Evening: Wizard of Oz Sphere Experience' sends achieved 0.0% open rate across 14 total recipients.
    Show reasoning

    Unlike the Golden Knights experience (which ranged from 27–33% open rates), the Oz Sphere Experience generated zero opens across two separate sends in February 2026. This could reflect audience fit, list quality, or that the cultural resonance of the Golden Knights (sports, Vegas) outperforms a theatrical/entertainment hook for a B2B healthcare audience. Sample sizes are small but the total failure across both sends is notable.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a clear, consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    High performers range from 9-word ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') to 18-word ('In Vegas? Join us for Medallion's Power Play: A Las Vegas Golden Knights Experience') subjects.

    Both very short and relatively long subject lines appear at the top and bottom of the open rate rankings. The 'In Vegas? Golden Knights' subject (33.3% open rate) is long, while short 'Don't Miss: Nashville' lines rank near the bottom. The venue/experience content appears to matter more than character count.

    Send timing (day of week or proximity to event) does not produce a distinguishable pattern in this dataset.

    Open rates range from 0% to 50% across emails sent on various dates with no clustering by weekday or lead time.

    The data lacks send-time granularity (no hour of day) and dates span a wide range without enough controlled comparisons to isolate a timing effect. Sequences with multiple sends show declining open rates over time within a sequence, but this is likely audience exhaustion rather than a timing signal.

    Data quality notes: Several critical caveats apply: (1) Sample sizes are very small—most sends range from 1 to 90 recipients, making statistical significance impossible; a single open or click can swing rates by 10+ percentage points. (2) The same subject line appears across multiple sends with different list sizes and dates (e.g., 'In Vegas? Golden Knights' sent to both 3 and 11 recipients on the same date; 'Aquavit' sent to 88 and 90 on different dates), suggesting these are segmented sends to different audience slices rather than A/B tests—open rate differences between them should not be interpreted as causal. (3) List quality and warmth almost certainly vary across sends (e.g., conference attendee lists vs. cold outreach), which confounds comparisons between campaigns. All insights should be treated as directional hypotheses to test at larger scale rather than proven conclusions.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    [Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing 108 43.5% 2.8%
     State-by-state payer delays and what they’re costing you 102 40.2% 3.9%
    Stuck in enrollment bottlenecks? Here’s what the data says 109 34.9% 3.7%
    When a local hospital closes, are you ready? 51 33.3% 0.0%
    The hidden crisis behind hospital closures 51 19.6% 2.0%
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails in this dataset consistently lean on urgency, financial stakes, and concrete data to drive opens — subject lines that name a specific cost (e.g., '$1M+'), reference a timely report, or invoke a pressing operational crisis outperform more generic or introspective framings. Bracket-formatted labels like '[Report]' and '[You're invited]' appear in three of the five highest open-rate emails, suggesting explicit content-type signaling is working. Click rates remain low even on high-open emails, indicating the content engages curiosity enough to open but isn't consistently converting to action. Future sends should pair strong urgency or data-driven subject lines with a clearer, more compelling in-email CTA to close the open-to-click gap.

    Strong Signal

    Hospital closure and operational crisis themes generate consistent mid-to-upper-tier opens across multiple sends.

    Four sends on the hospital closure theme ranged from 26.1% to 34.9% open rate across 43–51 recipients

    Try this next: Continue using hospital closure and operational disruption angles; this theme drives both opens and the rare high click-through, so test pairing it with a direct download or demo CTA.

    Emails with the highest open rates almost universally have 0.0% click rates, revealing a consistent open-to-click conversion failure.

    9 of the top 13 emails by open rate recorded 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA placement for high-open, zero-click emails; test adding a single, prominent, benefit-led CTA button rather than relying on embedded text links.

    Repeated sends of the same subject line to different segments show significant open rate variance, suggesting audience fit matters more than subject wording alone.

    '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' sent three times: 43.5%, 30.8%, and 20.8% open rates across lists of 108, 39, and 24 respectively

    Try this next: Before optimizing subject line wording, audit segment definitions; the same message can perform 2x better or worse depending on which list it reaches.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines that name a specific financial cost or quantified outcome drive the highest open rates.

    57.1% open rate for '$1M+' cost-framed subject vs. 12.1%–25.7% for generic operational themes
    Show reasoning

    The top-performing email references '$1M+' in direct costs and achieved 57.1% opens. The State of Payer Enrollment report (43.5%) and 'state-by-state payer delays' (40.2%) also use specific, data-anchored language. However, very small send volumes (7–8 recipients) inflate rates for the top two emails, so this pattern is directional rather than definitive.

    Bracket-formatted content-type labels (e.g., '[Report]', '[You're invited]', '[Final chance]') correlate with above-average open rates.

    Three of the top 5 open rates belong to bracketed subject lines, averaging ~50% vs. ~28% for non-bracketed
    Show reasoning

    Emails with bracket prefixes — '[Report]', '[You're invited]', '[Final chance for coffee]' — cluster at the top of the open-rate rankings. Non-bracketed emails are more scattered across mid and lower ranges. The caveat is that bracketed emails include very small send lists (7–8), which inflates rates and limits statistical reliability.

    The 'coffee break' conversational framing underperformed compared to direct urgency or data-driven subject lines.

    '☕ Coffee Break?' variant received 14.3% opens vs. 57.1% for the direct '[You're invited]' version on a similar topic
    Show reasoning

    Two emails on the same provider-readiness/time-to-revenue topic were sent on similar dates — one using a casual coffee emoji framing (14.3% open rate) and one using a direct cost-impact framing (57.1%). The audience sizes are tiny (7 each), so this is directional, but the gap is stark. The 'A latte teams are dealing with onboarding delays' pun subject (30.2%) performed better than the emoji version, adding a mild counterpoint.

    No Signal

    Subject line character length does not show a clear pattern correlated with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Top open rates span subjects from ~30 to ~80 characters with no discernible length gradient

    The highest-performing subjects range from short ('Why specialty care access is breaking down') to quite long ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+)'). Both long and short subjects appear across the full range of open rates, so length alone is not a reliable predictor here. The data does not support a length-based recommendation.

    Send timing (day of week or month) does not reveal a consistent performance pattern in this dataset.

    High open rates appear across April, May, July, October, and November sends with no clustering

    Top-performing emails span multiple months and no single send window clusters with notably higher performance. The dataset lacks send-time (hour) data entirely, and the date spread is too uneven to draw seasonal conclusions. No timing-based recommendation is supported by this data.

    Data quality notes: Several key caveats apply. First, the top two emails by open rate were sent to only 7–8 recipients each — at this size, a single open or non-open swings the rate by 12–14 percentage points, making those figures statistically unreliable. Second, at least five subject lines were sent multiple times to different segments on the same or nearby dates (e.g., '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment' appears three times; 'When a local hospital closes' and 'When hospitals close' each appear twice), so cross-send comparisons conflate subject line performance with segment quality. Third, several subject lines contain '\ xa0' encoding artifacts suggesting copy-paste or export issues, which may indicate data hygiene problems upstream. Insights should be treated as directional hypotheses rather than statistically validated findings given the small and segmented nature of the sends.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The highest open rates in this dataset belong almost exclusively to emails sent to very small audiences (2–5 recipients), which makes those percentages statistically unreliable as performance benchmarks. Among emails with larger sends (12+ recipients), the $43M funding announcement to 42 recipients achieved 40.5% opens — the strongest result at meaningful scale — suggesting that milestone or company news framing outperforms routine feature updates with broader audiences. Across the board, click rates are nearly zero regardless of open rate, indicating that announcement emails in this program are not driving downstream action. Future emails should focus on improving click-through by pairing announcements with a single, specific CTA rather than relying on open rate alone as a success metric.

    Strong Signal

    Emails sent to very small lists (≤5 recipients) dominate the top open rates, inflating apparent performance.

    7 of the top 11 open rates (≥50%) come from sends with 5 or fewer recipients.

    Try this next: Segment your analysis by audience size and set a minimum send threshold (e.g., n≥12) before drawing optimization conclusions from open rate data.

    Click rates are effectively zero across the entire announcement email program regardless of open rate.

    23 of 25 emails recorded 0.0% click rate; only 3 emails had any clicks at all, none above 33.3% on tiny lists.

    Try this next: Add one explicit, specific CTA to every announcement email (e.g., 'View the new report', 'Try it now in Medallion') and measure click rate as a primary KPI, not just opens.

    Moderate Signal

    Benefit- or action-oriented subject lines ('Submit multi-state enrollments easier', 'Unlocking faster Payer Enrollment follow-up') outperform feature-label subjects at comparable send sizes.

    'Submit multi-state enrollments easier with new license flexibility' (n=3) achieved the dataset's only non-zero click rate among small sends at 33.3%.
    Show reasoning

    Subjects framing the user benefit or workflow improvement appear to drive both opens and the rare clicks, while purely descriptive labels like 'New Analytic reports are now available for X' consistently produced 0% clicks. However, the send sizes are too small to be conclusive, and the benefit-framing emails are not all at comparable scale.

    Repeating the same subject line across multiple segmented sends does not consistently improve or hurt open rates — results are mixed.

    'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' achieved 100% opens at n=2 but 0% at n=1; '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse' got 23.5% at n=34 and 0% at n=1.
    Show reasoning

    Multiple subject lines were reused across separate sends, but the outcome differences are likely explained by audience size and segment quality rather than the repetition itself. No clear penalty or benefit from reuse is visible in this data.

    The $43M funding announcement achieved the best open rate among large-audience sends, suggesting company milestone news resonates broadly.

    40.5% open rate on n=42 send, the highest open rate for any email with 12+ recipients.
    Show reasoning

    The funding announcement outperformed all feature-update and product-change emails at comparable scale. However, this is a single event type and the two other versions of the same announcement (n=34 at 23.5%, n=2 at 0%) show inconsistent results, likely due to audience segment differences.

    'Coming soon' preview emails do not consistently outperform 'Now live' launch emails.

    'Coming soon: Provider profile redesign + new required fields' (n=4) hit 75% opens vs. 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' (n=2) at 50%, but both are tiny sends.
    Show reasoning

    There are matched preview/launch pairs in the data (provider profile, standard payer names), but both sends in each pair are at very small scale. No reliable performance difference between teaser and launch framing is observable at meaningful audience sizes.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both a 44-character subject ('Generate API keys directly in Medallion') and a 79-character subject ('New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding').

    Both short and long subject lines appear at the top and bottom of the open-rate ranking. The dominant driver appears to be audience size rather than subject length, so no length-based recommendation is supported by this data.

    Send timing (day of week or time of day) cannot be evaluated from this dataset.

    Only send dates are available — no send-time or day-of-week data is present for any of the 25 emails.

    Without hour-of-day and day-of-week fields, it is impossible to identify any timing pattern. Dates alone span from August 2025 to July 2026 with no clustering that correlates clearly with performance when audience size is controlled for.

    Data quality notes: Sample size and audience-size skew are the primary data quality concerns. Of 25 emails, 14 were sent to 5 or fewer recipients, meaning a single open or click produces outsized percentage rates that are not meaningful for optimization. Several subject lines appear across multiple sends/segments (e.g., 'New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' sent twice, '$43M raised to scale the first national credentialing clearinghouse' sent twice, 'Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon' sent twice with slightly different formatting), making it impossible to isolate subject line performance from audience segment effects. Conclusions should be drawn primarily from the 8 emails with 12 or more recipients.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    No emails with 50+ recipients
    What this tells us

    The top-performing emails are all 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' formats sent to very small audiences (3–8 recipients), consistently achieving 50–67% open rates. The larger-audience 'Medallion Product Update' series, sent to 24–25 recipients, shows a clear downward trend in open rates over time (44% in May down to 25% in February), suggesting list fatigue or declining relevance as the audience scales. The personalized, possessive framing ('Your') and the sparkle emoji appear correlated with stronger opens, though the audience-size difference makes this difficult to isolate. To improve the Product Update series, consider testing 'Your [Month] Roundup' style subject lines and adding more compelling CTAs, since click rates across the board are near zero despite reasonable opens.

    Strong Signal

    Click rates are effectively zero across almost all emails, indicating content or CTA execution is not driving action despite reasonable opens.

    9 of 11 emails have a 0.0% click rate

    Try this next: Audit the body content and CTA of all newsletter emails — add a single, prominent, value-driven link and test whether click rates respond, starting with the higher-open Roundup format.

    Emails sent to extremely small lists (1–3 recipients) produce unreliable rate data.

    'Your June Roundup ✨' sent to 1 recipient achieved 0.0% open rate; 'Your December Roundup ✨' sent to 3 achieved 66.7%

    Try this next: Exclude emails with fewer than 10 recipients from performance benchmarking until list sizes grow.

    Moderate Signal

    Possessive 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' subject lines outperform 'Medallion Product Update' branding on open rate.

    60–67% avg open rate vs. 25–44% for Product Update series
    Show reasoning

    All six 'Your [Month] Roundup ✨' emails (with 3–8 recipients each) achieved 50–67% open rates, while the four 'Medallion Product Update' emails (24–25 recipients) ranged from 25–44%. The possessive framing and emoji may drive curiosity, but the much smaller audience sizes in the Roundup group make a clean comparison impossible — small lists are prone to high variance.

    Shorter, warmer subject lines ('Your [Month] Roundup ✨') outperform longer, brand-forward ones ('Medallion Product Update - [Month] YYYY').

    'Your August Roundup ✨' (18 chars) at 66.7% vs. 'Medallion Product Update - February 2026' (40 chars) at 25.0%
    Show reasoning

    The Roundup subjects average ~22 characters while the Product Update subjects average ~38 characters. Shorter subjects consistently correspond to higher opens here, but again, audience size confounds this — the short-subject emails also had far fewer recipients. The pattern is directional but not conclusive.

    The 'Medallion Product Update' series shows a declining open-rate trend as the audience grew from 24 to 25 recipients over four months.

    Open rate fell from 41.7% (April) to 33.3% (March) to 25.0% (February) before recovering to 44.0% (May)
    Show reasoning

    Ordering the four Product Update emails chronologically shows February at 25%, March at 33%, April at 42%, and May at 44% — actually an improving trend, not declining. The February dip may reflect post-holiday inbox fatigue or a cold-start effect for this new format. The sample is only four sends, so no strong trend can be confirmed.

    No Signal

    No send-timing pattern is discernible from this dataset.

    Sends span July 2025–June 2026 with no repeated day-of-week data provided

    Send dates are available but day-of-week and send-time are not included. Among the dates available, sends are spread across different months with no clustering that reveals a timing pattern. No conclusion can be drawn about optimal send day or time.

    Data quality notes: Sample size is very small (11 emails total), and there is a significant audience-size skew: six 'Roundup' emails were sent to 1–8 recipients each, while five 'Medallion Product Update' emails were sent to 24–25 recipients. Open and click rates for micro-sends (especially the 1–3 recipient emails) are statistically meaningless — a single open or non-open moves the rate by 33–100 percentage points. No subject lines appear to have been repeated across segments. The two email series also differ in format, branding, and apparent audience segment, making direct performance comparisons unreliable without controlling for list composition.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectSentOpen RateClick Rate
    {{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights! 201 37.3% 9.0%
    Last call to take Medallion’s annual benchmarking survey 176 34.7% 6.8%
    Your insights are needed! 214 26.2% 6.1%
    Your chance to provide feedback + get $20 215 21.9% 6.5%
    What this tells us

    Survey emails that lead with a specific monetary incentive ($20) in the subject line outperform those with vague benefit language, and urgency framing ('Last call') sustains strong open rates even later in a sequence. The highest-volume sends that foregrounded the reward still underperformed the urgency-anchored follow-up, suggesting that scarcity language may amplify incentive effectiveness more than the incentive alone. For future campaigns, combining a clear dollar amount with deadline urgency in the subject line is the most data-supported lever to test. The top performer by volume-adjusted metrics is the 'Last call' email, making urgency + specificity the core formula to replicate.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines combining a dollar incentive with urgency or specificity outperform generic benefit language.

    15.4 percentage-point open rate gap between '$20 for your insights!' (37.3%) and 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' (21.9%)
    Show reasoning

    Both subjects reference the $20 reward, but the higher-performing one leads with the personalization token and frames the reward as immediate ('for your insights!'), while the lower-performing one buries the incentive at the end after vague language. The 'Last call' email (34.7%) has no monetary mention at all but uses urgency, suggesting the incentive is not the only driver. Sample sizes are comparable (201 vs. 215 sends), making this a reasonably fair comparison.

    Urgency framing ('Last call') drives above-average open rates even without a monetary incentive.

    34.7% open rate for 'Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey' vs. 26.2% for the non-urgent, non-incentive 'Your insights are needed!'
    Show reasoning
