    The 'Last call' email outperformed the non-incentive, non-urgent subject by 8.5 percentage points on comparable send volumes (176 vs. 214). This suggests deadline urgency is a meaningful driver on its own. However, send date differs (Oct 7 vs. Sep 23), so sequence position may partially explain the gap — recipients who ignored earlier emails may have responded to the final nudge.

    Personalization tokens in the subject line are associated with higher open rates, but the signal is confounded.

    37.3% open rate for the subject using '{{ contact.firstname }}' vs. 21.9%–26.2% for the non-personalized comparables
    Show reasoning

    The only email using a first-name token ('{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!') has the highest open rate among sends with 200+ recipients. However, it also leads with the incentive, making it impossible to isolate whether personalization or incentive framing is driving performance. The unrendered token ('{{ contact.firstname }}') also raises a data quality concern — if the token failed to render for some recipients, the true effect may differ.

    Click-through rates are highest in the email with the clearest, most immediate value proposition in the subject line.

    9.0% click rate for '$20 for your insights!' vs. 6.1%–6.8% for the other high-volume sends
    Show reasoning

    Among sends with 170+ recipients, the email leading with the personalized name and explicit $20 reward achieved a 9.0% click rate — roughly 2–3 percentage points above the others. This suggests that when recipients who open already expect a clear reward, they are more likely to click through. The 'Last call' email had a slightly higher open rate but lower click rate (6.8%), possibly because urgency drives opens but the body content matters more for clicks.

    No Signal

    Shorter, punchier subject lines do not consistently outperform longer, descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Subject lengths range from 4 words ('Your insights are needed!') to 9 words ('Last call to take Medallion's annual benchmarking survey') with no clear correlation to open rate.

    The longest subject line ('Last call…', 9 words) ranks second by open rate, while the shortest actionable subject ('Your insights are needed!', 4 words) ranks fourth. There is no discernible length-performance relationship across these five emails.

    Send timing and day-of-week patterns cannot be assessed from this data.

    Dates span Sep 17 to Oct 7, 2025, with one outlier in Jun 2026 — no time-of-day data provided.

    All sends fall within a 3-week window across varied weekdays, and no send-time data is available. Additionally, the Jun 2026 send has only 4 recipients, making it unsuitable for timing analysis. No timing conclusions can be drawn.

    Data quality notes: Sample sizes vary substantially: the top-ranked email by open rate ('Share your experience with Medallion?') was sent to only 4 recipients, making its 50% open rate and 25% click rate statistically meaningless and unsuitable for pattern extraction. The remaining four emails range from 176–215 sends, which is a small but more usable B2B sample. The '{{ contact.firstname }}' token appears unrendered in the subject line data, suggesting a possible merge-tag failure that could have affected deliverability or open rates for that send. No emails appear to be exact repeats, but the Sep 17, Sep 23, and Sep 29 sends appear to be a sequenced cadence to overlapping or identical audiences, meaning their performance is interdependent rather than independent observations.

    ⚠ Insufficient data (4 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ Insufficient data (2 emails, need 5+ for reliable analysis)

    ⚠ No eligible emails for this content type and persona combination.

    All Personas

    Emails across all content types for the selected persona, analyzed together — surfaces patterns a single content type can't (e.g. a persona that engages consistently, or is cold, regardless of what's sent).

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 2,010 64.6% 1.8%
    Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do Announcement 96 61.5% 2.1%
    New profile completion logic is now live Product Release 78 55.1% 10.3%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit Webinar 735 54.7% 2.2%
    You’re So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today Webinar 56 53.6% 32.1%
    What this tells us

    Across all 196 emails, engagement is heavily stratified by audience size and content type: highly targeted sends (under ~300 recipients) to existing customers or warm audiences consistently outperform broad prospecting blasts, with announcements and small-batch webinars averaging open rates well above 40% while cold micro-event and survey campaigns to thousands frequently fall below 10%. The data suggests this segment responds strongly to operational urgency and product relevance — subjects tied to specific platform changes, regulatory deadlines, or direct invitations to named experiences drive the highest opens and clicks. Broad Elevate conference promotion and Nashville/Atlanta dinner blasts show the weakest returns, indicating list saturation or poor targeting rather than message failure. The clearest lever for improvement is tightening list segmentation: replicating the conditions of the top performers (small, relevant audiences + urgent or personalized subjects) rather than scaling content indiscriminately.

    Strong Signal

    Announcements and small-batch webinars dramatically outperform all other content types in open rate.

    Announcements average ~38% open rate; micro-event and survey emails to large lists average under 10%

    Try this next: Prioritize announcement and operationally relevant emails for engagement; when running event or survey campaigns, aggressively shrink and qualify the list before sending rather than blasting the full database.

    Urgency and specificity in subject lines — especially regulatory actions, product changes, and named deadlines — consistently lift open rates above 40%.

    6 of the top 10 open-rate emails reference a specific action, deadline, or named feature (e.g. 'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC', 'New profile completion logic is now live')

    Try this next: Lead every subject line with the specific change, deadline, or benefit — avoid metaphor or teaser language as the primary hook for this segment.

    Click rates are highly decoupled from open rates for large-list sends, but correlate more closely for small targeted sends.

    'Thank You + Quick Feedback Request' (17 sent): 76.5% open / 58.8% click; '[RSVP now] Join healthcare leaders...' (11,209 sent): 22.8% open / 1.0% click

    Try this next: Use click rate — not open rate — as the primary KPI for evaluating whether a campaign reached the right audience; a high open/low click ratio is a signal to tighten the list.

    Moderate Signal

    Personalization token failures in subject lines correlate with below-average open rates.

    Both emails using unrendered '{{ personalization_token(...)}}' tokens had open rates of 22.8% and 20.0% despite lists of 10,000+
    Show reasoning

    Two Elevate reminder emails with broken personalization tokens in the subject line performed at 20–23% open rate, below the average for similarly timed conference promotion emails. However, these were also very large sends where lower rates are expected, so the token failure effect cannot be fully isolated from audience-size dilution.

    Incentive-led survey subjects ('$20 for your insights') outperform generic feedback requests but still underperform all other content types.

    '{{ contact.firstname }} $20 for your insights!' achieved 19.7% vs 5.0% for 'Your chance to provide feedback + get $20' — both surveys, 3.8x gap
    Show reasoning

    Among survey emails, the monetary incentive combined with firstname personalization token produced nearly 4x the open rate of a similarly incentivized but less personalized subject. However, all survey emails remain in the bottom half of the overall dataset, so the gain is relative rather than absolute, and sample sizes per survey send vary widely.

    Coffee-themed and food-incentive subject lines for webinar registration show moderate open rates but weak click-through relative to the hook.

    'You're So Close—Claim Your Elevate Gift Box Today' (56 sent): 53.6% open / 32.1% click vs 'You're so close—Claim your coffee kit' (735 sent): 54.7% open / 2.2% click
    Show reasoning

    The coffee/gift incentive concept works well for open rates at small scale (the gift box email to 56 people had 32% clicks), but the same hook scaled to 735 recipients dropped click rate to 2.2% — suggesting the appeal is not strong enough to convert a cold or broad audience even when it gets them to open. The pattern repeats with the 'bitter truth about payer enrollment delays' coffee pun series at 29–42% open but only 1–2% clicks at scale.

    No Signal

    Shorter, direct subject lines do not show a clear performance advantage over longer descriptive ones in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short ('Demo Day available on demand', 66.7%) and long ('[Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do]', 61.5%) subjects

    The data does not support a consistent length-based pattern: both short and medium-length subjects appear across the full range of open rates, and the bottom of the list also contains both short and long subjects. List size and content relevance appear to be stronger confounders than subject length, making it impossible to isolate length as a driver here.

    Send timing shows no reliable day-of-week or seasonal pattern that predicts higher engagement in this dataset.

    Top open rates are spread across Feb, Aug, Sep, Jan, Jul with no month or period consistently outperforming

    High-performing emails appear in January, February, July, August, and September; low performers are equally distributed across the calendar. Without controlled send-time variation and holding other variables constant, the data does not isolate timing as a performance driver. The audience-size confound is too strong to read timing signal here.

    Data quality notes: Several important caveats apply. First, audience size varies enormously — from 1 to 14,000 recipients — making direct open-rate comparisons unreliable without controlling for list size; small sends to warm audiences will almost always show inflated rates. Second, the dataset mixes all content types for a single broad segment ('All personas'), so type-level averages are computed across very unequal sample sizes (e.g., only 3 onboarding emails had more than 10 sends, while webinar has 60+ entries). Third, several subject lines appear across multiple rows with different send counts and dates, suggesting A/B tests or resends to different sub-segments — these are not independent data points and should not be averaged naively. Fourth, two survey emails show 0 sends and 0% rates, indicating data entry errors or suppressed sends, and one webinar email had only 1 send. Fifth, the onboarding series (Dec 2025) was sent to lists of 3–16 recipients, making any rate for those emails statistically meaningless. Cross-type comparisons should be treated as directional only given these confounds.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your copy is here! Unknown 87 74.7% 55.2%
    Your Medallion Demo Request Unknown 161 73.9% 11.2%
    Upcoming changes: New required fields coming soon Announcement 74 73.0% 14.9%
    New Analytic reports are now available for Consumption and Provider Onboarding Announcement 70 71.4% 1.4%
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 833 69.3% 4.0%
    What this tells us

    The Operations segment shows unusually strong engagement across nearly all content types, with many emails — announcements, webinars, newsletters, and transactional sends — routinely clearing 50%+ open rates. The segment's highest engagement concentrates in smaller, targeted sends (announcements, post-webinar follow-ups, product releases) rather than mass-blast webinar promotions, which tend to fall into the 25–40% range as list size grows. Clicks, however, are inconsistent: high open rates rarely translate to high click rates unless the email contains a clear, singular call to action like a resource download or feedback request. The clearest opportunity is pairing the segment's strong open propensity with more deliberate CTAs, and being cautious about reading into 100% open-rate outliers that were sent to only 1–2 recipients.

    Strong Signal

    Announcements and product releases consistently outperform other content types on open rate for this segment.

    13 of the top 20 emails by open rate (50%+) are announcements or product releases

    Try this next: Prioritize announcement and product release emails for this segment; frame regulatory and product change emails with urgency language to maintain the open-rate advantage.

    Micro event emails perform poorly with Operations, often falling below 20% open rates regardless of venue or occasion.

    Median open rate for micro event emails in this dataset is approximately 21%, with 10 of 22 micro event sends falling below 22%

    Try this next: Reduce micro event send volume to Operations or recraft the value proposition around operational ROI rather than exclusivity when targeting this segment.

    Emails with action-oriented or urgency-driven subject lines (regulatory deadlines, required actions, platform changes going live) generate the strongest open rates at scale.

    'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' achieved 78.6% open rate on 42 sends; 'New required fields coming soon' hit 73% on 74 sends

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with what is changing, what is required, or what is now live — avoid leading with event names or company milestones when sending to Operations personas.

    High open rates for Operations do not reliably predict high click rates — the two metrics are frequently decoupled.

    Multiple emails with 60–78% open rates recorded 0–5% click rates (e.g., 'New Analytic reports for Consumption and Provider Onboarding' at 71.4% open / 1.4% click)

    Try this next: Every announcement or product release email sent to Operations should include one explicit next step (view the feature, read the guide, take action in-platform) rather than treating the open as the goal.

    Moderate Signal

    Webinar emails show wide open-rate variance depending on targeting specificity — personalized or highly specific webinar subjects outperform generic event promotions.

    Specifically targeted webinar sends (e.g., 'You're in one of the slowest enrollment timeline states' at 52.5%; 'Thank you for spending time with us' at 77.8%) outperform broad Elevate promotional blasts averaging 35–39%
    Show reasoning

    Post-webinar follow-ups (thank-you, on-demand watch) and personalized or state-specific invitations consistently scored higher than mass conference promotion emails. However, sample sizes for the high performers are small (9–137), and large Elevate blasts had thousands of sends, making direct comparison imperfect.

    Emails offering a concrete deliverable (downloadable report, recorded demo, resource copy) generate both high open rates and above-average click rates.

    'Your copy is here!' across multiple sends averaged 55–86% open rate and 14–55% click rate; 'Demo Day available on demand' hit 80% open / 20% click
    Show reasoning

    The 'Your copy is here!' sends and on-demand webinar notifications consistently produced some of the highest click rates in the dataset, suggesting that Operations readers who open these emails convert at higher rates when a clear, specific asset is promised. However, these sends tend to go to smaller, pre-qualified lists, which inflates both metrics relative to cold sends.

    No Signal

    Subject line length does not show a consistent relationship with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates include both short subjects ('Your copy is here!' at 74–86%) and long ones ('Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do' at 78.6%)

    Both short and long subject lines appear across the top and bottom of the open-rate distribution. The data does not support a conclusion that shorter or longer subjects drive better performance for this segment — content type and relevance appear to be far stronger predictors than length.

    Send timing does not reveal a clear day-of-week or seasonal pattern that drives open rate differences in this dataset.

    High open rates appear across dates spanning July 2025 through July 2026 with no evident monthly clustering

    Top-performing emails are distributed across multiple months and years without a discernible seasonal or day-of-week pattern. The data does not include send-time granularity within the day, and the variance is better explained by content type and subject relevance than by timing.

    Data quality notes: The dataset contains 207 emails but send volumes vary enormously — from 1 recipient to over 7,700 — meaning many 'top open rate' entries (100% from 1–2 sends, 85.7% from 7 sends) are statistically unreliable and should not be weighted equally with results from 40–900 send emails. Content types are unevenly distributed: micro events (22+ sends) and webinars (40+ sends) dominate by volume, while product releases (5 sends), onboarding (8 sends), and surveys (5 sends) are sparsely represented, making cross-type comparisons directional at best for the less-common types. The subject line 'Your copy is here!' appears across at least 6 separate sends on the same date (2026-01-20) to different list segments, inflating its apparent prevalence. Several subjects contain unresolved personalization tokens (e.g., '{{ personalization_token(contact.firstname...) }}') indicating template errors in at least two Elevate promotional sends. Content typed as 'unknown' (17 emails) limits clean cross-type analysis. The 'Operations' audience likely includes contacts of varying seniority and sub-function within operations, which may mask persona-level differences that a more granular breakdown would reveal.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 178 74.2% 5.1%
    You’re so close—Claim your coffee kit Webinar 130 61.5% 13.1%
    [You’re invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue (and costs some teams $1M+) Content 189 60.3% 5.3%
    Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit Webinar 52 57.7% 11.5%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar Webinar 51 56.9% 3.9%
    What this tells us

    Finance segment contacts engage at notably high rates across nearly all content types, with a large share of emails achieving open rates above 40%—suggesting this is a highly receptive, likely small and relationship-adjacent audience. The strongest performers skew toward urgency-driven webinar reminders, compliance-relevant product announcements, and personalized or small-batch transactional messages, while micro event invitations to cold or broad lists consistently underperform. To improve, the team should lean into compliance/regulatory framing for announcements, maintain urgency in webinar reminder sequences, and reserve micro event sends for warmer, more targeted subsets of the Finance segment.

    Strong Signal

    Engagement is broadly strong across content types, with webinars, announcements, and content emails all regularly exceeding 40% open rates.

    ~70% of non-micro-event emails with 10+ sends achieved open rates above 40%

    Try this next: Maintain volume across all content types for Finance; consider treating micro event invitations as a separate, more tightly curated send rather than a broad Finance list blast.

    Micro event emails consistently underperform across the Finance segment, especially at larger send volumes.

    Median open rate for micro event emails with 40+ sends is ~15%, versus ~45% for webinars at similar volume

    Try this next: Cap broad micro event invitations to Finance contacts and prioritize only highly relevant, geographically or role-matched invites; test a Finance-specific value proposition in the subject line rather than venue-centric framing.

    Compliance and regulatory subject lines drive some of the highest open rates among announcement emails to Finance contacts.

    'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC' achieved 53.8%; 'Coming soon: Standard payer names will be required' achieved 63.3%

    Try this next: Frame product and policy announcement subject lines for Finance contacts around compliance deadlines or 'action required' language rather than feature benefit language.

    Very small send batches (1–4 contacts) inflate apparent open rates and are not reliable performance signals.

    7 of the 8 emails with 100% open rates were sent to 1–4 contacts

    Try this next: Exclude sends under 10 contacts from open-rate benchmarking when evaluating Finance segment campaign performance.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and countdown language in webinar subject lines lifts open rates compared to standard invitation framing.

    'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' hit 64.6%; 'You're So Close—Elevate is less than a month away!' hit 74.2%
    Show reasoning

    Webinar emails with 'last chance,' 'you're so close,' or explicit time-to-event language tend to outperform earlier-funnel invites with broader framing (e.g., 'Rethink revenue cycle performance at Elevate 2025' at 30.0%). However, the segment is small enough that individual list composition may explain some of the gap, and the 74.2% result came from a 178-person send that could reflect a particularly warm sub-list.

    Subject lines referencing financial or revenue-cycle pain points outperform generic operational framing for content emails.

    'The hidden cost of slow payer enrollment' (onboarding) hit 46.7%; '[Final chance for coffee]: Reduce onboarding delays and improve time to revenue' hit 56.2%
    Show reasoning

    Content emails that explicitly name revenue impact, cost, or time-to-revenue resonate with Finance contacts more than those using generic operational language. However, many of these sends are small (under 50 contacts), limiting statistical confidence. The pattern is directionally consistent across several emails but not definitive.

    No Signal

    Subject lines using emoji (☕, ✨, ⏰) do not show a consistent open-rate advantage or disadvantage for Finance contacts.

    Emoji subjects range from 25.8% ('⏰ Last Chance!') to 51.7% ('Your December Roundup ✨')

    Emoji appear in newsletters, webinar reminders, and content emails with wildly varying results. The variation is better explained by content type and list size than by the presence of an emoji, so no directional conclusion can be drawn from this data alone.

    Subject line length shows no clear correlation with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Short ('Your copy is here!' at 100%) and long ('[You're invited] How provider readiness impacts time to revenue' at 60.3%) both perform well

    Top-performing emails span a wide range of subject line lengths, from very short transactional lines to long descriptive ones. Bottom performers also appear across the length spectrum. No meaningful length-based pattern is visible in this Finance segment data.

    Emails sent in August–October 2025 and January–May 2026 both show strong engagement, with no evidence of a seasonal dip for Finance contacts.

    High-performing emails (40%+ open rate) appear in every month from July 2025 through June 2026

    The data spans roughly 12 months and high open rates appear consistently throughout, with no month clearly outperforming others. Send volume is also uneven across months, making a timing conclusion unreliable. No actionable send-timing pattern is supported by this data.

    Data quality notes: This dataset covers 186 email sends to the Finance segment across 11+ content types, but audience sizes vary enormously—from 1-contact sends to 1,386-contact sends—making direct open-rate comparisons unreliable without weighting by send volume. Several subject lines (e.g., 'Your copy is here!', 'Test your operational readiness with this worksheet', '$43M raised...', 'Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays') appear multiple times with different send counts and dates, likely representing the same campaign deployed to different sub-segments or retargeted lists; their results should not be treated as independent data points. Micro events and webinars are the most represented content types by send count, while onboarding, virtual event, and survey emails have very few sends, limiting cross-type conclusions for those categories. The 'unknown' content type (13 entries) introduces ambiguity—some of these (e.g., 'Your Medallion Demo Request' at 85.7% with 21 sends) could significantly affect type-level averages if correctly categorized.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    Your Medallion Demo Request Unknown 113 80.5% 12.4%
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 335 72.5% 3.9%
    Thanks for your interest in Medallion Unknown 139 71.9% 4.3%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  Webinar 246 63.0% 7.7%
    Medallion Product Update - February 2026 Newsletter 76 61.8% 2.6%
    What this tells us

    Founder/CEO/President contacts are highly engaged with transactional, product-relevant, and event-driven content, with many email types sustaining open rates above 50%. The segment responds especially well to personalized or high-context sends — demo requests, direct invitations, and product announcements — while broader cold-list outreach (micro events to large lists, generic webinar funnels) underperforms significantly. The clearest opportunity is to prioritize sends that feel directly relevant to a CEO's operational or strategic concerns rather than nurture-style or awareness content. Shrinking list sizes to better-matched audiences would likely lift overall performance metrics across the board.

    Strong Signal

    Engagement is consistently strong across nearly all content types, with open rates well above typical B2B benchmarks regardless of format.

    Median open rate across the dataset is approximately 43%, spanning announcements, webinars, newsletters, content, and micro events.

    Try this next: Maintain diversity of content types since this segment responds well across formats; focus optimization energy on list quality and relevance rather than content type selection.

    High-intent transactional emails — demo requests and event confirmations — achieve the highest reliable open rates among statistically meaningful send sizes.

    'Your Medallion Demo Request' sent to 113 contacts achieved 80.5% open rate; 'Confirm your registration for Elevate 2025 networking event' sent to 6 contacts achieved 83.3%.

    Try this next: Treat demo confirmation and event registration emails as prime real estate — include one strategic secondary CTA (e.g., a relevant case study or product feature) to extend engagement beyond the transactional action.

    Large list sends (1,000+ recipients) consistently produce lower open rates, clustering in the 38–44% range even for strong content.

    All six emails sent to 1,000+ contacts returned open rates between 38.0% and 44.2%, averaging ~41%.

    Try this next: Segment large broadcast sends into smaller audience cohorts with differentiated messaging to break through the 44% ceiling on high-volume sends.

    Micro event invitations show highly variable performance depending on list size, with small targeted sends significantly outperforming large broadcast invites.

    Executive Dinner at Flour + Water (n=55) opened at 56.4% vs. Boston TABLE dinner (n=246–253) opening at 17.0–19.4%.

    Try this next: Cap micro event invitation lists at 75–100 contacts maximum, prioritizing contacts with prior engagement signals, to maintain the exclusivity framing that drives opens.

    Emails with explicit click-driving CTAs achieve click rates of 5–12% even on large sends, but most emails in this dataset generate near-zero clicks despite strong opens.

    64 of 191 emails recorded 0.0% click rate; among emails with meaningful click rates, the survey and demo-request categories led with 5–12% click rates.

    Try this next: Add a single, high-relevance CTA to every announcement and newsletter targeting this segment — such as a benchmark report, live demo link, or peer comparison tool — to convert the strong open rates into downstream engagement.

    Moderate Signal

    Subject lines that reference specific, concrete operational concerns or named features outperform vague or generic subject lines.

    'Action required: Michigan is exiting the IMLC, here's what to do' achieved 71.4% open rate; 'Now live: A faster, cleaner provider profile experience' achieved 67.7%, both to audiences of 7–31.
    Show reasoning

    Subject lines naming a specific regulation, feature, or operational pain point cluster in the 60–80% open rate range, while more generic lines like 'This demo ends with a drink' (21.6%) or 'Crafting better care and cocktails' (22.9%) underperform substantially. However, list size confounds this comparison — specific-topic emails are often sent to smaller, more targeted lists, so the audience match may explain part of the gap.

    Short, direct subject lines without excessive punctuation or emoji tend to perform better than heavily stylized or pun-heavy lines.

    'We missed you!' achieved 100% open rate (n=1); 'Your Medallion Demo Request' at 80.5% (n=113) uses a clean, direct format vs. 'This demo ends with a drink' at 21.6% (n=379).
    Show reasoning

    There is a directional pattern where straightforward subject lines — especially those naming the recipient's situation or next step — outperform creative/themed lines. However, sample size differences make a clean comparison difficult, and several stylized lines (e.g., 'Coffee, chocolate, and faster provider onboarding?' at 49.3% to 355 contacts) perform adequately, weakening the signal.

    The 'Your copy is here!' content delivery emails achieve disproportionately high click rates (25–100%) across multiple sends, making content asset delivery a high-performing trigger.

    Five separate 'Your copy is here!' sends recorded click rates of 25%–100%, with a combined average well above any other email type in the dataset.
    Show reasoning

    This pattern is consistent across multiple send instances and date variants, suggesting that post-download follow-through emails to this segment are highly effective. However, the sends are very small (1–9 contacts each), so this may reflect selection bias — people who requested content are already highly motivated, not a generalizable tactic.

    No Signal

    Send timing does not show a clear performance pattern that can be attributed to day or month in this dataset.

    Top open rates appear in dates ranging from July 2025 to April 2026 with no concentration in any month or period.

    High-performing emails are distributed across the entire date range in the dataset. The data is not structured in a way that isolates day-of-week, and seasonal patterns are absent or confounded by list size differences. No timing-based recommendation is supported by this data alone.

    Data quality notes: This dataset spans 191 emails across at least 10 content types with highly skewed send sizes — ranging from 1 to 2,016 recipients per email. Single-recipient emails (where open rate is always 0% or 100%) should be treated as noise rather than signal, and approximately 15 emails fall into this category. The 'unknown' content type accounts for a non-trivial share of high-performing sends (including multiple 'Your copy is here!' variants and the demo request email), limiting type-level analysis. Several subject lines appear across multiple sends with different recipient counts, likely representing A/B tests or audience splits (e.g., 'Your copy is here!' appears 7 times, 'New Analytic reports...' appears twice, 'Your Medallion Demo Request' at varying sizes) — these splits are not labeled, making it difficult to isolate which audience definition or send variant drove differences. Cross-content-type comparisons are also complicated by the fact that smaller, targeted sends skew toward higher-intent audiences, confounding content-type conclusions with audience-quality effects.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 105 74.3% 5.7%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  Webinar 113 60.2% 8.8%
     Medallion Demo Day Webinar Webinar 91 58.2% 4.4%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? Webinar 104 56.7% 2.9%
    Discover the bitter truth about payer enrollment delays Webinar 76 56.6% 2.6%
    What this tells us

    The Compliance/IT segment shows remarkably high open rates for small-batch, targeted sends — particularly product announcements, advisory board communications, and personalized event invitations — while large-list broadcast emails (500–800+ recipients) consistently fall into the 23–37% open range. The segment appears to be dominated by engaged customers and warm prospects who respond to operational relevance (analytics updates, enrollment process changes, product releases) rather than broad awareness campaigns. Click rates are almost universally near zero, suggesting this audience reads but rarely acts on CTAs in email, making open rate the primary success metric. Future strategy should concentrate on tightly-targeted sends with operationally specific subject lines and reserve broadcast sends for high-stakes moments only.

    Strong Signal

    Small, targeted sends dramatically outperform large broadcast sends in open rate across all content types.

    10 of the top 15 highest open-rate emails were sent to 12 or fewer recipients, averaging above 70% open rate.

    Try this next: Segment large lists into smaller, role-specific cohorts before sending; even splitting a 200-person list into 4 targeted groups of 50 may meaningfully lift open rates.

    Announcements and product releases achieve the highest open rates of any content type in this segment.

    Announcements average well above 55% open rate across dozens of sends, with multiple hitting 75–100%.

    Try this next: Prioritize announcement-style emails for product updates and operational changes to this segment; frame content and webinar invites with announcement-like specificity about what is new or changing.

    Click rates are near zero across virtually all email types, regardless of open rate performance.

    Over 80% of emails in the dataset show a 0.0% click rate despite open rates often exceeding 50%.

    Try this next: Redesign CTAs for this segment to be lower-friction and informational (e.g., 'See what changed' linking to a changelog page) rather than sales-oriented; consider whether click rate is even a meaningful KPI for this audience.

    Operationally specific subject lines with concrete details outperform vague or teaser-style subject lines.

    Subjects naming specific features or actions (e.g., 'Generate API keys directly in Medallion' at 83.3%, 'Submit multi-state enrollments easier' at 87.5%) consistently outperform generic teasers.

    Try this next: Lead subject lines with the specific feature, report, or process being updated; avoid teaser formats that withhold the core value proposition.

    Moderate Signal

    Urgency and scarcity subject lines drive above-average open rates for webinar emails specifically when send sizes are large.

    'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' hit 60.2% open rate with 113 recipients; '⏰ Last Chance! Elevate is on Wednesday!' hit 35.9% with 594 recipients.
    Show reasoning

    Among large-list webinar emails (100+ sends), urgency-framed subjects ('Last chance,' 'Time is running out,' 'Final chance') consistently land at the higher end of the 25–60% range compared to non-urgent counterparts for the same event. However, this pattern is clearer at mid-range send sizes (100–150) than at 500+ sends where all rates compress toward 30–36%.

    Micro-event dinner invitations to small, local lists perform well, but geography-agnostic or city-mismatched sends tank.

    Nashville-specific dinner emails sent to 30–47 recipients hit 3.2–3.3% open rates; Boston dinner emails to 16–88 recipients ranged from 22.9% to 43.8%.
    Show reasoning

    The Nashville executive dinner series ('An Exclusive Evening with Nashville Healthcare Executives') performed at 3.2–6.7% open rates, suggesting the list was not well-matched to the event geography. In contrast, Boston and Atlanta dinners to similarly-sized or smaller lists achieved 27–43% open rates. The Emerald Evening Vegas event hit 50–62.5% across two small sends. This pattern is partially confounded by send size differences.

    On-demand or post-event replay emails generate strong engagement when sent to small, pre-qualified lists.

    'Watch Medallion Demo Day on demand' (1 recipient, 100% open, 100% click); 'Elevate on-demand: Watch it anytime, anywhere' (8 recipients, 62.5% open, 12.5% click).
    Show reasoning

    Several on-demand replay emails posted above-average open and click rates relative to their content type peers. However, the best performers were sent to very small lists (1–9 people), making it difficult to separate the small-send effect from genuine interest in replay content. The click rates on replay emails are among the highest in the dataset, suggesting actual intent to view.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no consistent relationship with open rate performance in this dataset.

    Top-performing subjects range from short ('Dinner @ Stillwell's ?') to long ('New fields now live for provider, group, facility, and practice location profiles').

    Both short and long subject lines appear throughout the top and bottom of the open-rate distribution. The dominant variable appears to be send size and content specificity, not length. No reliable length-based pattern is visible in this data.

    Early-week and mid-month send timing does not show a distinguishable lift over other send windows.

    Top-performing emails are spread across dates including January, February, March, August, September, and October with no clustering.

    High-performing emails appear on Mondays, Tuesdays, Thursdays, and across all months in the dataset. Low performers are similarly distributed. With many confounding variables (send size, content type, audience size), no send-timing signal is isolable from this data alone.

    Data quality notes: Several key caveats apply to this analysis. First, send sizes range from 1 to 888, creating extreme audience-size skew — open rates of 100% on single-recipient sends are not statistically meaningful and inflate the top of the rankings. A pattern seen only in sends of 1–3 recipients should be treated with skepticism. Second, the dataset mixes all content types, so cross-type comparisons are complicated by the fact that different content types tend to be sent to different list sizes (e.g., announcements tend to go to smaller lists while webinar invites go to large lists). Third, several subject lines appear multiple times across different send dates and sizes (e.g., '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' appears three times; 'Your copy is here!' appears four times; 'Dinner @ Stillwell's ?' appears three times), making it difficult to isolate subject line effects from list composition effects. Fourth, 'unknown' content type accounts for a non-trivial number of emails, limiting clean content-type segmentation. Fifth, click rates of 0.0% dominate the dataset, which may reflect tracking limitations or link placement issues rather than true disengagement.

    Top Performing Emails

    SubjectContent TypeSentOpen RateClick Rate
    You’re So Close—Elevate is less than a month away! Webinar 115 78.3% 4.3%
    Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day  Webinar 112 59.8% 7.1%
     Ready to reduce cost and remove provider abrasion? Webinar 103 57.3% 2.9%
    Thanks for your interest in Medallion Unknown 64 56.2% 4.7%
    How AI is transforming hospital operations Webinar 100 54.0% 5.0%
    What this tells us

    The HR/Admin segment shows strong baseline engagement across most content types, with announcement, product release, and webinar emails achieving some of the highest open rates in the dataset — but this is heavily skewed by very small send lists (1–4 recipients) where a single open registers as 100%. At scale (100+ sends), open rates settle in the 35–55% range, which is still healthy, but click rates remain consistently low across all types, suggesting this audience opens out of relevance or curiosity but rarely takes action. The segment responds well to content that is directly operational — analytics updates, platform changes, enrollment timelines — more than to event invitations or micro-event dinners. To improve, the team should lean into operational/product messaging with clearer single CTAs, and deprioritize micro-event outreach to this segment where engagement drops sharply at scale.

    Strong Signal

    Micro-event invitation emails consistently underperform compared to other content types at comparable send volumes.

    Micro-event open rates range 6–31% at 16–90 sends vs. 35–57% for webinars at similar or larger volumes

    Try this next: Reduce micro-event sends to HR/Admin contacts or reframe the invitation copy to emphasize operational relevance rather than exclusivity.

    Operational and product-focused subject lines (analytics, platform features, enrollment) drive the strongest engagement among emails with credible sample sizes.

    57–78% open rates for enrollment/analytics-themed emails at 13–122 sends

    Try this next: Prioritize subject lines that name a specific operational pain point or platform feature for the HR/Admin segment rather than generic event or brand news framing.

    Click rates are consistently low across all content types and send sizes for this segment, even when open rates are high.

    Median click rate is approximately 0% across the majority of emails; most emails with 50%+ open rates record 0% clicks

    Try this next: Test single, highly specific CTAs tied directly to a named operational benefit (e.g., 'See your state's enrollment timeline') rather than generic 'learn more' or 'register' links to improve click conversion.

    Moderate Signal

    Engagement is strong across content types at small scale but converges to a moderate baseline at volume, with no single content type dominating at scale.

    35–55% open rate across webinar, content, and announcement emails with 100+ sends
    Show reasoning

    Announcements, product releases, and some webinars hit 100% open rates, but virtually all are sent to 1–4 recipients, making these statistically unreliable. At meaningful scale (100+ sends), webinars average roughly 35–54% open rates, content emails 25–45%, and announcements 23–50% — the ranges overlap substantially, so no single content type clearly outperforms others for this segment at volume. Micro-event emails are the exception, trending lower (6–31%) at scale.

    Subject lines using urgency or 'last chance' framing perform moderately well for webinar emails but show no consistent lift over non-urgency peers.

    'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' hit 59.8% (n=112) vs. 48.3% for a non-urgent Demo Day subject (n=29)
    Show reasoning

    Some urgency-framed subjects outperform their non-urgent counterparts ('Last Chance!' credentialing webinar: 43.9% at n=130), but others do not ('Last Chance—Claim your coffee kit': 0% at n=1, though n=1 is meaningless). The pattern is directionally positive for webinar urgency emails at scale but is not consistent enough to call strong, and click rates remain low regardless.

    Personalization tokens used as subject line openers did not produce notably higher open rates than non-personalized equivalents.

    Personalized Elevate countdown subjects averaged ~35% open rates (n=551–584) vs. 37–39% for non-personalized Elevate subjects at similar volumes
    Show reasoning

    Two emails used HubSpot personalization tokens in the subject line at large scale; their open rates (36.1% and 34.1%) are nearly identical to non-personalized Elevate reminder emails (37.8%, 35.2%) at comparable volumes. This is a narrow comparison within one campaign, so conclusions are limited, but there is no visible lift from token-based personalization here.

    Survey emails achieve above-average click rates for this segment relative to other content types at scale.

    Survey emails averaged 6–9% click rates at 176–215 sends, above the near-zero median for most other types
    Show reasoning

    Three survey emails sent to 176–215 recipients show click rates of 6.1%, 6.5%, and 9.0% — meaningfully above the near-zero click rates typical of webinar and content emails at similar volumes. Open rates were moderate (21–37%). The sample is only three emails in this type, but the pattern is directionally consistent.

    No Signal

    Subject line length shows no clear correlation with open rate in this dataset.

    Top open rates span from 4-word subjects ('Your copy is here!') to 15-word subjects across the dataset

    Short subjects like 'Your copy is here!' (100%, n=3) and long ones like '[You're invited] Rethinking cost and innovation in payer enrollment and medical credentialing' (100%, n=1) both hit 100%, but nearly all high-open-rate emails in this range have trivially small send counts. At scale, there is no visible pattern where shorter or longer subjects consistently outperform. The data does not support a length-based recommendation.

    Emails sent on weekdays throughout the year show no clear send-day or send-month timing advantage for this segment.

    High-performing emails are distributed across Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Jan, Feb, and May with no cluster

    Top open rates appear in emails sent across all quarters represented in the data (July 2025 through June 2026), with no month or apparent day-of-week pattern distinguishable from the data provided. The dataset does not include day-of-week granularity, and volume skew across months makes seasonal comparison unreliable.

    Data quality notes: This dataset of 173 emails has a severe audience-size skew: a large share of the highest open-rate emails were sent to only 1–4 recipients, where a single open inflates the rate to 100% with no statistical meaning. Meaningful volume (50+ sends) is concentrated in webinar and content emails, leaving announcement, newsletter, and product release performance based on very small samples. Several subject lines appear multiple times across different send dates and segments (e.g., 'New Analytic reports are now available for Provider Summary and Credentialing' sent twice, '[Report] The 2026 State of Payer Enrollment and Medical Credentialing' sent three times to potentially overlapping lists), which may reflect A/B tests, re-sends, or multi-segment overlap — these duplicates can distort aggregate patterns. Content type classification also includes an 'unknown' category (9 emails) that cannot be properly benchmarked. Cross-type comparisons should be treated cautiously given these imbalances.

    ⚠ No eligible emails for this persona.

    –
    No pipeline data for the selected filters.

    Nurture Opportunities

    Coverage reality

    1 active nurture — Branched 2026 Onboarding Nurture (20 emails) — covering onboarding contacts only. All other segments below have no active nurture coverage.

    Suggested angle

    Post-event follow-up and on-demand availability emails achieve the highest open rates in the dataset.

    Suggested angle

    Urgency-driven subject lines with 'Last Chance' or countdown language consistently outperform generic invitations.

    63.0% open rate for 'Last chance: Secure your spot for Demo Day' vs. ~30% for early-stage 'You're invited' variants

    Based on webinar performance for this persona

    Starting point: a 4–5 email sequence over 3–4 weeks. Validate with a small test send before committing to the full path.

    ⚠ Informed by one-off send performance, not validated nurture data.
